> 7. **Mixing up Stack and Heap error names** — `StackOverflowError` is from the JVM Stack; `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space` is from the Heap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **JVM = abstract specification + concrete runtime engine** that enables WORA.
- **Three subsystems:** Class Loader, Runtime Data Areas, Execution Engine.
- **Class loading sequence:** Load → Link (Verify, Prepare, Resolve) → Initialize.
- **Three class loaders (Parent Delegation):** Bootstrap (native) → Platform → Application.
- **Memory regions:** Method Area (shared), Heap (shared), Stack (private), PC (private), Native Method Stack (private).
- **Execution engine components:** Interpreter (line-by-line), JIT Compiler (hot-path native), Garbage Collector (auto memory).
- **JIT thresholds:** C1 ≈ 1,500 invocations; C2 ≈ 10,000 invocations (HotSpot default).
- **Bytecode Verifier** = first security gate; runs during Linking.
- **Garbage collection eligibility** = no strong reference chain from any GC root.
- **WORA is enabled because** bytecode is OS-independent; the JVM is the only platform-specific layer.
- **JNI** bridges JVM execution engine with native C/C++ libraries via the Native Method Stack.
- **Default HotSpot heap sizing:** `Xms = RAM/64`, `Xmx = min(RAM/4, 32 GB)` on 64-bit systems.
- **Method Area is now called Metaspace** (Java 8+) and uses native memory (off-heap).
- **Common errors:** `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space`, `StackOverflowError`, `OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace`.
- **Watch the pitfall callouts** during valuation — most marks are lost on memory-region confusion and loader-direction errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Primitive Data Types — Formal Definition

In the Java programming language, **primitive data types** are the most fundamental, built-in data types that are predefined by the language itself and are not objects. They represent raw, single-valued storage units allocated directly on the **stack memory** (for local variables) or as part of an object on the **heap memory** (for instance variables). The Java Language Specification (JLS) defines **eight primitive types**: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, and `boolean`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECST615 — Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to *"describe the syntax and usage of primitive data types, variables, and wrapper classes in Java."* Mastery of the size, range, and default value of each primitive is **mandatory** for both Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Wrapper Types — Formal Definition

A **wrapper class** is a class in the `java.lang` package that *encapsulates* (wraps) a primitive data type within a Java object. Each of the eight primitives has a corresponding wrapper: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, and `Boolean`. Since Java is a strongly-typed, purely object-oriented language that powers frameworks like Collections, Generics, and Serialization, primitives must sometimes be converted into objects — this is precisely what wrapper classes enable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (JLS §5.1.7):** *Boxing conversion* is the conversion of a value of a primitive type to a corresponding value of a reference type (wrapper). *Unboxing conversion* is the inverse operation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are packing a lunchbox for school:

- **Primitive Data Type** 🍎 — A bare apple. Lightweight, easy to grab, stored in a simple basket, has a fixed weight and color. You can eat it directly without any packaging.
- **Wrapper Class** 📦 — The *same* apple, but now placed inside a decorated gift box with a label (e.g., "Apple — 182 grams — Red — Harvested 12-Oct-2025"). The box allows the apple to be **mailed**, **inspected**, **sorted by type on a conveyor belt**, and **handled by automated machinery**.

The fruit inside is identical — but the wrapper adds **object-oriented capabilities**: identity, methods (`getWeight()`), participation in frameworks (Collections, Generics), and the ability to hold a `null` value (an "empty" box).

| Aspect | Primitive (🍎) | Wrapper (📦) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Location | Stack (local) / Heap (field) | Heap only |
| Identity / Object | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Can be `null` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Usable in `ArrayList`, etc. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Performance | ⚡ Faster (no dereferencing) | 🐢 Slower (object overhead) |
| Methods available | ❌ None (except `String` concat) | ✅ `parseInt()`, `valueOf()`, etc. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** *"I B-SSIL FD CB"* → **I**nt, **B**yte, **S**hort, **S**hort(no, second **S** for Short again — corrected: **B**oolean, **B**yte, **S**hort, **I**nt, **L**ong, **F**loat, **D**ouble, **C**har) — but easier: remember the **Integer family** (B, S, I, L) and the **Floating family** (F, D), plus **C**har and **B**oolean.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Primitives — Structured Breakdown

### 🔢 Integer Family (Signed, Two's Complement Representation)
- **`byte`** — 8-bit signed integer. Used for saving memory in large arrays, raw binary data, and network I/O streams.
- **`short`** — 16-bit signed integer. Rarely used; appears in legacy code and low-level image processing.
- **`int`** — 32-bit signed integer. **Default** for integral literals. The workhorse type for loops, counters, and array indices.
- **`long`** — 64-bit signed integer. Used for timestamps (`System.currentTimeMillis()`), large counters, and unique IDs.

### 🔬 Floating-Point Family (IEEE 754 Standard)
- **`float`** — 32-bit single-precision IEEE 754. Suffixed with `f` or `F`. Used in graphics and GPU programming.
- **`double`** — 64-bit double-precision IEEE 754. **Default** for decimal literals. Used in scientific computation.

### 🔤 Character Type
- **`char`** — 16-bit unsigned Unicode (UTF-16 code unit). Range: `\u0000` to `\uFFFF`. Stores a single character.

### ☑️ Boolean Type
- **`boolean`** — Represents logical state. Only two valid values: `true` and `false`. **Size is JVM-implementation-dependent** (typically 1 bit logically, 1 byte physically).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Sizes, Ranges & Defaults

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range Derivation Rule (Two's Complement):** For an *n*-bit signed integer, the range is:
> $$ \text{Min} = -2^{n-1} \quad , \quad \text{Max} = +2^{n-1} - 1 $$

| Primitive | Wrapper | Size (bits) | Size (bytes) | Min Value | Max Value | Default Value | Literal Suffix |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `byte` | `java.lang.Byte` | 8 | 1 | $-2^7$ = **−128** | $2^7 - 1$ = **+127** | `(byte) 0` | none |
| `short` | `java.lang.Short` | 16 | 2 | $-2^{15}$ = **−32,768** | $2^{15} - 1$ = **+32,767** | `(short) 0` | none |
| `int` | `java.lang.Integer` | 32 | 4 | $-2^{31}$ = **−2,147,483,648** | $2^{31} - 1$ = **+2,147,483,647** | `0` | none |
| `long` | `java.lang.Long` | 64 | 8 | $-2^{63}$ = **−9.22 × 10¹⁸** | $2^{63} - 1$ = **+9.22 × 10¹⁸** | `0L` | `L` or `l` |
| `float` | `java.lang.Float` | 32 | 4 | `-3.4028235E+38` | `+3.4028235E+38` | `0.0f` | `F` or `f` |
| `double` | `java.lang.Double` | 64 | 8 | `-1.7976931348623157E+308` | `+1.7976931348623157E+308` | `0.0d` | `D` or `d` |
| `char` | `java.lang.Character` | 16 | 2 | `'\u0000'` (0) | `'\uFFFF'` (65,535) | `'\u0000'` | none |
| `boolean` | `java.lang.Boolean` | 1 (logical) | ~1 (physical) | — | — | `false` | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — The `boolean` Question:** Examiners frequently ask *"What is the size of a `boolean`?"* Many students answer "1 byte." Technically, the **JLS leaves the size unspecified** — the JVM encodes it as `int` (1 in the local variable array). Never answer "1 bit" without the qualifier *"logical"*.

## 2.3 Wrapper Classes — The Engineering Rationale

Java primitives cannot be used in scenarios that **require objects**. The wrapper classes were introduced to bridge this gap. The key engineering motivations are:

1. **Collections Framework** — `ArrayList<Integer>`, `HashMap<Long, String>`. Pre-Java 1.5, you had to write `new Integer(5)`.
2. **Generics** — Type parameters must be reference types: `List<Double>` is valid, `List<double>` is a compile error.
3. **Reflection API** — `Class.getMethod()` and `Method.invoke()` operate on `Object`.
4. **Serialization** — The `Serializable` interface requires objects.
5. **Nullability** — Primitives cannot be `null`; a wrapper can represent *absence of value* (database NULL, optional fields).
6. **Utility Methods** — `Integer.parseInt("42")`, `Double.parseDouble("3.14")`, `Character.isDigit('5')`.

### 2.4 Autoboxing & Unboxing (JDK 1.5 / Tiger Release)

- **Autoboxing** — Automatic conversion performed by the compiler: `int → Integer`
- **Unboxing** — The reverse: `Integer → int`

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | `byte` arrays for serial port I/O and firmware |
| **Financial Software** | `BigDecimal` (not `float`) for currency, but primitives for indices |
| **Scientific Computing** | `double` for floating-point matrix operations in simulations |
| **Network Programming** | `byte[]` buffers for TCP/UDP packet payloads |
| **Database / ORM (Hibernate)** | Wrapper types allow `null` for missing DB columns |
| **Distributed Systems** | `long` for 64-bit unique IDs (Twitter Snowflake, UUID) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Range of Signed Integer Types

Java uses **two's complement** representation for all signed integers. The mathematical range for an *n*-bit signed integer is derived as follows:

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Bits

For a `byte`, $n = 8$. For a `short`, $n = 16$. For an `int`, $n = 32$. For a `long`, $n = 64$.

### Step 2 — Determine the Sign Bit

The most significant bit (MSB) acts as the sign indicator. This leaves $n - 1$ bits for the magnitude.

### Step 3 — Derive the Maximum Value

The maximum value occurs when the MSB is **0** (positive) and all remaining bits are **1**. This represents $2^{n-1} - 1$.

$$
\text{Max} = 2^{n-1} - 1
$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Minimum Value

The minimum value occurs when the MSB is **1** (negative) and all remaining bits are **0**. In two's complement, this represents $-2^{n-1}$.

$$
\text{Min} = -2^{n-1}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example for `byte` (n = 8)

$$
\text{Max} = 2^{8-1} - 1 = 2^7 - 1 = 128 - 1 = \mathbf{+127}
$$

$$
\text{Min} = -2^{8-1} = -2^7 = \mathbf{-128}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Example for `int` (n = 32)

$$
\text{Max} = 2^{32-1} - 1 = 2^{31} - 1 = \mathbf{2{,}147{,}483{,}647}
$$

$$
\text{Min} = -2^{32-1} = -2^{31} = \mathbf{-2{,}147{,}483{,}648}
$$

### Step 7 — Total Count of Distinct Values

$$
\text{Count} = \text{Max} - \text{Min} + 1 = 2^n
$$

For `byte`: $2^8 = 256$ distinct values. ✅ Matches the 8-bit capacity.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Type Conversion Rules (JLS §5.1)

Java enforces strict rules for converting between primitive types:

| Conversion Type | Direction | Bits Lost? | Explicit Cast Required? | Example |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Widening (Implicit)** | `byte` → `short` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` | ❌ No | ❌ No | `int x = 10; long y = x;` |
| **Widening (Implicit)** | `char` → `int` | ❌ No | ❌ No | `char c = 'A'; int i = c;` |
| **Narrowing (Explicit)** | `double` → `float` → `long` → `int` → `short` → `byte` | ✅ Possible | ✅ **Yes** | `double d = 10.5; int i = (int) d;` |
| **Narrowing (Explicit)** | `int`/`long` → `char` | ✅ Truncation | ✅ **Yes** | `int i = 65; char c = (char) i;` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Loss of Precision vs. Loss of Magnitude:** When narrowing a floating-point value to an integer, Java **truncates toward zero**, it does *not* round. Casting `9.999` to `int` yields `9`, not `10`. When narrowing `int 130` to `byte`, modular arithmetic occurs: $130 \mod 256 = -126$ (because 130 = 0b10000010, and the MSB is 1, so it becomes negative).

## 3.3 Autoboxing Mechanics — Compiler-Generated Code

Java's autoboxing is **syntactic sugar**. The compiler transparently inserts calls to `Integer.valueOf()` and `Integer.intValue()`. This is crucial for exam answers that ask *"What really happens during autoboxing?"*.

### Analytical Example

Consider the source code:

```java
Integer boxed = 100;          // Autoboxing
int unboxed = boxed;          // Unboxing
Integer sum = boxed + 5;      // Auto-unbox, add, auto-rebox
```

### Step-by-Step Decompilation (Conceptual)

$$
\text{Integer boxed = 100;} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Integer boxed = Integer.valueOf(100);}
$$

$$
\text{int unboxed = boxed;} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{int unboxed = boxed.intValue();}
$$

$$
\text{Integer sum = boxed + 5;} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Integer sum = Integer.valueOf(boxed.intValue() + 5);}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — The `valueOf()` Cache:** `Integer.valueOf(int)` uses an internal **cache for values from −128 to +127** (configurable via `-XX:AutoBoxCacheMax`). Therefore, `Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127;` yields `a == b` → **true** (same cached object). But `Integer a = 128; Integer b = 128;` yields `a == b` → **false** (different objects). Always use `.equals()` for wrapper comparison — this is one of the most heavily tested KTU pitfalls.

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification — Why `long` for Timestamps

The Unix epoch (00:00:00 UTC on 1 January 1970) is conventionally stored as `long` because:

$$
\text{Milliseconds since epoch} = 2^{63} - 1 \text{ milliseconds} \approx 292{,}471{,}208 \text{ years}
$$

This is more than sufficient to represent any timestamp in human history and the foreseeable future, eliminating the Y2K38 problem that plagued 32-bit systems (which would overflow in January 2038).

## 3.5 Default Value Initialization Matrix

| Variable Scope | Default Value Applied? | Reason |
|---|:---:|---|
| **Instance variable** (class field) | ✅ Yes | JVM zero-initializes heap memory |
| **Static variable** (class field) | ✅ Yes | Stored in method area, zero-initialized |
| **Local variable** (method scope) | ❌ No | Must be explicitly assigned before use |
| **Method parameter** | ❌ No | Caller must supply an argument |

```java
class Demo {
    static int s;          // s = 0 (default)
    int i;                 // i = 0 (default)
    void m(int p) {       // p must be passed by caller
        int l;             // l is UN-initialized; using it → compile error
        System.out.println(l); // ❌ "variable l might not have been initialized"
    }
}
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Java Type System Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Java Types"]
    rootB["Reference Types"]
    rootC["Primitive Types"]
    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootB --> ref1["Class Types"]
    rootB --> ref2["Interface Types"]
    rootB --> ref3["Array Types"]
    rootB --> ref4["Enum Types"]
    rootB --> ref5["Wrapper Classes"]
    rootC --> p1["byte - 8 bit"]
    rootC --> p2["short - 16 bit"]
    rootC --> p3["int - 32 bit"]
    rootC --> p4["long - 64 bit"]
    rootC --> p5["float - 32 bit"]
    rootC --> p6["double - 64 bit"]
    rootC --> p7["char - 16 bit"]
    rootC --> p8["boolean - 1 bit"]
    p1 -.-> w1["java.lang.Byte"]
    p2 -.-> w2["java.lang.Short"]
    p3 -.-> w3["java.lang.Integer"]
    p4 -.-> w4["java.lang.Long"]
    p5 -.-> w5["java.lang.Float"]
    p6 -.-> w6["java.lang.Double"]
    p7 -.-> w7["java.lang.Character"]
    p8 -.-> w8["java.lang.Boolean"]
```

*The dotted arrows (`-.->`) represent the **wrapping relationship** — every primitive has a corresponding wrapper class located in `java.lang`.*

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Autoboxing & Unboxing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph srcCode["Source Code"]
        s1["int x = 42"]
        s2["Integer y = x"]
        s3["int z = y"]
    end
    subgraph compile["Compiler Inserts"]
        c1["Integer y = Integer.valueOf(42)"]
        c2["int z = y.intValue()"]
    end
    subgraph runtime["JVM Runtime"]
        r1["Stack: primitive int 42"]
        r2["Heap: Integer object on IntegerCache 128 to 127"]
    end
    s1 --> s2
    s2 -- "Autoboxing" --> c1
    s1 --> r1
    c1 -- "Cache lookup" --> r2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 -- "Unboxing" --> c2
    c2 -- "Method call" --> r1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Wrapper Class Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    obj["Integer Object on Heap"]
    obj --> f1["private final int value"]
    obj --> f2["private static final int MIN_VALUE = 2147483648"]
    obj --> f3["private static final int MAX_VALUE = 2147483647"]
    obj --> f4["private static final Class TYPE"]
    obj --> m1["intValue"]
    obj --> m2["doubleValue"]
    obj --> m3["parseInt String"]
    obj --> m4["valueOf int"]
    obj --> m5["toString"]
    obj --> m6["equals Object"]
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout Comparison

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 1
    block:stack["STACK MEMORY - Primitive Local Variable"]
        A["int x = 42 \n Size 4 bytes \n Direct value 42"]
    end
    block:heap["HEAP MEMORY - Wrapper Object"]
        B["Integer object reference \n Object header 12 bytes"]
        C["Field int value 42 \n 4 bytes"]
        D["Padding alignment \n 4 bytes"]
        E["Total 16 bytes per wrapper"]
    end
    stack ~~~ heap
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single `Integer` wrapper consumes **~16 bytes** of heap memory (object header + value + padding) compared to a **4-byte** raw `int`. In memory-constrained systems or large arrays, this 4× overhead justifies the use of primitive collections like **Eclipse Collections `IntList`** or **Trove / fastutil** libraries.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### 📘 Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Q: List the eight primitive data types in Java along with their size in bytes and default value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Primitive | Size (bytes) | Default Value |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | `byte` | 1 | `0` |
| 2 | `short` | 2 | `0` |
| 3 | `int` | 4 | `0` |
| 4 | `long` | 8 | `0L` |
| 5 | `float` | 4 | `0.0f` |
| 6 | `double` | 8 | `0.0d` |
| 7 | `char` | 2 | `'\u0000'` |
| 8 | `boolean` | JVM-dependent (~1) | `false` |

*(1 mark for listing all eight, 1 mark for correct sizes, 1 mark for correct defaults.)*

---

### 📘 Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q: What are wrapper classes in Java? Why are they needed when Java already has primitive types?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wrapper classes are object representations of Java's primitive types, defined in the `java.lang` package. They "wrap" a primitive value inside an object instance. They are needed because:

1. **Collections and Generics require objects** — `ArrayList<int>` is illegal; we must use `ArrayList<Integer>`.
2. **Nullability** — Primitives cannot be `null`, but wrappers can, enabling the representation of missing/optional values.
3. **Reflection and Serialization** — These object-oriented features work only on `Object` subclasses.
4. **Utility methods** — `Integer.parseInt()`, `Double.parseDouble()`, `Character.isDigit()` provide conversion and validation.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE Rule: Each Part B question has an **internal choice** between two alternatives. We provide both options below.

---

### 📗 Question B-A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain autoboxing and unboxing in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the `Integer.valueOf()` cache. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates type casting between primitive types (both widening and narrowing) and explain the data loss that occurs in narrowing conversions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion of a primitive value to its corresponding wrapper object, performed by the Java compiler (JDK 1.5+).

**Unboxing** is the reverse automatic conversion of a wrapper object to its primitive value.

**Example code:**

```java
public class BoxDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int     prim   = 100;                 // primitive
        Integer wrap   = prim;                // AUTOBBOXING: int -> Integer
        int     prim2  = wrap;                // UNBOXING: Integer -> int

        // Use in Collections
        java.util.ArrayList<Integer> list = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
        list.add(50);                          // autoboxes 50 to Integer
        int first = list.get(0);               // unboxes Integer back to int

        // NullPointerException risk
        Integer nullBox = null;
        // int bad = nullBox;                  // ❌ throws NullPointerException
    }
}
```

**The `Integer.valueOf()` Cache — 3 marks out of 7:**

The `Integer` class maintains a **static cache** of `Integer` objects for the range `[−128, +127]`. When `Integer.valueOf(int i)` is called for any value in this range, the same cached object is returned.

```java
Integer a = 127, b = 127;
System.out.println(a == b);    // true  — same cached object
System.out.println(a.equals(b)); // true — value comparison

Integer x = 200, y = 200;
System.out.println(x == y);    // false — different objects (outside cache)
System.out.println(x.equals(y)); // true  — value comparison
```

> **Best Practice:** Always use `.equals()` for comparing wrapper objects, never `==` (which compares references).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Java Program demonstrating type casting:**

```java
public class CastingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // ---- WIDENING (Implicit, Safe) ----
        byte  b  = 50;
        int   i  = b;          // byte -> int (implicit)
        long  l  = i;          // int  -> long (implicit)
        double d = l;          // long -> double (implicit)
        System.out.println("Widening: " + d);

        // ---- NARROWING (Explicit, Risky) ----
        double pi = 3.14159;
        int    truncated = (int) pi;   // double -> int: 3.14159 -> 3 (truncation)
        System.out.println("Narrowing double->int: " + truncated);

        long  big = 130L;
        byte  small = (byte) big;     // long -> byte: 130 -> -126 (overflow)
        System.out.println("Narrowing long->byte: " + small);

        int   code = 65;
        char  letter = (char) code;   // int -> char: 65 -> 'A'
        System.out.println("Narrowing int->char: " + letter);
    }
}
```

**Data Loss Analysis — 4 marks out of 7:**

1. **Truncation:** `(int) 3.999` → `3` (decimal portion discarded).
2. **Overflow / Modular Wraparound:** `(byte) 130` → 130 = `0b10000010`. Interpreted as signed 8-bit = −126.
3. **Sign Loss:** Assigning a negative `int` to a `char` yields a large positive Unicode value.

---

### 📕 Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**Q: (a)** Describe the eight primitive data types in Java with their size, range, and default value. Provide one real-world engineering use case for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of wrapper classes with a suitable example. Differentiate between `parseInt()` and `valueOf()` methods of the `Integer` class. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Primitive | Size | Range | Default | Engineering Use Case |
|---|:---:|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 1 B | −128 to 127 | 0 | Storing raw PCM audio samples (signed 8-bit) |
| `short` | 2 B | −32,768 to 32,767 | 0 | 16-bit image pixel values in legacy formats |
| `int` | 4 B | ≈ ±2.15 × 10⁹ | 0 | Loop counters, array indices, general arithmetic |
| `long` | 8 B | ≈ ±9.22 × 10¹⁸ | 0L | Unix timestamps, nanosecond precision measurements |
| `float` | 4 B | ±3.4 × 10³⁸ (≈7 digits) | 0.0f | OpenGL vertex coordinates, embedded GPU data |
| `double` | 8 B | ±1.8 × 10³⁰⁸ (≈15 digits) | 0.0d | Scientific simulations, financial analytics |
| `char` | 2 B | 0 to 65,535 | `'\u0000'` | Parsing single Unicode characters from text |
| `boolean` | 1 bit | true / false | false | Conditional flags, status indicators in state machines |

*(2 marks for the table, 3 marks for use cases, 2 marks for range derivation logic.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Wrapper Class Example:**

```java
public class WrapperDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int primitive = 42;
        Integer wrapper = Integer.valueOf(primitive);   // explicit boxing
        Integer autoBoxed = primitive;                   // autoboxing (JDK 1.5+)

        // Useful wrapper methods
        String str     = Integer.toString(255);          // "255" (base 10)
        String binary  = Integer.toBinaryString(10);     // "1010"
        String hex     = Integer.toHexString(255);       // "ff"

        int parsed = Integer.parseInt("100");            // returns primitive int
        Integer boxed = Integer.valueOf("100");          // returns Integer object

        System.out.println("Sum = " + (parsed + boxed.intValue()));
    }
}
```

**Difference between `parseInt()` and `valueOf()` — 4 marks out of 7:**

| Aspect | `Integer.parseInt(String s)` | `Integer.valueOf(String s)` |
|---|---|---|
| **Return type** | Primitive `int` | Wrapper `Integer` (object) |
| **Internal implementation** | Direct conversion | Calls `parseInt()` then boxes the result |
| **Memory** | No object allocated | Allocates an `Integer` object |
| **Usage** | When you only need the numeric value | When you need an object (e.g., for `List<Integer>`) |
| **Throws** | `NumberFormatException` | `NumberFormatException` |
| **Cache benefit** | N/A | Uses cache for values −128 to +127 |

> **One-line summary:** *"`parseInt()` returns a primitive; `valueOf()` returns an object."*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (Cost You 1–3 Marks Each):**
> 
> 1. **Comparing wrappers with `==`** — In code-writing questions, never use `==` to compare `Integer` or `String` objects. Examiners *expect* you to use `.equals()`. Using `==` outside the cache range loses you 1 mark immediately.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting literal suffixes** — Writing `long x = 10000000000;` (10 billion) is a **compile error** because it exceeds `int` range. Correct form: `long x = 10000000000L;`. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this oversight.
> 
> 3. **Mis-stating `boolean` size** — Answering "1 bit" without the qualifier *"logically, JVM-implementation dependent"* is a half-mark loss. The JLS does not mandate a specific physical size.
> 
> 4. **Confusing `char` signedness** — Students often write "char is signed, range −32,768 to 32,767." This is wrong. `char` is **unsigned**, range `0` to `65,535`.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the autoboxing NullPointerException trap** — When asked to list wrapper disadvantages, *always* mention that unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException` — this is a frequently-tested 2-mark question.
> 
> 6. **Writing `Integer` where `int` suffices** — In performance-sensitive code, autoboxing creates objects on the heap and stresses the GC. Examiners may deduct marks for not mentioning this trade-off.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 🎯 **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Java has **exactly 8 primitive types** — *byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean*. Memorize this set.
- ✅ **Default `int` for integer literals; default `double` for decimal literals.** Use `L`/`l` for `long`, `F`/`f` for `float`.
- ✅ Range formula for *n*-bit signed integer: $\text{Min} = -2^{n-1}$, $\text{Max} = 2^{n-1} - 1$.
- ✅ `char` is **unsigned 16-bit** Unicode (range `0` to `65,535`). Not signed like `short`.
- ✅ `boolean` size is **JVM-implementation dependent** — not officially defined.
- ✅ **Instance/static fields** get default values; **local variables** must be explicitly initialized.
- ✅ **Widening** is implicit & safe; **narrowing** is explicit & risky (truncation, overflow).
- ✅ Each primitive has a corresponding wrapper in `java.lang`: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, `Boolean`.
- ✅ **Autoboxing** (primitive → wrapper) and **unboxing** (wrapper → primitive) introduced in **JDK 1.5**.
- ✅ `Integer.valueOf()` **caches** values in the range **[−128, +127]** — `==` works for these, fails outside.
- ✅ Use `.equals()` for wrapper comparison; never use `==` (except for the cached range).
- ✅ `parseInt()` returns `int`; `valueOf()` returns `Integer`.
- ✅ **Unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException`**.
- ✅ Wrappers enable primitives to be used in **Collections, Generics, Reflection, Serialization**.
- ✅ Wrappers add **~16 bytes overhead** per object vs. 4 bytes for a raw `int` — performance trade-off.
- ✅ Real-world timestamp storage uses `long` to avoid the **Y2K38** (Year 2038) problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Casting and Autoboxing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Casting and Autoboxing # 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Casting in Java? **Casting** in Java is the process of converting a value from one data type to another. It is one of the most fundamental compile-time and runtime mechanisms in the Java Type System, and is heavily tested in KTU ESE papers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Type Casting):** > *"Type casting is the explicit or implicit conversion of a value of one data type into another data type, governed by JLS (Java Language Specification) rules, ensuring type compatibility and data integrity."* Java recognises **two broad families** of casting: | Family | Operates On | Also Known As | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Primitive Casting** | `byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean` | Type Promotion / Demotion | | **Reference Casting** | Class / Interface object references | Upcasting / Downcasting | Within primitive casting, there are again two sub-categories: - **Widening (Implicit / Automatic) Casting** → smaller → larger type (e.g., `int` → `long`). **Safe**, no data loss, no syntax required. - **Narrowing (Explicit / Manual) Casting** → larger → smaller type (e.g., `double` → `int`). **Risky**, may lose data or precision, requires `(targetType)` cast operator. ## 1.2 What is Autoboxing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Autoboxing):** > *"Autoboxing is the automatic conversion that the Java compiler makes between primitive data types and their corresponding wrapper class objects (e.g., `int` ↔ `Integer`), introduced as a language feature in Java 5 (J2SE 5.0)."* The reverse process — converting a wrapper object back to its primitive — is called **Unboxing**. > [!NOTE] > Wrapper classes in `java.lang` package: `Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double, Character, Boolean`. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogies (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 — Casting as Currency Exchange 💱** > Think of casting as converting money. Converting **1 Rupee → 1 Dollar** (widening) is automatic at the bank and you never lose value. But converting **1 Dollar → 1 Rupee** (narrowing) is explicit, the bank charges you, and you lose a fraction (data loss). The compiler behaves exactly like a strict bank teller: it allows safe conversions silently, but demands a written "request form" (the cast operator `(int)`) for risky ones. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 — Autoboxing as Gift Wrapping 🎁** > An `int` value like `42` is a **raw item** sitting on a table. A **wrapper class** like `Integer` is that same item **wrapped in a decorative gift box**, which can now be stored in collections (`ArrayList`), passed to methods that accept `Object`, or used where objects are required. **Autoboxing** = "the shopkeeper wraps it for you automatically at the counter." **Unboxing** = "you tear the wrapper off to use the raw item." Before Java 5, the customer had to do this wrapping manually using `new Integer(42)` and `intValue()` — which was tedious and bug-prone. ## 1.4 Why These Concepts Matter in OOP - **Collections Framework** (`ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `LinkedList`) only stores **objects**, not primitives. Autoboxing made generics + primitive interaction elegant. - **Polymorphism** in inheritance requires **upcasting** (safe) and **downcasting** (explicit), tested heavily in Module 2 / Module 3 OOP questions. - **Method Overloading Resolution** depends on the exact widening / boxing hierarchy — a KTU favourite. # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Primitive Casting — The Java Type Promotion Ladder Java internally ranks primitives from "narrowest" to "widest." This ranking is **not arbitrary**; it is mandated by the JLS §5.1.2 (Widening Primitive Conversion) and §5.1.3 (Narrowing Primitive Conversion). **Widening Order (smallest to largest, in bits of information capacity):** $$\text{byte} \;(8) \;\rightarrow\; \text{short} \;(16) \;\rightarrow\; \text{int} \;(32) \;\rightarrow\; \text{long} \;(64) \;\rightarrow\; \text{float} \;(32\text{ but wider range}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{double} \;(64)$$ $$\text{char} \;(16) \;\rightarrow\; \text{int} \;(32) \;\rightarrow\; \text{long} \;(64) \;\rightarrow\; \text{float} \;\rightarrow\; \text{double}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Notice that `char` is treated as an unsigned 16-bit type and **does not widen to `short` or `byte`**. This is a classic KTU trick question. ### 2.1.1 Why is Widening Safe? Because the destination type can hold **every possible value** of the source type. There is no information loss. ### 2.1.2 Why is Narrowing Risky? Because the destination type is **smaller** and cannot represent the full range. Bits are **truncated**, fractional parts are **discarded**, and overflow wraps around modulo the new type's size. $$\text{truncated value} = \text{source value} \mod 2^{n} \quad \text{where } n = \text{bits in destination}$$ For floating → integer narrowing, the **fractional part is simply dropped** (truncation toward zero, **not** rounding). ## 2.2 Reference (Object) Casting | Direction | Syntax | Cast Required? | Risk | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Upcasting** (Subclass → Superclass) | Implicit | ❌ No | Safe — guaranteed by inheritance | | **Downcasting** (Superclass → Subclass) | Explicit `(Subclass)obj` | ✅ Yes | Risky — throws `ClassCastException` at runtime if actual type is incompatible | > [!NOTE] > The Java compiler checks the **declared reference type** at compile time, but the JVM checks the **actual object type** at runtime using RTTI (Run-Time Type Information). This is why an `instanceof` check is mandatory before downcasting in production code. ## 2.3 Autoboxing & Unboxing — Compiler Magic > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule:** Autoboxing and unboxing are **compiler-level** transformations. The Java compiler inserts the equivalent of `valueOf()` and `xxxValue()` method calls automatically. The **JVM bytecode** still contains the manual conversion — there is zero runtime overhead difference. The compiler performs autoboxing when a primitive is passed to a method expecting a wrapper, assigned to a wrapper variable, or placed into a collection. The compiler performs unboxing when a wrapper object is used in an arithmetic expression, assigned to a primitive, or passed to a method expecting a primitive. ## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Rule | Formula / Syntax | Safe? | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Widening (Implicit) | `target = source;` | ✅ Yes | `int a = 5; long b = a;` | | 2 | Narrowing (Explicit) | `target = (TargetType) source;` | ⚠️ Risk | `double d = 9.78; int i = (int) d;` | | 3 | Autoboxing (Primitive → Wrapper) | `Integer obj = primitive;` | ✅ Yes | `Integer x = 10;` | | 4 | Unboxing (Wrapper → Primitive) | `primitive = obj;` | ⚠️ NPE if null | `int y = x;` | | 5 | Upcasting (Sub → Super) | `Super s = subObj;` | ✅ Yes | `Object o = "Hello";` | | 6 | Downcasting (Super → Sub) | `Sub s = (Sub) superObj;` | ⚠️ `ClassCastException` | `String s = (String) o;` | | 7 | char → int | `(int) ch` | ✅ Gives Unicode value | `(int) 'A'` → `65` | | 8 | Floating truncation | `int x = (int) 9.99;` | Drops fraction | Result: `9` | | 9 | Boxing happens in `==` | ❌ Compares references for `Integer ≥ 128` | ⚠️ Trap | `Integer a = 127, b = 127; a == b` → `true`; `a = 200; b = 200; a == b` → `false` | | 10 | `instanceof` guard | `if(obj instanceof Sub)` | ✅ Defensive | Required before downcasting | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems & IoT (KTU ECE/CS context):** Narrowing is essential when 32-bit sensor data must be packed into 8-bit ADC registers. - **Database Drivers (JDBC):** SQL `null` values are returned as wrapper objects, not primitives, so unboxing safely is critical to avoid `NullPointerException`. - **Collection Pipelines:** Modern data processing in Java (`Stream`, `mapToInt(...)`) relies entirely on boxing / unboxing internals. - **API Interoperability:** Legacy APIs accept `Object` — autoboxing lets primitives participate transparently. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** This section provides **line-by-line traceable** examples. Every example below is structured as a question-style derivation that you can reproduce in the exam for full marks. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Implicit Widening of an `int` to a `double` **Problem:** What is the value and type of `result` after execution? ```java int count = 25; double result = count; ``` **Step 1 — Type identification:** The source variable `count` is declared as `int`, occupying 32 bits. The destination variable `result` is declared as `double`, occupying 64 bits. **Step 2 — Lookup the JLS widening chain:** The JLS §5.1.2 list explicitly includes the conversion `int → double` as a **widening primitive conversion**. No operator is required. **Step 3 — Bit-level transformation:** The 32-bit two's complement integer `25` is converted to the nearest representable IEEE-754 double. Since 25 is exactly representable in binary, no rounding occurs. **Step 4 — Result:** $$\text{result} = 25.0$$ Type of `result` = `double`. The `.0` is **appended automatically** by the compiler. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Explicit Narrowing of a `double` to an `int` **Problem:** Evaluate step-by-step: ```java double price = 149.99; int rupees = (int) price; System.out.println(rupees); ``` **Step 1 — Identify the cast operator `(int)`:** The presence of the cast operator tells the compiler that the programmer **accepts responsibility** for possible data loss. **Step 2 — Apply the truncation rule (JLS §5.1.3):** For floating-to-integer narrowing, the fractional part is **discarded** (rounding toward zero, not banker's rounding). $$\text{rupees} = \lfloor 149.99 \rfloor_{\text{toward zero}} = 149$$ **Step 3 — Output trace:** ``` 149 ``` **Step 4 — Common Mistake Trap:** If a student writes `(int) Math.round(price)`, the answer would be `150`. Many KTU answer sheets lose marks because students confuse **truncation with rounding**. The cast operator `(int)` does **not** round. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overflow during Narrowing (`int` → `byte`) **Problem:** Predict the output of: ```java int big = 257; byte small = (byte) big; System.out.println(small); ``` **Step 1 — Determine the bit-width of the destination:** A `byte` holds 8 bits, giving a signed range of $-2^{7}$ to $2^{7}-1$, i.e., $-128$ to $127$. **Step 2 — Convert 257 to binary (32 bits, but only the lowest 8 matter):** $$257_{10} = 0000\,0001\,0000\,0001_{2}$$ **Step 3 — Keep the lowest 8 bits (JLS narrowing rule):** $$0000\,0001$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the lowest 8 bits as a signed 8-bit value using two's complement:** $$\text{decimal value} = 1$$ **Step 5 — Output:** ``` 1 ``` > [!WARNING] > This is **not** 257, nor 0. The high-order bits are **silently discarded**. The compiler does not warn you unless the cast is from a `final` constant expression. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Autoboxing in a Collection **Problem:** Trace the execution of: ```java import java.util.ArrayList; public class BoxDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { ArrayList marks = new ArrayList<>(); marks.add(85); marks.add(92); int total = marks.get(0) + marks.get(1); System.out.println("Total = " + total); } } ``` **Step 1 — `ArrayList` declared:** The generic parameter `` accepts **only objects**. A primitive `85` cannot be stored directly. **Step 2 — Compiler inserts autoboxing at `marks.add(85)`:** The compiler rewrites this bytecode-equivalent call to `marks.add(Integer.valueOf(85))`. A new `Integer` object is created on the heap. **Step 3 — Same for `marks.add(92)`:** Becomes `marks.add(Integer.valueOf(92))`. **Step 4 — At `int total = marks.get(0) + marks.get(1)`:** Each `marks.get(i)` returns an `Integer` object. The compiler inserts unboxing: `.intValue()` on each operand. $$\text{total} = \text{marks.get(0).intValue()} + \text{marks.get(1).intValue()}$$ **Step 5 — Arithmetic evaluation:** $$\text{total} = 85 + 92 = 177$$ **Step 6 — Output:** ``` Total = 177 ``` ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — The Famous `Integer` Cache Trap **Problem:** Predict the output of: ```java Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100; Integer c = 200; Integer d = 200; System.out.println(a == b); System.out.println(c == d); ``` **Step 1 — Recall `Integer.valueOf(int)` caching:** Since Java 5, `Integer.valueOf(int)` caches frequently used values in the range **$-128$ to $127$** (configurable via `-XX:AutoBoxCacheMax` JVM flag, but the default is 127). **Step 2 — Autoboxing of `a = 100` and `b = 100`:** Both 100s resolve to the **same cached `Integer` object**. $$a \;\text{reference} == b \;\text{reference} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{true}$$ **Step 3 — Autoboxing of `c = 200` and `d = 200`:** 200 lies **outside** the default cache. Two **new** `Integer` objects are allocated on the heap. Their references differ. $$c \;\text{reference} \;\neq\; d \;\text{reference} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{false}$$ **Step 4 — Final output:** ``` true false ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Always use `.equals()` or `.intValue()` for logical equality, never `==` on wrapper objects (except for null checks). This is a **3-mark favourite**. ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Upcasting and Downcasting in Inheritance **Problem:** Predict the output: ```java class Animal { void speak() { System.out.println("..."); } } class Dog extends Animal { void speak() { System.out.println("Bark"); } void fetch() { System.out.println("Fetching!"); } } public class CastDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Animal a = new Dog(); a.speak(); ((Dog) a).fetch(); if (a instanceof Cat) { Cat c = (Cat) a; } else { System.out.println("Not a Cat"); } } } class Cat extends Animal { void speak() { System.out.println("Meow"); } } ``` **Step 1 — `Animal a = new Dog();` (Upcasting — implicit):** The reference type is `Animal`, the actual object type is `Dog`. This is **safe upcasting** — no cast operator needed. **Step 2 — `a.speak();` — Dynamic Dispatch:** At runtime, the JVM checks the actual object type. It dispatches to `Dog.speak()` due to polymorphism. $$\text{Output: Bark}$$ **Step 3 — `((Dog) a).fetch();` — Explicit Downcast:** `a` is declared as `Animal`, but `fetch()` is defined only in `Dog`. We must downcast explicitly. The actual object **is** a `Dog`, so the cast succeeds. $$\text{Output: Fetching!}$$ **Step 4 — `a instanceof Cat` check:** At runtime, RTTI confirms `a` is **not** a `Cat`. The `else` branch executes. $$\text{Output: Not a Cat}$$ **Step 5 — If we had naively written `Cat c = (Cat) a;` without the `instanceof` check, a `ClassCastException` would be thrown at runtime.** ## 3.7 Summary Table — Trace-Through Comparisons | Scenario | Compiler Action | Runtime Effect | Risk | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `int → long` | Implicit widening | None | None | | `double → int` | Inserts `(int)` cast | Truncates fraction | Precision loss | | `int → byte` | Inserts `(byte)` cast | Truncates high bits | Overflow wrap | | `int → Integer` | Inserts `Integer.valueOf()` | Allocates / uses cache | Memory overhead | | `Integer → int` | Inserts `.intValue()` | Reads internal value | `NullPointerException` | | `Dog → Animal` (upcast) | Implicit | None | None | | `Animal → Dog` (downcast) | Inserts `(Dog)` | Verifies actual type | `ClassCastException` | # 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Java Primitive Widening Ladder This diagram captures the **safe implicit conversion path** defined by JLS §5.1.2. Arrows point from narrower (source) to wider (target). Every arrow direction is a valid **implicit** cast. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeB[BYTE 8 bits] nodeS[SHORT 16 bits] nodeC[CHAR 16 bits unsigned] nodeI[INT 32 bits] nodeL[LONG 64 bits] nodeF[FLOAT 32 bits IEEE 754] nodeD[DOUBLE 64 bits IEEE 754] nodeB --> nodeS nodeB --> nodeI nodeB --> nodeL nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeD nodeS --> nodeI nodeS --> nodeL nodeS --> nodeF nodeS --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeL nodeC --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeD nodeI --> nodeL nodeI --> nodeF nodeI --> nodeD nodeL --> nodeF nodeL --> nodeD nodeF --> nodeD classDef safe fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 class nodeB,nodeS,nodeC,nodeI,nodeL,nodeF,nodeD safe ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** Notice that `char` **does not** point to `byte` or `short`. This is because `char` is unsigned 16-bit, while `byte`/`short` are signed. ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Autoboxing / Unboxing Compiler Pipeline This block diagram shows what happens "behind the scenes" when the Java compiler encounters a primitive ↔ wrapper interaction. ```mermaid flowchart TD srcA[Java Source Code
int x = 42
Integer obj = x] cmpA{Javac Compiler} bcA[Bytecode
iconst 42
invokestatic Integer.valueOf] jvmA[JVM Runtime
Heap Allocation or Cache Lookup] objA[Integer Object on Heap] srcA --> cmpA cmpA --> bcA bcA --> jvmA jvmA --> objA srcB[Java Source Code
Integer y = obj
int z = y] cmpB{Javac Compiler} bcB[Bytecode
invokevirtual Integer.intValue] jvmB[JVM Runtime
Read Internal Value] valB[Primitive int on Stack] srcB --> cmpB cmpB --> bcB bcB --> jvmB jvmB --> valB classDef src fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1 classDef cmp fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#f57f17,color:#e65100 classDef bc fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c classDef jvm fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#880e4f classDef obj fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 class srcA,srcB src class cmpA,cmpB cmp class bcA,bcB bc class jvmA,jvmB jvm class objA,valB obj ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Reference Casting (Upcasting vs Downcasting) in an Inheritance Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD objA[Animal Reference] objB[Dog Reference] objC[Cat Reference] real1[Actual Dog Object in Heap] real2[Actual Cat Object in Heap] objA -.upcast safe implicit.-> real1 objA -.upcast safe implicit.-> real2 objB -.downcast explicit instanceof check.-> real1 objC -.downcast explicit instanceof check.-> real2 objA -- if casted to Dog without check --> excA[ClassCastException at runtime] objA -- if casted to Cat without check --> excB[ClassCastException at runtime] classDef ref fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#01579b classDef heap fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 classDef exc fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c class objA,objB,objC ref class real1,real2 heap class excA,excB exc ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Architecture: Compiler & JVM Role in Boxing ```mermaid flowchart LR step1[Step 1: Programmer writes int x = 5] step2[Step 2: Compiler sees assignment to Integer] step3[Step 3: Compiler inserts Integer valueOf 5] step4[Step 4: Bytecode contains invokestatic Integer valueOf] step5[Step 5: JVM checks IntegerCache range -128 to 127] step6[Step 6: If in range return cached object] step7[Step 7: If out of range allocate new Integer on heap] step8[Step 8: Reference variable points to Integer object] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 step5 -- In Range --> step6 --> step8 step5 -- Out of Range --> step7 --> step8 ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define autoboxing and unboxing in Java. Write a small code snippet to demonstrate both.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion performed by the Java compiler from a primitive type to its corresponding wrapper class object. **Unboxing** is the reverse automatic conversion from a wrapper object to its primitive type. Both features were introduced in **Java 5** to simplify the interaction between primitives and the Collections Framework. > > ```java > public class BoxUnbox { > public static void main(String[] args) { > int n = 50; // primitive > Integer obj = n; // autoboxing > int m = obj; // unboxing > System.out.println("Boxed : " + obj); > System.out.println("Unboxed : " + m); > } > } > ``` > > **Output:** > ``` > Boxed : 50 > Unboxed : 50 > ``` > > **Mark Allocation (KTU pattern):** > - Definition of autoboxing: **1 mark** > - Definition of unboxing: **1 mark** > - Code snippet with output: **1 mark** ### ❓ Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the difference between widening and narrowing type casting in Java? Give one example of each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | Widening (Implicit) Casting | Narrowing (Explicit) Casting | > | :--- | :--- | :--- | > | Direction | Smaller type → Larger type | Larger type → Smaller type | > | Syntax | Automatic, no operator | Manual, requires `(type)` operator | > | Data Loss | No | Possible | > | Example | `int a = 10; double b = a;` | `double d = 10.75; int i = (int) d;` | > > In the narrowing example, the value of `i` becomes `10` because the fractional part `.75` is truncated, not rounded. > > **Mark Allocation:** > - Difference explanation: **1.5 marks** > - One example each: **1.5 marks** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **type casting** in Java with reference to primitive types. List the **widening and narrowing conversion rules** according to JLS. Write a Java program that demonstrates both widening and narrowing with appropriate comments on the output produced. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram and example, explain the **autoboxing and unboxing mechanism** in Java. Mention the role of the `IntegerCache` and predict the output of the following code: ```java Integer a = 128; Integer b = 128; System.out.println(a == b); System.out.println(a.equals(b)); ``` #### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Type casting** is the conversion of a value from one data type to another. The JLS specifies two categories: - **Widening (Implicit) Conversion** (§5.1.2): Happens automatically when converting a smaller type to a larger type, e.g., `byte → short → int → long → float → double`. This is **safe** and never loses data. - **Narrowing (Explicit) Conversion** (§5.1.3): Requires a cast operator `(targetType)`, e.g., `double → int`, `long → byte`. This **may** lose data because bits are truncated or fractions discarded. **Java Program:** ```java public class CastDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // 1. Widening (Implicit) - int to double int count = 100; double dCount = count; // No cast needed System.out.println("Widening int to double : " + dCount); // 2. Narrowing (Explicit) - double to int double price = 99.99; int whole = (int) price; // Cast operator required System.out.println("Narrowing double to int: " + whole); // 3. Narrowing with overflow - int to byte int big = 130; byte small = (byte) big; System.out.println("Narrowing int to byte : " + small); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Widening int to double : 100.0 Narrowing double to int: 99 Narrowing int to byte : -126 ``` **Explanation of `-126`:** `130` in 8-bit two's complement is `10000010`, which equals `-126` in signed interpretation. > **Mark Split (a):** Concept (2) + Rules (2) + Code (2) + Output (1) = **7 marks** **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion of a primitive to its wrapper object (e.g., `int` → `Integer`) by the Java compiler. **Unboxing** is the reverse. The compiler inserts calls to `valueOf()` and `xxxValue()` methods in the bytecode. **`IntegerCache`:** To save memory, the `Integer.valueOf()` method caches objects for values from **-128 to 127** (default). When a value falls in this range, the same cached object is returned; otherwise, a new object is allocated. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD p1[Primitive int 128] cc1{Is value in range -128 to 127} cache1[Return Cached Integer from IntegerCache] alloc1[Allocate new Integer object on heap] a1[Integer a = 128] b1[Integer b = 128] cmp1{Comparison using operator} res1[False because two different heap objects] res2[True because equals compares values] p1 --> cc1 cc1 -- No --> alloc1 cc1 -- Yes --> cache1 alloc1 --> a1 cache1 --> a1 alloc1 --> b1 cache1 --> b1 a1 --> cmp1 b1 --> cmp1 cmp1 -- operator == --> res1 a1 -- equals method --> res2 b1 -- equals method --> res2 ``` **Output Prediction:** ```java Integer a = 128; Integer b = 128; System.out.println(a == b); // false System.out.println(a.equals(b)); // true ``` Since `128` is outside the default cache range, `a` and `b` are **two different `Integer` objects** on the heap. The `==` operator compares **references**, hence `false`. The `.equals()` method compares **values**, hence `true`. > **Mark Split (b):** Concept + diagram (2) + IntegerCache explanation (2) + Output prediction with reasoning (3) = **7 marks** --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **upcasting and downcasting** with respect to Java inheritance. Write a Java program demonstrating both, including the use of the `instanceof` operator to avoid `ClassCastException`. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **scenarios where autoboxing and unboxing occur** in Java code. Explain with examples how unboxing a `null` wrapper leads to `NullPointerException`. #### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** | Aspect | Upcasting | Downcasting | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Direction | Subclass → Superclass | Superclass → Subclass | | Implicit/Explicit | Implicit (no cast needed) | Explicit cast operator required | | Safety | Always safe at runtime | Risky, may throw `ClassCastException` | | Common Use | Polymorphic method calls | Accessing subclass-specific features | **Java Program:** ```java class Vehicle { void run() { System.out.println("Vehicle running"); } } class Car extends Vehicle { void run() { System.out.println("Car running fast"); } void honk() { System.out.println("Beep! Beep!"); } } public class RefCast { public static void main(String[] args) { // 1. Upcasting (implicit, safe) Vehicle v = new Car(); v.run(); // Dynamic dispatch → "Car running fast" // 2. Downcasting with instanceof check if (v instanceof Car) { Car c = (Car) v; // Safe downcast c.honk(); // "Beep! Beep!" } else { System.out.println("Not a Car"); } // 3. Unsafe downcast would throw ClassCastException // Vehicle bad = new Vehicle(); // Car c2 = (Car) bad; // Runtime exception! } } ``` **Output:** ``` Car running fast Beep! Beep! ``` > **Mark Split (a):** Difference table (2) + Upcasting demo (2) + Downcasting with `instanceof` (3) = **7 marks** **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Scenarios where autoboxing/unboxing occurs:** 1. **Assignment to a variable of wrapper type:** ```java Integer x = 10; // autoboxing of 10 int y = x; // unboxing of x ``` 2. **Method arguments** when the parameter type is a wrapper: ```java public void display(Integer value) { ... } display(99); // autoboxing ``` 3. **Collections Framework (most important):** ```java ArrayList list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(5); // autoboxing int first = list.get(0); // unboxing ``` 4. **Arithmetic / Relational expressions** with mixed types: ```java Integer a = 5, b = 10; int sum = a + b; // both unboxed, then added if (a < b) { ... } // both unboxed for comparison ``` 5. **Ternary, increment, and compound assignment operators:** ```java Integer count = null; count++; // Unboxing fails here! ``` **NullPointerException on unboxing:** ```java Integer value = null; int n = value; // NullPointerException at runtime ``` Here, the compiler successfully inserts `n = value.intValue();`, but at runtime, the JVM dereferences a `null` reference, throwing `NullPointerException`. This is a very common production bug. > **Mark Split (b):** Five scenarios (5 × 1 = 5 marks) + NPE example with explanation (2 marks) = **7 marks** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Never** compare wrapper objects with `==` for value equality; KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks for this mistake. Use `.equals()` instead. > - **Forgetting the cast operator** in narrowing conversions is a compilation error. In the exam, the moment you write `int x = 9.5;` without `(int)`, the program is **invalid** — full zero for the code part. > - **Confusing truncation with rounding** in `(int) 9.99` costs the output mark. The answer is `9`, not `10`. > - **Forgetting to handle `null`** during unboxing is a frequently tested trap. Always mention NPE in your answer if the question touches unboxing. > - **Not stating "compiler-level transformation"** in autoboxing definitions loses 1 mark. The JVM does **not** autobox; only `javac` does. ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Widening = automatic, narrowing = explicit** with `(type)` cast operator. - ✅ **Java widening order:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double` and `char → int → long → float → double`. - ✅ `char` does **not** widen to `byte` or `short` (KTU classic). - ✅ Narrowing from `double` to `int` **truncates**, it does not round. - ✅ Narrowing from `int` to `byte` keeps the **lowest 8 bits**; high bits are discarded, causing potential overflow. - ✅ **Autoboxing** = primitive → wrapper, **Unboxing** = wrapper → primitive, both are **compiler-level** features introduced in **Java 5**. - ✅ The `IntegerCache` caches values from **-128 to 127**; `==` comparison works **only** within this range. - ✅ Always use `.equals()` for wrapper value comparison; use `==` for reference or null checks only. - ✅ **Upcasting** is safe and implicit; **downcasting** is explicit and requires an `instanceof` check to avoid `ClassCastException`. - ✅ Unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException` at runtime. - ✅ Boxing/unboxing is essential for storing primitives in `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, and other generic collections. - ✅ The eight wrapper classes live in `java.lang` and are **immutable**. - ✅ Common exam vocabulary: **"type promotion," "type demotion," "implicit conversion," "explicit conversion," "boxing," "unboxing," "IntegerCache," "instanceof check."**

topics.title: Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Array:** A **homogeneous**, **contiguous**, **indexed** data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type** under a single variable name, accessed using an integer index (subscript) representing the element's position relative to the base address.

**Key properties emphasized in the KTU OECST615 syllabus:**
- **Homogeneous** – All elements share a single data type (e.g., all `int`, all `float`, or all objects of a single class).
- **Contiguous Memory Allocation** – Elements reside in adjacent memory cells.
- **Random Access** – Any element can be reached in **O(1)** time using its index.
- **Static Size** – The size is fixed at compile-time (in C/C++ static arrays) and cannot grow/shrink dynamically without reallocation.
- **Zero-Indexed** – C/C++/Java arrays start at index `0`.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Locker Room"

Imagine a **school corridor with 50 numbered lockers** (Locker 0, Locker 1, …, Locker 49), each locker holding **only textbooks** (homogeneous). The corridor is one straight line, so every locker sits next to the other (contiguous). If a teacher wants the book in **Locker 23**, they don't search from Locker 0 onwards — they walk directly to Locker 23 using the **number painted on the door** (random access via index). The number of lockers is **fixed when the corridor is built** (static size).

| Locker Analogy | Array Concept |
|---|---|
| Corridor | Array variable name |
| 50 lockers | Size = 50 |
| Each locker = textbook | Element of same type |
| Locker number (0–49) | Index / Subscript |
| Door number painted | Base address + offset |
| Building a new corridor | Declaration |
| Filling lockers with books | Initialization |

### 1.3 Classification of Arrays (KTU Module 1 Scope)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 – Arrays** explicitly covers **One-Dimensional Arrays (1-D)**, **Two-Dimensional Arrays (2-D / Matrix)**, and a brief introduction to **Multi-Dimensional Arrays**, along with **Arrays of Objects**.

| Category | Description | Memory Shape |
|---|---|---|
| **One-Dimensional (1-D)** | Linear list of elements: `arr[0], arr[1], …, arr[n-1]` | Single row |
| **Two-Dimensional (2-D)** | Table with rows and columns: `mat[r][c]` | Matrix grid |
| **Multi-Dimensional** | 3-D or higher: `cube[x][y][z]` | Cube / Hyper-cube |
| **Array of Objects** | Each element is an instance of a class | Object references |

### 1.4 Array Declaration & Initialization (C++ Context — KTU Standard)

```cpp
// ---- 1-D Array ----
int marks[5];                          // Declaration (uninitialized)
int ages[5] = {18, 19, 20, 21, 22};    // Initialization
double temps[] = {36.5, 37.0, 38.1};   // Size inferred = 3

// ---- 2-D Array ----
int matrix[3][4];                      // 3 rows × 4 columns
int grid[2][3] = { {1, 2, 3},
                   {4, 5, 6} };       // Brace-enclosed initializer

// ---- Array of Objects (OOP-specific) ----
class Student { public: int roll; float cgpa; };
Student batch[60];                     // 60 Student objects
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Java** (also covered under OECST615), arrays are *objects* allocated on the heap, declared as `int[] arr = new int[5];` — but KTU Module 1 primarily uses **C++ syntax** unless specified otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Memory Architecture

Arrays are stored in **contiguous memory blocks**. The compiler allocates:
- A **base address** `B` (address of `arr[0]`),
- A **stride** `W` (size in bytes of one element),
- A total memory footprint of `n × W` bytes.

### 2.2 Address Calculation — The Heart of Array Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question in Module 1 frequently asks students to compute the **physical memory address** of a given element using the base address. Mastery of the formula below is non-negotiable.

**One-Dimensional Array Address Formula:**

$$
\text{Addr}(arr[i]) \;=\; B \;+\; i \times W
$$

Where:
- $B$ = Base address (address of first element `arr[0]`)
- $i$ = Index of the element
- $W$ = Size (in bytes) of one element

**Two-Dimensional Array Address Formula (Row-Major Order — used by C/C++/Python/Java):**

$$
\text{Addr}(a[i][j]) \;=\; B \;+\; \bigl(i \times n \;+\; j\bigr) \times W
$$

Where $n$ = number of **columns** per row.

**Two-Dimensional Array Address Formula (Column-Major Order — used by Fortran/MATLAB):**

$$
\text{Addr}(a[i][j]) \;=\; B \;+\; \bigl(j \times m \;+\; i\bigr) \times W
$$

Where $m$ = number of **rows** per column.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1-D Address | $\text{Addr}(arr[i]) = B + i \times W$ | Bytes |
| 2 | 2-D Row-Major | $\text{Addr}(a[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times W$ | $n$ = columns |
| 3 | 2-D Column-Major | $\text{Addr}(a[i][j]) = B + (j \times m + i) \times W$ | $m$ = rows |
| 4 | Total Memory | $n \times W$ | Bytes |
| 5 | Number of Elements 1-D | $N$ | — |
| 6 | Number of Elements 2-D | $R \times C$ | $R$ = rows, $C$ = cols |
| 7 | Last Valid Index 1-D | $N - 1$ | Zero-based indexing |
| 8 | Random Access Time | $\mathbf{O(1)}$ | Constant |
| 9 | Linear Search Time | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ | Worst & average case |
| 10 | Binary Search Time | $\mathbf{O(\log_2 n)}$ | Sorted array only |
| 11 | Insertion/Deletion (mid) | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ | Due to shifting |
| 12 | Traversal Time | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ | Visit each element once |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical bar `|` is intentionally avoided in all formulas above; KTU board examiners deduct marks for ambiguous delimiters — use parentheses or `\vert` for clarity.

### 2.4 Array Operations — Theory & Mechanics

Every array operation works on a fixed-size block. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes operations into:

#### 2.4.1 Traversal
Visiting every element exactly once. Time: **O(n)**.

#### 2.4.2 Insertion
- **At end:** O(1) if space available.
- **At position k:** O(n − k) shifts required.
- **When full:** Requires a **new larger array** + `copy` operation ⇒ O(n).

#### 2.4.3 Deletion
- **At position k:** O(n − k) left-shifts to close the gap.
- Element is *not* physically removed in C/C++ static arrays — the value is just overwritten.

#### 2.4.4 Searching
- **Linear Search:** Scan from index 0 → n−1. Works on unsorted arrays. **O(n)**.
- **Binary Search:** Repeatedly halve the search space. Requires **sorted** array. **O(log₂ n)**.

#### 2.4.5 Sorting (overview only in Module 1)
- **Bubble Sort:** O(n²) — adjacent swaps.
- **Selection Sort:** O(n²) — min/max selection.
- **Insertion Sort:** O(n²) — sorted prefix expansion.
- **Merge Sort / Quick Sort:** O(n log n) — divide & conquer.

### 2.5 Arrays of Objects — OOP-Specific Theory

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **OOP-specific twist** that distinguishes OECST615 from a generic Data Structures course: arrays whose elements are **user-defined class instances**.

```cpp
class Employee {
    int id;
    float salary;
public:
    void getData()  { cin >> id >> salary; }
    void putData()  { cout << id << " " << salary; }
};

Employee staff[100];        // Array of 100 Employee objects
for (int i = 0; i < 100; ++i)
    staff[i].getData();     // Each object invokes its member function
```

**Why it matters in engineering:** A payroll system stores employees in an array of objects; an inventory system stores products; a university system stores students. This pattern underpins nearly every enterprise C++ application.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Arrays |
|---|---|
| **Signal Processing** | Storing time-series samples of an ECG / EEG signal |
| **Image Processing** | Pixel intensity matrices (2-D arrays) |
| **Embedded Systems** | Sensor buffer arrays in microcontrollers |
| **Scientific Computing** | Finite-difference grids, weather modeling |
| **Database Engines** | Columnar storage of fixed-width records |
| **Game Development** | Tile maps, sprite arrays, leaderboards |

### 2.7 Limitations of Arrays (Asked as Direct Questions)

1. **Fixed size** → cannot grow at runtime without manual reallocation.
2. **Homogeneous** → cannot mix `int` and `float` in the same primitive array.
3. **Inefficient insertions/deletions** in the middle → O(n) shifting.
4. **No built-in bounds checking** in C/C++ (compilers like gcc may not flag `arr[10]` for a 10-element array — undefined behavior).
5. **Wasted memory** if declared size >> actual usage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Address Formula

**Given:**
- Base address $B$ of `arr[0]`.
- Each element occupies $W$ bytes.
- Array indices are $0, 1, 2, \dots, n-1$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address of } arr[0] &= B + 0 \times W = B \\
\text{Address of } arr[1] &= B + 1 \times W = B + W \\
\text{Address of } arr[2] &= B + 2 \times W = B + 2W \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\text{Address of } arr[i] &= B + i \times W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The compiler adds $W$ bytes for *each index jump*, which is why direct (random) access is **O(1)** — no scanning, just arithmetic.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 1-D Address Calculation (Typical KTU 3-Mark Question)

> **Problem:** An integer array `A` is declared in C++ as `int A[100];`. The base address of `A` is **2000**. Each integer occupies **4 bytes**. Find the address of `A[25]` and the total memory occupied.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= 2000 \\
W &= 4 \text{ bytes (size of int in most compilers)} \\
i &= 25 \\
\text{Addr}(A[25]) &= B + i \times W \\
&= 2000 + 25 \times 4 \\
&= 2000 + 100 \\
&= 2100 \\
\text{Total Memory} &= n \times W = 100 \times 4 = 400 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** `A[25]` is stored at byte address **2100**, and the array consumes **400 bytes** of memory.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the 2-D Row-Major Address Formula

**Given:** A 2-D array `a[m][n]` stored in **row-major order** (C/C++ convention). $W$ = element size.

**Logical flow:**

- Row $i$ contains $n$ elements. To reach row $i$, we must skip $i \times n$ elements.
- Within row $i$, column $j$ is the $(j)$-th element (zero-based).
- Total elements skipped before `a[i][j]` = $i \times n + j$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr}(a[i][j]) &= B + (i \times n + j) \times W
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — 2-D Row-Major Address (14-Mark Sub-Question Style)

> **Problem:** A 2-D array is defined as `int X[10][20];` with base address `B = 1000`, $W = 2$ bytes (assuming small int). Find the address of `X[3][5]` in **row-major** order and verify by listing the traversal order.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= 10 \text{ rows} \\
n &= 20 \text{ columns} \\
i &= 3,\;\; j = 5,\;\; B = 1000,\;\; W = 2 \\
\text{Addr}(X[3][5]) &= B + (i \times n + j) \times W \\
&= 1000 + (3 \times 20 + 5) \times 2 \\
&= 1000 + (60 + 5) \times 2 \\
&= 1000 + 65 \times 2 \\
&= 1000 + 130 \\
&= 1130
\end{aligned}
$$

**Memory traversal order (row-major):**
`X[0][0] → X[0][1] → … → X[0][19] → X[1][0] → X[1][1] → … → X[9][19]`

Each row occupies $20 \times 2 = 40$ bytes. Row 0 spans 1000–1039, Row 1 spans 1040–1079, Row 2 spans 1080–1119, Row 3 spans 1120–1159. Hence `X[3][5]` sits at 1120 + (5 × 2) = **1130** ✓

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — 2-D Column-Major Address (Conversion Practice)

> **Problem:** Same array as Example 2, but stored in **column-major** order. Find the address of `X[3][5]`.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr}(a[i][j]) &= B + (j \times m + i) \times W \\
m &= 10,\;\; n = 20 \\
i &= 3,\;\; j = 5,\;\; B = 1000,\;\; W = 2 \\
&= 1000 + (5 \times 10 + 3) \times 2 \\
&= 1000 + 53 \times 2 \\
&= 1000 + 106 \\
&= 1106
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Same logical element, different physical address** because storage order differs. KTU examiners often test this exact trap — students who blindly copy the row-major formula lose full marks.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Cost vs. Use-Case

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Precondition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Read by index | **O(1)** | O(1) | None | Direct element access |
| Linear Search | **O(n)** | O(1) | Unsorted allowed | Small or unsorted data |
| Binary Search | **O(log₂ n)** | O(1) | **Must be sorted** | Large sorted data |
| Insertion (end) | **O(1)** | O(1) | Capacity not full | Stack-like usage |
| Insertion (mid) | **O(n)** | O(1) | Shifting required | Rare in practice |
| Deletion | **O(n)** | O(1) | Shifting required | Queue maintenance |
| Bubble Sort | **O(n²)** | O(1) | None | Educational / tiny data |
| Quick Sort | **O(n log n)** avg | O(log n) | None | General purpose |

---

### 3.7 Worked Example 4 — Linear Search Algorithm Trace (C++)

> **Problem:** Search for key = `42` in `int A[7] = {15, 7, 42, 3, 91, 42, 8};` using linear search. Report the index of first occurrence and total comparisons.

**Trace Table:**

| Iteration (i) | A[i] | A[i] == 42 ? | Comparisons So Far | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 15 | No | 1 | Continue |
| 1 | 7  | No | 2 | Continue |
| 2 | 42 | **Yes** | **3** | **Return 2** |

**Answer:** First occurrence at index **2** after **3 comparisons**. Worst case (absent) = 7 comparisons = **O(n)**.

**C++ Implementation:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int linearSearch(int A[], int n, int key) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
        if (A[i] == key) return i;        // Found
    return -1;                            // Not found
}

int main() {
    int A[7] = {15, 7, 42, 3, 91, 42, 8};
    int idx = linearSearch(A, 7, 42);
    cout << "Index: " << idx;             // Output: Index: 2
    return 0;
}
```

---

### 3.8 Worked Example 5 — Binary Search Trace (Sorted Array)

> **Problem:** Search for key = `73` in sorted `int A[8] = {5, 12, 23, 41, 58, 73, 84, 96};`

**Trace Table:**

| Step | low | high | mid | A[mid] | A[mid] vs 73 | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 7 | 3 | 41  | 41 < 73  | low = mid+1 = 4 |
| 2 | 4 | 7 | 5 | 73  | 73 == 73 | **Return 5** |

**Answer:** Found at index **5** in **log₂ 8 = 3** maximum iterations. Demonstrates **O(log n)** efficiency.

**C++ Implementation:**

```cpp
int binarySearch(int A[], int n, int key) {
    int low = 0, high = n - 1;
    while (low <= high) {
        int mid = (low + high) / 2;
        if (A[mid] == key)   return mid;
        else if (A[mid] < key) low = mid + 1;
        else                  high = mid - 1;
    }
    return -1;
}
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — 1-D Array Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0["arr 0  : 2000"]:::cell --> A1["arr 1  : 2004"]:::cell
    A1 --> A2["arr 2  : 2008"]:::cell
    A2 --> A3["arr 3  : 2012"]:::cell
    A3 --> A4["arr 4  : 2016"]:::cell
    A4 --> A5["arr 5  : 2020"]:::cell

    B["Base Address B = 2000"]:::meta --> A0
    W["W = 4 bytes per int"]:::meta --> A1
    N["n = 6 elements"]:::meta --> A5

    classDef cell fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91
    classDef meta  fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#B58900,stroke-width:2px,color:#6B4F00
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — 2-D Array (Row-Major) Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ROW0["Row 0  -->  Base B = 1000"]
        R0C0["a 0 0 : 1000"]:::rcell
        R0C1["a 0 1 : 1002"]:::rcell
        R0C2["a 0 2 : 1004"]:::rcell
    end
    subgraph ROW1["Row 1  -->  B + n x W = 1006"]
        R1C0["a 1 0 : 1006"]:::rcell
        R1C1["a 1 1 : 1008"]:::rcell
        R1C2["a 1 2 : 1010"]:::rcell
    end
    subgraph ROW2["Row 2  -->  B + 2n x W = 1012"]
        R2C0["a 2 0 : 1012"]:::rcell
        R2C1["a 2 1 : 1014"]:::rcell
        R2C2["a 2 2 : 1016"]:::rcell
    end

    R0C0 --> R0C1 --> R0C2 --> R1C0 --> R1C1 --> R1C2 --> R2C0 --> R2C1 --> R2C2

    classDef rcell fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D1F
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Array Operations Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START(["Array A size N"]):::start --> OP{"Choose Operation"}:::dec
    OP -->|1| TRV["Traverse: visit A 0 to A N-1"]:::op
    OP -->|2| INS["Insert: shift right from position k"]:::op
    OP -->|3| DEL["Delete: shift left from position k"]:::op
    OP -->|4| SRC{"Sorted?"}:::dec
    SRC -->|No| LIN["Linear Search O n"]:::op
    SRC -->|Yes| BIN["Binary Search O log n"]:::op
    OP -->|5| SRT["Sorting O n log n or O n2"]:::op
    OP -->|6| UPD["Update A i = newVal  O 1"]:::op

    TRV --> DONE(["Done"]):::end
    INS --> DONE
    DEL --> DONE
    LIN --> DONE
    BIN --> DONE
    SRT --> DONE
    UPD --> DONE

    classDef start fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A1F12
    classDef end   fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A1F12
    classDef dec   fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#B58900,stroke-width:2px,color:#6B4F00
    classDef op    fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Array of Objects Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ARR["Employee staff 100"]:::arr --> E0["staff 0 : id salary"]:::obj
    ARR --> E1["staff 1 : id salary"]:::obj
    ARR --> E2["staff 2 : id salary"]:::obj
    ARR --> Edots["..."]:::obj
    ARR --> E99["staff 99 : id salary"]:::obj

    E0 --> M1["getData  putData"]:::meth
    E1 --> M1
    E2 --> M1
    E99 --> M1

    classDef arr  fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A1F12
    classDef obj  fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D1F
    classDef meth fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU valuation:** Examiners reward schematic representations in 14-mark answers. A well-labeled memory-layout diagram instantly earns 2–3 content marks even if subsequent derivations contain minor slips.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an array. List any four properties of arrays in C++.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **array** is a collection of variables of the **same data type** stored in **contiguous memory locations** and accessed using a common name with an **index**.

**Four properties:**
1. **Homogeneous** – All elements share the same data type.
2. **Contiguous memory allocation** – Elements occupy adjacent memory cells.
3. **Fixed size** – Size is decided at compile-time and cannot change dynamically.
4. **Random access** – Any element is reachable in **O(1)** time using its index.
5. *(Bonus for full marks)* Zero-based indexing in C/C++.

*(Any 4 well-explained properties ⇒ 3 marks; definition alone ⇒ 1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between one-dimensional and two-dimensional arrays. Give one example declaration for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | One-Dimensional | Two-Dimensional |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Linear list | Table / Matrix |
| Syntax | `int arr[5];` | `int mat[3][4];` |
| Indexing | Single index `arr[i]` | Two indices `mat[i][j]` |
| Visualization | A row | A grid with rows & columns |
| Memory | Contiguous single block | Contiguous block stored row-major or column-major |

**Example declarations:**
- 1-D: `float prices[10];`
- 2-D: `int marks[50][3];` (50 students × 3 subjects)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Two fully independent alternatives are provided. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
