```java
        System.out.println("Maximum from array = " + result3);
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{Maximum value = 89} \\
&\texttt{No-argument call returns = -2147483648} \\
&\texttt{Maximum from array = -1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- The method accepts any number of `int` values via `int... numbers`.
- If the array is empty (`length == 0`), the sentinel value `Integer.MIN_VALUE` is returned.
- Otherwise, we initialize `max` with the first element and traverse the rest, updating `max` whenever a larger value is encountered.
- When passing an array directly (`findMax(data)`), no new array is allocated — the existing reference is reused.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> List and explain the **four golden rules** that must be followed when declaring a method with variable length arguments in Java. Provide an **invalid** and a **valid** declaration example for each rule.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> Explain the **method resolution priority** followed by the Java compiler when an overloaded method call could match both a varargs method and a non-varargs method. Illustrate with a **complete Java program** showing all four tiers.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Four Rules of Varargs (7 Marks)

**Rule 1: Only ONE varargs parameter is allowed (2 marks)**

```java
// ❌ Invalid
void method(int... a, String... b) { }

// ✅ Valid
void method(int... a) { }
```

**Rule 2: Varargs parameter must be the LAST parameter (2 marks)**

```java
// ❌ Invalid — varargs not last
void method(int... nums, String name) { }

// ✅ Valid — varargs is last
void method(String name, int... nums) { }
```

**Rule 3: Varargs can be combined with regular parameters (1.5 marks)**

```java
// ✅ Valid — fixed parameter before varargs
void greet(String greeting, String... names) {
    System.out.print(greeting + " ");
    for (String n : names) {
        System.out.print(n + " ");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
```

**Rule 4: Calling can be done with zero, one, many, or a pre-built array (1.5 marks)**

```java
sum();              // zero args → new int[0]
sum(1, 2, 3);       // many args → new int[]{1, 2, 3}
sum(existingArray); // direct array → no new allocation
```

#### Part (b) — Method Resolution Priority + Java Program (7 Marks)

**Priority Order (3 marks):**

The compiler resolves overloaded calls using a strict 4-tier priority:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Tier 1: Exact match (no conversion)} \\
&\text{Tier 2: Widening primitive conversion} \\
&\text{Tier 3: Autoboxing / Unboxing} \\
&\text{Tier 4: Varargs (wrap into array)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **most specific match wins**. Varargs is the *last* fallback.

**Java Program (4 marks):**

```java
public class ResolutionDemo {

    // Tier 1: Exact match
    static void display(int x) {
        System.out.println("Tier 1: exact int match → " + x);
    }

    // Tier 2: Widening
    static void display(long x) {
        System.out.println("Tier 2: widened to long → " + x);
    }

    // Tier 3: Autoboxing
    static void display(Integer x) {
        System.out.println("Tier 3: autoboxed to Integer → " + x);
    }

    // Tier 4: Varargs
    static void display(int... x) {
        System.out.println("Tier 4: varargs (array length = " + x.length + ")");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        display(100);          // → Tier 1 wins
        System.out.println();

        // Force Tier 2 by removing Tier 1
        // (comment out display(int x) and re-run)
    }
}
```

**Output (with all four overloads):**

$$
\texttt{Tier 1: exact int match → 100}
$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- All four overloads can technically accept an `int` value.
- However, **Tier 1 (exact match)** is the most specific — no conversion is needed.
- The compiler therefore binds the call to `display(int x)`.
- If `display(int x)` were removed, the compiler would try Tier 2, then Tier 3, then Tier 4 — in that exact order.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing varargs in the wrong position** (e.g., `void m(int... x, String s)`) — loses 2 marks immediately as it does not compile.
> 2. **Saying varargs becomes `null` when called with zero arguments** — wrong; it becomes an empty array `new T[0]`.
> 3. **Forgetting that varargs is internally an array** — answer is considered incomplete without this mention.
> 4. **Confusing varargs priority with autoboxing** — the correct order is **Exact → Widen → Autobox → Varargs**, not the reverse.
> 5. **Not mentioning Java 5 as the version that introduced varargs** — a 0.5-mark deduction is common.
> 6. **In Part B programs, forgetting to handle the zero-argument case** — leads to `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` and full-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024):**

- **Definition:** Varargs allows a method to accept **zero or more arguments** of the same type using the `...` (ellipsis) syntax.
- **Java version:** Introduced in **Java 5 (J2SE 5.0)** in 2004.
- **Internal type:** `T... x` is treated as `T[] x` by the compiler.
- **Rule 1:** Only **one** varargs parameter per method.
- **Rule 2:** Varargs must be the **last** parameter.
- **Rule 3:** Varargs can coexist with **regular parameters** placed before it.
- **Rule 4:** Zero-argument call results in a **new empty array** (`length = 0`), not `null`.
- **Direct array passing:** Allowed, and **no new array** is allocated in this case.
- **Resolution priority (in order):** **Exact Match → Widening → Autoboxing → Varargs**.
- **Iteration idiom:** Always use the enhanced for-loop: `for (T x : param) { }`.
- **Common library uses:** `System.out.printf`, `String.format`, `Arrays.asList`.
- **Ambiguity warning:** Combining `int...` and `Integer...` overloads can lead to compile errors.
- **Overload design tip:** When a method accepts 1, 2, or 3 specific parameters, prefer **fixed overloads** over varargs for performance clarity.
- **Exam buzzword:** Always say "**the compiler wraps the arguments into an array**" when explaining varargs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **class** in Object-Oriented Programming is a **user-defined composite datatype** that serves as a **blueprint/prototype/template** for creating objects. It encapsulates **data members (attributes/state)** and **member functions (methods/behaviour)** into a single logical unit, thereby binding data and operations together under one scope. A class does not occupy memory by itself; memory is allocated only when an **object (instance)** of that class is created.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Blueprint Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering Blueprint Analogy:** Imagine a **civil engineering architectural blueprint** of a duplex villa. The blueprint itself is *not* a house — you cannot live in it. It merely describes **how a house should look**: number of rooms, dimensions of windows, placement of doors, colour of paint. From a *single blueprint*, a builder can construct **many physical houses**, each on a different plot, with the *same structural plan* but possibly *different interior colours*.

> **Mapping to OOP:**
> - The **blueprint** = the **Class** (template, no memory)
> - Each **physical house** = an **Object** (instance, real memory)
> - **Room sizes, door count** = **Data Members** (attributes)
> - **"Open the door", "Switch on light"** = **Member Functions** (behaviour)

### 1.2 Why Classes Matter — The OOP Motivation

| Without Classes (Procedural) | With Classes (OOP) |
|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Data and functions are **decoupled** | Data and functions are **tightly coupled** |
| Global data exposed to all functions | Data is **hidden** using access specifiers |
| Reusability requires re-coding | Reusability via **objects/inheritance** |
| Real-world entities hard to model | Real-world entities mapped **1-to-1** |
| Maintenance is **error-prone** | Maintenance is **localized & safe** |

### 1.3 Standard Class Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard KTU 2024 Vocabulary to Memorize:**
> - **Class** — A logical template; occupies **0 bytes** in memory until instantiated.
> - **Object/Instance** — A concrete realization of a class occupying **heap/stack memory**.
> - **Data Members / Attributes / State Variables** — Variables that hold the *state* of an object.
> - **Member Functions / Methods / Behaviour** — Functions that operate on the data members.
> - **Access Specifier** — Keyword (`public`, `private`, `protected`) controlling *visibility* of members.
> - **Encapsulation** — The act of binding data + functions + access control inside a class.
> - **Size of an empty class in C++ = 1 byte** (to ensure unique addressability of objects).

### 1.4 Generic Class Declaration Syntax (Language-Neutral)

The conceptual structure of any class declaration follows this canonical pattern:

```
Class  ClassName
  AccessSpecifier_1:
      data_member_1
      data_member_2
      member_function_1()
      member_function_2()
  AccessSpecifier_2:
      ...
```

In **C++** (the standard KTU-OOP implementation language), this becomes:

```cpp
class ClassName {
private:            // Hidden by default
    int dataMember;
public:             // Publicly accessible
    void memberFunction();
protected:          // Accessible to derived classes
    int protectedMember;
};
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Class — The Five Logical Layers

A KTU-style class declaration is decomposed into **five logical layers**, each answering a specific design question:

1. **Class Header (Visibility Tag)**
   - *Question answered:* "Is this a `class` or a `struct`?"
   - In C++, the only **technical** difference is the **default access specifier**:
     - `class` → members are **private** by default
     - `struct` → members are **public** by default
   - *Why it matters:* This subtle distinction is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question** in viva and Part-A.

2. **Class Name (Identifier)**
   - Must follow C++ identifier rules: starts with letter/underscore, no reserved keyword, case-sensitive.
   - *KTU Convention:* Class names start with an **uppercase letter** (e.g., `Student`, `BankAccount`).

3. **Access Specifiers (Visibility Labels)**
   - The **three pillars of access control**:

| Specifier | Inside Class | Derived Class | Outside (main/object) |
|-----------|:------------:|:-------------:|:---------------------:|
| `private` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `protected` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `public` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

4. **Data Members (State)**
   - **Non-static data members** belong to **each object** independently (one copy per object).
   - **Static data members** belong to the **class as a whole** (single shared copy across all objects).

5. **Member Functions (Behaviour)**
   - **Non-static member functions** receive an implicit `this` pointer.
   - **Static member functions** do **NOT** receive `this` and can access **only static** members.

### 2.2 Two Styles of Member Function Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD DISTINCTION — "Inside vs Outside the Class":**

| Definition Style | Treated as | `inline` by Default? | Effect on OOP |
|------------------|------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Inside** class body | Implicitly `inline` | ✅ Yes | All functions small → faster |
| **Outside** class body | Normal function | ❌ No (unless declared `inline`) | Better **separation of interface and implementation** |

> **Why "Outside" definition matters in OOP:**
> - Keeps the **class declaration clean** (only the interface/prototype is visible).
> - Allows function code to be placed in a **separate `.cpp` file** for modular compilation.
> - Uses the **Scope Resolution Operator (`::`)** to bind the function to its class.

### 2.3 The Scope Resolution Operator (`::`)

> [!NOTE]
> The **scope resolution operator** is the **glue** that connects a function defined *outside* a class back to its parent class. Its general form is:
> **`ReturnType ClassName :: functionName (parameters) { body }`**

**Why it is needed:**
Without `::`, the compiler would treat `display()` as a **global function** unrelated to `Student`. The operator explicitly says: *"This `display` belongs to the `Student` class."*

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Class & Object Memory Model**

| # | Property | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|----------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Size of empty class | $\text{sizeof}(\text{EmptyClass}) = 1 \text{ byte}$ | C++ guarantee for unique address |
| 2 | Size of object | $\text{sizeof}(\text{Object}) = \sum \text{sizeof}(\text{non-static data members}) + \text{padding}$ | **Functions do NOT add to size** |
| 3 | Number of objects | $\text{Memory}_{\text{used}} = N \times \text{sizeof}(\text{Class})$ | Each object gets its own data copy |
| 4 | Static data member | $\text{sizeof}(\text{Object}) \text{ does NOT include static members}$ | Class-level, not object-level |
| 5 | Access boundary | $\text{private} \subset \text{protected} \subset \text{public}$ | Strict subset hierarchy |
| 6 | Default access — `class` | All unlabelled members → `private` | Common KTU trick question |
| 7 | Default access — `struct` | All unlabelled members → `public` | Inverse of `class` |
| 8 | Number of `this` pointers | Exactly **1 per non-static member function call** | Hidden, implicit parameter |
| 9 | `this` pointer type | `ClassName * const` (constant pointer to object) | Cannot reassign `this` |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Classes in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Application Matrix:**

| Engineering Domain | Class Example | Data Members | Member Functions |
|--------------------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| Civil — Structural Design | `Beam` | length, breadth, grade | `computeMoment()`, `deflect()` |
| Mechanical — CAD | `Gear` | teeth, module, pressureAngle | `computePitch()`, `drawProfile()` |
| Electrical — Circuit Sim | `Resistor` | resistance, tolerance, power | `computeCurrent()`, `dissipateHeat()` |
| CS — Database | `StudentRecord` | rollNo, name, cgpa | `insert()`, `update()`, `fetch()` |
| CS — GUI | `Button` | label, x, y, width, height | `onClick()`, `render()`, `disable()` |

### 2.6 Special Class Categories (KTU Module-1 Coverage)

1. **Nested Class** — A class declared *inside* another class. Used for tightly-coupled helper types.
2. **Local Class** — A class declared *inside a function body*. Scope limited to that function.
3. **Empty Class** — A class with no members. Size = **1 byte** by C++ guarantee.
4. **Static Member Class Concept** — Class having `static` data/function members belonging to the class itself.
5. **Friend Class / Function** — A non-member that is *granted* private access to a class.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive — *step by step* — the **memory model**, **access resolution**, and **member function binding** of a class, transforming a class declaration into a working, instantiated object.

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — From Class Declaration to Object Memory Map

**Problem Setup:** Consider the following C++ class declaration.

```cpp
class Student {
    int rollNo;        // 4 bytes
    char section;      // 1 byte
    double cgpa;       // 8 bytes
public:
    void display();    // 0 bytes (function, not data)
    void setData(int, char, double);
};
```

**Derivation — Step 1: Identify data members only.**

$$
\text{Data Members} = \{\text{rollNo},\; \text{section},\; \text{cgpa}\}
$$

> Functions (`display`, `setData`) are **excluded** because they occupy code memory, **not** object memory.

**Derivation — Step 2: Compute the raw sum of data-member sizes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Size} &= \text{sizeof(int)} + \text{sizeof(char)} + \text{sizeof(double)} \\
&= 4 + 1 + 8 \\
&= 13 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation — Step 3: Apply structure-padding rules.**

The compiler aligns each member to the size of the **largest** member (here, `double` = 8 bytes). It also aligns the *total* object size to the largest alignment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset of rollNo}  &= 0 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{4-byte aligned}) \\
\text{Offset of section} &= 4 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{1-byte aligned}) \\
\text{Padding}           &= 3 \text{ bytes after section} \\
\text{Offset of cgpa}    &= 8 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{8-byte aligned}) \\
\text{Total size}        &= 8 + 8 = 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result (KTU Viva/Exam Ready):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{sizeof}(\text{Student}) = 16 \text{ bytes}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Access Specifier Resolution

**Problem:** Determine whether each access is **Allowed** or **Denied**.

```cpp
class Bank {
    double balance;            // private
public:
    void deposit(double amt);  // public
protected:
    int pin;                   // protected
};
```

| Access Attempt | Location | Member | Allowed? | Reason |
|----------------|----------|--------|:--------:|--------|
| `b.balance` | `main()` (outside) | `private` | ❌ | Outside cannot see private |
| `b.deposit(500)` | `main()` (outside) | `public` | ✅ | Public — universally visible |
| `b.pin` | `main()` (outside) | `protected` | ❌ | Outside cannot see protected |
| `this->balance` | Inside `Bank::deposit()` | `private` | ✅ | Same class — full access |
| `this->pin` | Inside `Bank::deposit()` | `protected` | ✅ | Same class — full access |
| `obj.pin` | Inside `Savings` (derived) | `protected` | ✅ | Derived class allowed |

### 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Member Function Definition (Inside vs Outside)

**Step-by-step transformation of an inside definition into an outside definition using `::`:**

**Version A — Function defined INSIDE the class:**

```cpp
class Box {
public:
    void setVolume(int l, int b, int h) {   // Inside → implicit inline
        cout << l*b*h;
    }
};
```

**Version B — Function defined OUTSIDE the class (preferred in KTU modular design):**

```cpp
class Box {
public:
    void setVolume(int l, int b, int h);    // Declaration only (prototype)
};

// Outside definition with scope resolution:
void Box :: setVolume(int l, int b, int h) {
    cout << l * b * h;
}
```

**Step-by-step mapping:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inside:}  \quad & \texttt{void setVolume(...)} \quad \xRightarrow{\text{Extract}} \quad \texttt{void setVolume(...)} \\
\text{Outside:} \quad & \texttt{void Box :: setVolume(...)} \quad \xRightarrow{\text{Add}} \quad \texttt{Box ::}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The `::` operator is **mandatory** in the outside definition; omitting it causes a *compile error* (function treated as global, signature mismatch with prototype).

### 3.4 Worked Derivation #4 — Object Instantiation Memory Model

Given `Student s1, s2, s3;` (three objects of `Student` class of size 16 bytes each):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total memory} &= N_{\text{objects}} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{Student}) \\
&= 3 \times 16 \\
&= 48 \text{ bytes (on stack)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each object gets **independent copies** of `rollNo`, `section`, `cgpa` — but **shares** a single copy of any `static` member and the function code.

### 3.5 Worked Derivation #5 — The `this` Pointer Invariant

For any non-static member function call `obj.func(args)`, the compiler implicitly rewrites it as:

$$
\text{func}(\text{obj}, \text{args}) \quad \text{and sets} \quad \texttt{this} = \texttt{\&obj}
$$

> **Why this matters:** The `this` pointer is what allows two objects `s1` and `s2` of the same class to have **independent state** even though they share the same function code.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1.** *Define a class. How is it different from an object? Illustrate with a suitable example. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A **class** is a user-defined blueprint that defines the attributes (data members) and behaviours (member functions) common to a category of entities. It is a **logical template** that does not consume memory by itself. An **object**, in contrast, is a **runtime instance** of a class that occupies actual memory in the stack or heap, and each object maintains its own independent state.

*Example:* `class Car { ... };` defines a class. `Car c1, c2;` creates two distinct objects of that class, each with its own `speed`, `fuel`, etc.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]**
>
> **Q2.** *What is the significance of access specifiers in a class? List the three access specifiers in C++ and state their default visibility in a `class` and a `struct`. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Access specifiers control the **visibility and accessibility** of class members, forming the basis of **encapsulation** and **data hiding** in OOP. The three access specifiers in C++ are:

| Specifier | Default in `class` | Default in `struct` |
|-----------|:------------------:|:-------------------:|
| `private` | ✅ **Yes** (default) | ❌ No |
| `protected` | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `public` | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** (default) |

They prevent unauthorized access to internal data and enforce a clean **public interface** to outside code.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** *Design a C++ class named `Employee` with the following private data members: `empId` (int), `name` (char[40]), `basicSalary` (double), and `department` (char[20]). Include appropriate public member functions to read, display, and compute the net salary (HRA = 20% of basic, DA = 10% of basic, PF deduction = 8% of basic). Define the member functions **outside the class** using the scope resolution operator. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *Demonstrate the creation of three `Employee` objects, the use of the dot operator for member access, and explain with a memory diagram why each object maintains independent state despite sharing the same function code. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>
using namespace std;

class Employee {
    int empId;
    char name[40];
    double basicSalary;
    char department[20];
public:
    void readData();
    void displayData();
    double computeNetSalary();
};

void Employee :: readData() {
    cout << "Enter EmpID, Name, Basic Salary, Department: ";
    cin >> empId;
    cin.ignore();
    cin.getline(name, 40);
    cin >> basicSalary;
    cin.ignore();
    cin.getline(department, 20);
}

void Employee :: displayData() {
    cout << "ID: " << empId 
         << " | Name: " << name 
         << " | Dept: " << department 
         << " | Basic: " << basicSalary << endl;
}

double Employee :: computeNetSalary() {
    double hra = 0.20 * basicSalary;
    double da  = 0.10 * basicSalary;
    double pf  = 0.08 * basicSalary;
    return basicSalary + hra + da - pf;
}
```

**Explanation of key elements:**

- The class `Employee` declares **4 private data members** and **3 public member functions**.
- Each function is **defined outside** using the `::` (scope resolution) operator — e.g., `void Employee :: displayData()`.
- `computeNetSalary` returns a `double` and computes $\text{Net} = \text{Basic} + 0.20 \cdot \text{Basic} + 0.10 \cdot \text{Basic} - 0.08 \cdot \text{Basic}$.

$$
\text{Net Salary} = \text{Basic} \times (1 + 0.20 + 0.10 - 0.08) = 1.22 \times \text{Basic}
$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

```cpp
int main() {
    Employee e1, e2, e3;   // Three independent objects

    e1.readData();
    e2.readData();
    e3.readData();

    e1.displayData();
    cout << "Net Salary: " << e1.computeNetSalary() << endl;
    e2.displayData();
    cout << "Net Salary: " << e2.computeNetSalary() << endl;
    e3.displayData();
    cout << "Net Salary: " << e3.computeNetSalary() << endl;

    return 0;
}
```

**Memory Model Explanation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sizeof(Employee)} &= 4 + 40 + 8 + 20 = 72 \text{ bytes (approx, with padding)} \\
\text{sizeof(e1) + sizeof(e2) + sizeof(e3)} &= 3 \times 72 = 216 \text{ bytes (independent)} \\
\text{Function code (readData, displayData, computeNetSalary)} &= 1 \text{ shared copy in code segment}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each object stores its **own copy** of the 4 data members in a unique memory region; the function code is **shared** and identified at call-time using the hidden `this` pointer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** *Explain the three access specifiers in C++ with a sample class. Construct a table showing which specifier allows access from (i) within the same class, (ii) from a derived class, and (iii) from an outside function. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *Write a complete C++ program that defines a class `Rectangle` with `length` and `breadth` as private data members, member functions `setDim()`, `getArea()`, and `getPerimeter()` defined **inside** the class. Create two objects, display their areas and perimeters, and comment on why the inside-defined functions are treated as `inline`. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

```cpp
class AccessDemo {
private:    int a;        // Accessible only inside this class
protected:  int b;        // Accessible in this + derived classes
public:     int c;        // Accessible everywhere
};
```

| Access Location | `private` | `protected` | `public` |
|-----------------|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|
| (i) Same Class | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| (ii) Derived Class | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| (iii) Outside Function | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

**Model Solution to (b):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Rectangle {
    double length, breadth;
public:
    void setDim(double l, double b) {   // INSIDE → implicit inline
        length = l; breadth = b;
    }
    double getArea() {                   // INSIDE → implicit inline
        return length * breadth;
    }
    double getPerimeter() {              // INSIDE → implicit inline
        return 2 * (length + breadth);
    }
};

int main() {
    Rectangle r1, r2;
    r1.setDim(10.0, 5.0);
    r2.setDim(7.0, 3.0);

    cout << "R1 Area: " << r1.getArea() 
         << " | R1 Perimeter: " << r1.getPerimeter() << endl;
    cout << "R2 Area: " << r2.getArea() 
         << " | R2 Perimeter: " << r2.getPerimeter() << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Inline Explanation:**
A function defined **inside** a class body is treated by the C++ compiler as an **inline candidate** — the compiler *may* replace the function call with the actual function body at the call site, eliminating call overhead. This is the reason small, frequently-called member functions are conventionally defined **inside** the class.

$$
\text{Output: } \text{R1 Area} = 50, \quad \text{R1 Perimeter} = 30, \quad \text{R2 Area} = 21, \quad \text{R2 Perimeter} = 20
$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in "Classes" questions — Common pitfalls observed in KTU valuation scripts:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `::` operator** when defining functions outside the class → Compiler treats it as a global function → Linker error → **Full 7-mark deduction** in program questions.
> 2. **Confusing `class` default access with `struct` default access** → A classic 2-mark "trick" question. Always state: `class` → private, `struct` → public.
> 3. **Including member functions in `sizeof()` calculation** → Functions reside in the **code segment**, not in object memory. Marks deducted if the derivation is wrong.
> 4. **Forgetting padding alignment** in object size derivations → Many students compute raw sums like `4+1+8=13` and forget that the compiler aligns to the largest member's size, making the answer **16 bytes**, not 13.
> 5. **Declaring data members as `public`** — violates encapsulation principles → Examiners deduct at least 1–2 marks even if the program runs.
> 6. **Missing `inline` keyword awareness** — students often miss writing that *inside-class* function definitions are **implicitly inline** but the compiler has the final say (inline is a *request*, not a *command*).
> 7. **Mismatched function signature** between the inside prototype and outside definition (parameter type, return type) → Compilation fails → 0 marks for that sub-question.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Classes" (Module 1, KTU 2024)**

- ⭐ A **class** is a *blueprint*; an **object** is an *instance*. Class itself occupies **0 bytes** (memory is allocated only on instantiation).
- ⭐ The **default access** in a `class` is `private`; the **default access** in a `struct` is `public`. This is the *only* technical difference.
- ⭐ The **three access specifiers** are `private`, `protected`, `public` — with visibility strictly expanding: `private` ⊂ `protected` ⊂ `public`.
- ⭐ Functions defined **inside** the class are *implicitly* `inline`; functions defined **outside** must use the **scope resolution operator `::`** to bind to the class.
- ⭐ The `sizeof` of an **empty class** is exactly **1 byte** (C++ guarantee for unique address).
- ⭐ `sizeof(object)` = sum of `sizeof` of all **non-static data members** + **padding**. Member functions do **not** contribute.
- ⭐ Each object has its own **independent copy** of non-static data members but **shares** the function code (and any static members).
- ⭐ Every non-static member function receives a hidden `this` pointer of type `ClassName * const`, pointing to the calling object.
- ⭐ **Encapsulation** = Data + Functions + Access Control bundled inside a class. It is the **core OOP advantage** over procedural programming.
- ⭐ The `::` operator's general form: `ReturnType ClassName :: memberFunction (params) { body }`.
- ⭐ **Common KTU exam keywords** to recognize: "blueprint", "data hiding", "encapsulation", "scope resolution", "inline by default", "object instantiation", "memory layout".

> *End of Notes — Classes (Module 1, OECST615, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Programming Paradigms

A **Programming Paradigm** is a fundamental style or approach to building the structure and elements of computer programs. Two of the most dominant paradigms in software engineering are **Procedural Programming** and **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to clearly differentiate between Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming paradigms and to articulate the **"Four Pillars of OOP"** — Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism — with real-world analogies and code-level understanding.

### Procedural Programming Paradigm

> **Formal Definition:** A programming paradigm based on the concept of **procedure calls**, where statements are structured into procedures (also known as functions or subroutines). It follows a **top-down** approach, where a problem is decomposed into smaller functions that operate on data.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Old Kitchen Recipe":**
Imagine a chef in a traditional kitchen. The chef (the *function*) is given raw ingredients (*data*) one at a time. The chef performs a series of steps (procedures) like chopping, boiling, frying — and produces a dish. Here, the *chef and the ingredients are separate entities*. The data flows through the functions. This is **Procedural Programming** — *data is open, functions operate on it, and there is no binding between the two.*

### Object-Oriented Programming Paradigm

> **Formal Definition:** A programming paradigm based on the concept of **objects**, which bundle together **data** (attributes) and **behavior** (methods) into cohesive units. OOP follows a **bottom-up** approach, where real-world entities are modeled as objects that interact with each other.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Kitchen Robot":**
Now imagine a smart kitchen robot. It comes pre-packaged with its own ingredients (data) inside sealed compartments and its own cooking instructions (methods) that *only it knows how to execute*. You just press "Make Curry" — the robot handles everything internally. You don't know (or care) about the exact spices, the order of heating, or the algorithm inside. This is **OOP** — *data and behavior are tightly bound together inside an object, and the outside world interacts with it through a clean, controlled interface.*

---

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of OOP

### 🔷 Pillar 1: Data Abstraction

> **Formal Definition:** Data Abstraction is the process of exposing only the **essential features** of an object while **hiding the complex implementation details**. It answers the question: *"What does an object do?"* — not *"How does it do it?"*

**Conceptual Analogy — "Driving a Car":**
When you drive a car, you interact with the steering wheel, accelerator, and brake (the **abstract interface**). You do not need to understand how the internal combustion engine, the fuel injection system, or the braking hydraulics work. The car manufacturer has **abstracted away** the internal complexity and exposed only the essential controls. That is **Data Abstraction**.

In Java/C++, Data Abstraction is achieved primarily through **Abstract Classes** and **Interfaces**.

### 🔷 Pillar 2: Encapsulation

> **Formal Definition:** Encapsulation is the mechanism of **binding data (attributes) and the methods (functions) that operate on that data into a single unit** (a class), and **restricting direct access** to some of the object's components. It is the *implementation* that enforces data hiding.

**Conceptual Analogy — "A Capsule Medicine":**
A medical capsule binds multiple ingredients (data + behavior) inside a sealed shell. You cannot directly access the inner powder — you swallow the whole capsule, and it works as a unit. The sealed shell represents the **access modifiers** (`private`, `protected`, `public`) that control visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Confusion Clarification:**
> - **Abstraction** = Hiding complexity (design-level concept — *what to show*).
> - **Encapsulation** = Hiding data (implementation-level concept — *how to protect*).
> - **Abstraction is a *plan*, Encapsulation is the *execution* of that plan.**

### 🔷 Pillar 3: Inheritance

> **Formal Definition:** Inheritance is the OOP mechanism by which a new class (called the **child/subclass/derived class**) is created from an existing class (called the **parent/superclass/base class**), thereby acquiring its attributes and methods, and being able to extend or override them.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Genetic Inheritance in Families":**
A child inherits physical traits (eye color, height) and behavioral tendencies from parents. The child can also have its own unique features (e.g., a different hairstyle). In OOP, a `Car` class can inherit from a `Vehicle` class — gaining `speed` and `fuel` attributes, and overriding the `start()` method to behave specifically for a car.

### 🔷 Pillar 4: Polymorphism

> **Formal Definition:** Polymorphism (from Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a single interface (method name) to represent different underlying forms (implementations). It allows **one name, many behaviors**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "A Person's Role":**
The same person behaves differently in different contexts — as an *employee* in office, a *parent* at home, a *customer* in a shop, a *player* on the field. The person is one, but the **behavior changes based on context**. In OOP, a method `draw()` can produce different output depending on whether the object is a `Circle`, `Square`, or `Triangle`.

There are two main types of Polymorphism in OOP:
1. **Compile-Time Polymorphism** (Static Binding) — achieved via *Method Overloading* and *Operator Overloading*.
2. **Run-Time Polymorphism** (Dynamic Binding) — achieved via *Method Overriding*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Definition Box:**
> **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm that uses **objects** — instances of **classes** — to design and build applications. OOP rests on four foundational principles: **Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism**, which together promote **modularity, reusability, extensibility, and maintainability** of software systems.

---

## 1.3 Advantages of OOP over Procedural Programming

| Aspect | Procedural | OOP |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Security** | Data is global and exposed | Data is hidden via encapsulation |
| **Reusability** | Limited (function libraries) | High (inheritance & polymorphism) |
| **Modeling** | Function-centric | Real-world entity-centric |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult in large codebases | Easier due to modular classes |
| **Extensibility** | Requires code rewrite | New classes can be added with minimal change |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Procedural vs Object-Oriented Programming Paradigm — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Procedural Programming — Operational Breakdown

1. **Problem is decomposed into functions** (top-down approach).
2. Each function performs a specific task and may call other functions.
3. **Data is typically global or passed as arguments** — it is not tightly bound to any function.
4. **Execution flow** is determined by function call order and control structures (`if`, `while`, `for`).
5. **Examples:** C, Pascal, FORTRAN, early BASIC.

**Limitation:** As programs grow, global data becomes a source of bugs, since any function can modify it. This is the classic **"data is unprotected"** problem.

### 2.1.2 Object-Oriented Programming — Operational Breakdown

1. **Problem is modeled as a set of interacting objects** (bottom-up approach).
2. Each object belongs to a **class** (a blueprint) that defines its attributes and methods.
3. **Data and methods are bundled together** inside the object.
4. **Access to internal data is restricted** through access modifiers.
5. **Objects communicate** by calling each other's public methods (message passing).
6. **Examples:** Java, C++, Python, C#, Ruby.

---

## 2.2 The Four Pillars — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Data Abstraction — "What vs How"

| Layer | Role |
|---|---|
| **Abstract Layer (Interface)** | Declares *what* operations are available |
| **Implementation Layer (Class)** | Defines *how* the operations are actually performed |

**Mechanism in Java:**

```java
// Abstract class - partial abstraction
abstract class Shape {
    abstract double area();   // 'what' - declared but not defined
    void display() {         // concrete method
        System.out.println("I am a shape");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }
    
    @Override
    double area() {            // 'how' - defined here
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}
```

The user of `Shape` knows that `area()` exists but does not know *how* the area is calculated for a circle. That detail is **abstracted away**.

### 2.2.2 Encapsulation — "Binding + Hiding"

**The Two-Step Rule of Encapsulation:**

1. **Bind** data and methods into a single unit (class).
2. **Hide** the internal state by declaring data members as `private`.
3. **Expose** controlled access through `public` getter and setter methods.

**Why it matters in engineering:** Encapsulation enables **information hiding**, which is essential in team-based software development. A team working on the `Engine` class does not need to know the internals of the `Transmission` class — they only interact through the public interface.

### 2.2.3 Inheritance — "IS-A Relationship"

Inheritance establishes a **parent-child hierarchy**. The keyword is **"IS-A"**:
- A `Car` **is-a** `Vehicle`.
- A `Dog` **is-a** `Animal`.
- A `SavingAccount` **is-a** `Account`.

**Types of Inheritance in Java:**

| Type | Structure | Java Support |
|---|---|---|
| Single | A → B | ✅ Yes |
| Multilevel | A → B → C | ✅ Yes |
| Hierarchical | A → B, A → C | ✅ Yes |
| Multiple | A, B → C | ❌ No (use interfaces) |
| Hybrid | Combination | ❌ No (use interfaces) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Java does **not** support *Multiple Inheritance* (a class inheriting from two parent classes) to avoid the **Diamond Problem**. This is achieved using **interfaces**.

### 2.2.4 Polymorphism — "One Interface, Many Implementations"

**Type 1: Compile-Time (Static) Polymorphism**

*Method Overloading* — multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** in the same class.

```java
class Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
}
```

The compiler decides **at compile time** which method to invoke based on the argument types. This is called **Static Binding** or **Early Binding**.

**Type 2: Run-Time (Dynamic) Polymorphism**

*Method Overriding* — a subclass provides a **specific implementation** of a method already defined in its parent class.

```java
class Animal {
    void sound() { System.out.println("Animal makes sound"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Dog barks"); }
}
class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Cat meows"); }
}

// Runtime decision
Animal obj = new Dog();
obj.sound();   // Output: Dog barks
```

The JVM decides **at runtime** which `sound()` to call based on the actual object type. This is called **Dynamic Binding** or **Late Binding**, and it is enabled by **Upcasting** and **Method Dispatch via the Virtual Method Table (vtable)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition | Mechanism | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Abstraction** | Hiding complexity, showing essentials | Abstract classes, Interfaces | Car dashboard (only controls visible) |
| **Encapsulation** | Binding data + methods, restricting access | `private` fields + `public` getters/setters | Capsule medicine (sealed shell) |
| **Inheritance** | Acquiring properties from parent class | `extends` keyword (Java) | Parent-child genetic traits |
| **Polymorphism (Compile-Time)** | Same name, different signatures | Method Overloading | One person playing multiple roles |
| **Polymorphism (Run-Time)** | Same name, different behavior | Method Overriding | One interface (remote), many devices |
| **Procedural Paradigm** | Function-based, top-down | Functions + global data | Old kitchen recipe |
| **OOP Paradigm** | Object-based, bottom-up | Classes + Objects | Smart kitchen robot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** OOP is the backbone of modern software engineering. **Encapsulation** enables secure banking systems, **Inheritance** powers GUI frameworks (Swing, JavaFX), **Polymorphism** is the foundation of plugin architectures, and **Abstraction** is what makes APIs (like JDBC, REST) usable without knowing their internals.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Procedural vs OOP

| Parameter | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Unit** | Function | Object (instance of class) |
| **Approach** | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
| **Data Handling** | Data is separate from functions; usually global | Data is encapsulated inside objects |
| **Access Control** | No formal access modifiers | `public`, `private`, `protected` |
| **Reusability** | Via function libraries | Via inheritance and composition |
| **Extensibility** | Difficult; often requires rewriting | Easy; add new classes with minimal changes |
| **Modeling Power** | Poor for real-world entities | Excellent; maps directly to real-world entities |
| **Security** | Low (global data is exposed) | High (data hiding via encapsulation) |
| **Code Organization** | Module-based | Class-based |
| **Examples** | C, Pascal, COBOL | Java, C++, Python, C# |
| **Polymorphism Support** | Not natively supported | Fully supported (overloading + overriding) |
| **Best Suited For** | Small, mathematical, system-level programs | Large-scale, real-world, GUI, enterprise apps |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Demonstrating the Four Pillars

Let us build a single coherent example that demonstrates **all four pillars** working together — a scenario that KTU frequently tests.

### Step 1: Define an Abstract Class (Demonstrates **Abstraction**)

```java
abstract class BankAccount {
    String accountHolder;
    double balance;
    
    BankAccount(String name, double initialBalance) {
        this.accountHolder = name;
        this.balance = initialBalance;
    }
    
    // Abstract method - 'what' (abstraction)
    abstract double calculateInterest();
    
    // Concrete method
    void displayBalance() {
        System.out.println("Balance: " + balance);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The `BankAccount` class declares `calculateInterest()` as abstract. It defines the *contract* (what must be done) but does not specify *how*. This is **Data Abstraction**.

---

### Step 2: Apply Encapsulation to Hide Data

```java
class SecureAccount {
    private double balance;  // HIDDEN from outside world
    
    public double getBalance() { return balance; }   // controlled read
    public void deposit(double amount) {             // controlled write
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The `balance` field is marked `private`. The outside world cannot directly modify it — they must use `deposit()`. This is **Encapsulation**.

---

### Step 3: Inherit from a Parent Class

```java
class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    double interestRate;
    
    SavingsAccount(String name, double balance, double rate) {
        super(name, balance);
        this.interestRate = rate;
    }
    
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {  // specific implementation
        return balance * interestRate / 100;
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** `SavingsAccount` extends `BankAccount`, inheriting `accountHolder`, `balance`, and `displayBalance()`. This is **Inheritance**.

---

### Step 4: Demonstrate Polymorphism (Method Overriding)

```java
class CurrentAccount extends BankAccount {
    CurrentAccount(String name, double balance) {
        super(name, balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {  // DIFFERENT implementation
        return balance * 0.02;   // 2% for current accounts
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** Both `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` override `calculateInterest()` with **different logic**. When called via a `BankAccount` reference, the JVM dispatches to the correct version at runtime.

---

### Step 5: Polymorphism in Action (Dynamic Dispatch)

```java
public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc1 = new SavingsAccount("Alice", 10000, 5);
        BankAccount acc2 = new CurrentAccount("Bob", 20000);
        
        System.out.println("Alice's interest: " + acc1.calculateInterest());
        System.out.println("Bob's interest: "   + acc2.calculateInterest());
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**

```
Alice's interest: 500.0
Bob's interest:   400.0
```

**Analysis:**
- The variable type is `BankAccount` (parent reference).
- The actual object is `SavingsAccount` (child object) — this is **Upcasting**.
- At runtime, the JVM checks the actual object type and calls the appropriate `calculateInterest()` — this is **Dynamic Method Dispatch**.
- This is **Run-Time Polymorphism** in action.

---

## 3.3 Conceptual Equations of OOP (Logical Form)

While OOP does not have physical formulas, the following **logical relationships** are essential for KTU answers:

$$\text{Class} = \text{Data Members (State)} + \text{Member Functions (Behavior)} + \text{Access Control}$$

$$\text{Object} = \text{Instance of Class with unique identity and state}$$

$$\text{Inheritance}: \text{Child} \supseteq \text{Parent's Members} + \text{New Members}$$

$$\text{Polymorphism}: f_{\text{interface}}(\text{object}) \rightarrow f_{\text{implementation}}(\text{object})$$

$$\text{Encapsulation} = \text{Data Hiding} + \text{Controlled Access Interface}$$

$$\text{Abstraction} = \text{Essential Features (visible)} + \text{Implementation Details (hidden)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These logical relationships form the foundation of **Unified Modeling Language (UML) Class Diagrams** used in software design. KTU 2024 scheme expects familiarity with mapping these concepts to UML notation.

---

## 3.4 KTU-Focused Comparison: Overloading vs Overriding

| Parameter | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymorphism Type** | Compile-Time (Static) | Run-Time (Dynamic) |
| **Where it occurs** | Same class | Two classes (parent-child) |
| **Method Signature** | Must be **different** | Must be **same** |
| **Return Type** | Can be different | Must be same (or covariant) |
| **Binding** | Early Binding (by compiler) | Late Binding (by JVM) |
| **Keyword** | Not required | `@Override` annotation recommended |
| **Inheritance Required?** | No | Yes |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Question]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming paradigms.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Unit** | Function | Object |
| **Approach** | Top-down | Bottom-up |
| **Data Security** | Data is global and unprotected | Data is encapsulated and hidden |
| **Reusability** | Limited (function libraries) | High (inheritance) |
| **Real-World Modeling** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Examples** | C, Pascal | Java, C++ |

Procedural programming is function-centric and treats data and functions as separate entities, whereas OOP binds data and behavior together inside objects, providing security, reusability, and better real-world modeling. *(Examiner expects at least 4 distinct points.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question]**

### **Q2. Explain any two of the four pillars of OOP with examples.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Encapsulation:** It is the wrapping of data and methods into a single unit (class) and restricting direct access using access modifiers. *Example:* Declaring a `balance` field as `private` in a `BankAccount` class and providing `public` `getBalance()` and `deposit()` methods for controlled access.

**(ii) Polymorphism:** It allows the same interface to represent different behaviors. *Example:* A `draw()` method behaves differently when called on a `Circle` object versus a `Square` object — this is method overriding, a form of run-time polymorphism.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the four fundamental concepts of Object-Oriented Programming with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate polymorphism using a shape hierarchy consisting of an abstract class `Shape` with subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle`. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The four fundamental concepts (pillars) of OOP are:

**1. Data Abstraction:**
Abstraction is the process of hiding implementation details and exposing only the essential features of an object. In Java, it is achieved through **abstract classes** and **interfaces**. *Example:* A `Shape` class declares an abstract `area()` method, but the actual calculation is hidden inside the `Circle` subclass.

**2. Encapsulation:**
Encapsulation binds data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit and restricts external access. It is implemented using `private` data members and `public` getter/setter methods. *Example:* A `Student` class hides its `marks` field as `private` and provides controlled access through `getMarks()` and `setMarks(int m)` methods.

**3. Inheritance:**
Inheritance allows a new class (child) to acquire the properties and methods of an existing class (parent), promoting code reuse. *Example:* `class Car extends Vehicle` — `Car` inherits `speed` and `fuel` from `Vehicle` and can add its own features like `ACCapacity`.

**4. Polymorphism:**
Polymorphism enables one interface to be used for different underlying forms. *Example:* A method `calculateInterest()` behaves differently in `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` classes that both extend `BankAccount`. The JVM decides at runtime which version to invoke.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b):

```java
// Abstract class demonstrating Abstraction
abstract class Shape {
    String name;
    Shape(String n) { this.name = n; }
    abstract double area();          // abstract method
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Shape: " + name + ", Area: " + area());
    }
}

// Subclass demonstrating Inheritance
class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(double r) { super("Circle"); this.radius = r; }
    
    @Override
    double area() {                    // method overriding - polymorphism
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

// Subclass demonstrating Inheritance
class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length, width;
    Rectangle(double l, double w) { 
        super("Rectangle"); 
        this.length = l; this.width = w; 
    }
    
    @Override
    double area() {                    // method overriding - polymorphism
        return length * width;
    }
}

// Driver class demonstrating Run-Time Polymorphism
public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s1 = new Circle(5.0);            // upcasting
        Shape s2 = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);   // upcasting
        
        s1.display();   // dynamic dispatch
        s2.display();   // dynamic dispatch
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Shape: Circle, Area: 78.53981633974483
Shape: Rectangle, Area: 24.0
```

**Explanation of Concepts Demonstrated:**
- `Shape` is **abstract** → demonstrates **Abstraction**.
- `Circle` and `Rectangle` **extend** `Shape` → demonstrates **Inheritance**.
- Both override `area()` differently → demonstrates **Polymorphism (Run-Time)**.
- Data members like `radius`, `length` would typically be `private` with getters/setters → demonstrates **Encapsulation**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast Procedural Programming with Object-Oriented Programming. Highlight at least 6 differences. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain method overloading and method overriding with Java code examples. State how they differ. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a):

| S.No. | Parameter | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Unit** | Function | Object |
| 2 | **Approach** | Top-down | Bottom-up |
| 3 | **Data Handling** | Data is separate, often global | Data is encapsulated within objects |
| 4 | **Access Control** | No formal modifiers | `public`, `private`, `protected` |
| 5 | **Reusability** | Via function libraries only | Via inheritance and polymorphism |
| 6 | **Security** | Low (global data exposed) | High (data hiding via encapsulation) |
| 7 | **Polymorphism** | Not natively supported | Fully supported (overloading + overriding) |
| 8 | **Real-World Modeling** | Difficult | Natural and intuitive |
| 9 | **Code Organization** | Module/procedure-based | Class-based |
| 10 | **Examples** | C, Pascal | Java, C++, Python |

**Conclusion:** OOP provides better modularity, security, reusability, and real-world modeling compared to procedural programming, making it the preferred choice for large-scale software development.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism):**
Multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** in the same class.

```java
class MathOps {
    int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
}
```

**Method Overriding (Run-Time Polymorphism):**
A subclass provides a **new implementation** for a method already defined in its parent class.

```java
class Vehicle {
    void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle starts"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void start() { System.out.println("Car starts with a key"); }
}
```

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Overloading | Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Polymorphism Type | Compile-Time | Run-Time |
| Occurs In | Same class | Parent-Child classes |
| Parameters | Must differ | Must be same |
| Binding | Static (early) | Dynamic (late) |
| Inheritance Needed? | No | Yes |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Abstraction with Encapsulation** — Examiners explicitly test this. Always state: *Abstraction hides complexity at the design level; Encapsulation hides data at the implementation level.*
> 2. **Writing Java code without explaining the OOP concept being demonstrated** — KTU expects **concept + code** together. A code snippet without explanation loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the `extends` keyword** when explaining inheritance in Java.
> 4. **Using the word "overloading" and "overriding" interchangeably** — they are fundamentally different. Overloading = same name, different parameters (compile-time). Overriding = same signature, different implementation (run-time).
> 5. **Not writing the output of polymorphism programs** — Always trace and show the output, including which version (parent/child) is invoked.
> 6. **Omitting the `super()` call** in inheritance constructors — Java implicitly calls `super()` as the first statement.
> 7. **Confusing IS-A vs HAS-A** — Inheritance is **IS-A**; Composition is **HAS-A**. KTU frequently tests this distinction.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Programming Paradigm** = A fundamental style of programming (Procedural vs OOP).
- ✅ **Procedural** = Function-based, top-down, data is separate. (Examples: C, Pascal)
- ✅ **OOP** = Object-based, bottom-up, data + methods bundled. (Examples: Java, C++)
- ✅ **Four Pillars of OOP** = **Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism**.
- ✅ **Abstraction** = Hiding complexity, showing essentials → Abstract classes & Interfaces.
- ✅ **Encapsulation** = Binding data + methods + restricting access → `private` + getters/setters.
- ✅ **Inheritance** = Child acquires parent's properties → `extends` keyword, supports **IS-A** relationship.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** = One name, many forms.
  - *Compile-Time:* Method Overloading (same name, different parameters).
  - *Run-Time:* Method Overriding (parent-child, same signature, different body).
- ✅ **Dynamic Method Dispatch** = JVM decides at runtime which overridden method to invoke.
- ✅ **Upcasting** = Parent reference = new Child() — enables run-time polymorphism.
- ✅ **Java does NOT support Multiple Inheritance** for classes (Diamond Problem) — uses **interfaces**.
- ✅ **Access Modifiers in Java:** `public` (anywhere), `protected` (subclass + same package), `default` (same package), `private` (same class only).
- ✅ **Abstraction vs Encapsulation:** Abstraction = design (WHAT); Encapsulation = implementation (HOW).
- ✅ **IS-A** = Inheritance; **HAS-A** = Composition.
- ✅ **Class** = Blueprint; **Object** = Instance of a class.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OOP Concepts: Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming Paradigm**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Microservices # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microservices** (also called the **Microservice Architecture**) is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, autonomous, loosely coupled services, where each service is: > - **Self-contained** (owns its own data and business logic) > - **Independently deployable** (can be released without coordinating with other services) > - **Organized around a single business capability** (bounded context) > - **Communicable via lightweight mechanisms** (typically HTTP/REST APIs, message queues, or gRPC) > - **Technology-agnostic** (polyglot — different services can use different programming languages, databases, and frameworks) The term was popularized by **Martin Fowler and James Lewis in 2014**, who described it as an approach to developing a single application as a suite of small services, each running in its own process and communicating with lightweight mechanisms. ### Mathematical / Structural Formalism Let a distributed application $\mathcal{A}$ be defined as: $$\mathcal{A} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\}$$ where each $S_i$ is an independent microservice such that: $$S_i = \langle L_i, D_i, C_i, B_i, T_i \rangle$$ | Component | Symbol | Meaning | |-----------|--------|---------| | Language Stack | $L_i$ | Programming language/framework used | | Database | $D_i$ | Private data store owned by $S_i$ | | API Contract | $C_i$ | Interface exposed to other services | | Business Capability | $B_i$ | The single domain function $S_i$ fulfills | | Technology Profile | $T_i$ | Runtime, OS, and tooling profile | The **inter-service communication graph** is: $$G = (\mathcal{A}, E) \quad \text{where} \quad E = \{(S_i, S_j) \mid S_i \text{ invokes } S_j\}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OECST615, Module 1):** Microservices is introduced as the modern evolution of Object-Oriented design principles — particularly **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** and **encapsulation** — applied at the *architectural* (system) level rather than the *class* level. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) > [!TIP] > **Think of Microservices like a modern shopping mall:** > > 🏬 **Monolith** = A single, giant shop that sells *everything* — groceries, clothes, electronics, pharmacy, food court — all under one roof with one billing counter. If the billing system crashes, the *entire* shop stops. If you want to renovate the clothing section, you must shut down the whole building. > > 🧩 **Microservices** = A shopping mall made of **independent kiosks** — a *Sephora* store, a *Croma* electronics outlet, a *Domino's* food kiosk, a *pharmacy*. Each kiosk: > - Has its own entrance, staff, cash register, and inventory > - Can open, close, or renovate **without affecting** other kiosks > - Talks to other kiosks only through the **mall's central pathways (network)** > - Is **owned and operated by different teams** (different technology stacks allowed) **In software terms:** Just as you can update the Croma electronics kiosk's billing system without touching Domino's pizza ordering system, in a microservice architecture you can update the *Payment Service* without redeploying the *User Profile Service*. ## 1.3 The Problem Microservices Solves As applications grew in scale (think Amazon, Netflix, Uber), the traditional **monolithic architecture** created several pain points: | Pain Point | Monolithic Reality | Microservice Solution | |------------|-------------------|----------------------| | Deployment | One change → redeploy the *entire* application | Deploy only the affected service | | Scalability | Scale the *whole* app even for one hot module | Scale only the bottleneck service | | Technology | Locked into one language/framework | Each service picks the best tool | | Team velocity | Multiple teams stepping on each other | Each team owns one or more services | | Fault isolation | One bug crashes everything | A service failure is contained | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Microservice Maturity Model" (Lewis & Fowler, 2014) > [!IMPORTANT] > Martin Fowler defined **9 common characteristics** of microservices. KTU examiners frequently test at least 3–4 of these. Memorize them. 1. **Componentization via Services** — Replaceable, independent units 2. **Organized around Business Capabilities** — Not technical layers (no "UI team" or "DB team") 3. **Products not Projects** — "You build it, you run it" (DevOps ownership) 4. **Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes** — Business logic lives *in* services, not in ESBs/message buses 5. **Decentralized Governance** — Each team picks its own tech stack 6. **Decentralized Data Management** — Each service owns its database 7. **Infrastructure Automation** — CI/CD pipelines, automated testing 8. **Smart Design for Failure** — Services *expect* others to fail (circuit breakers, retries) 9. **Evolutionary Design** — Replace services as understanding grows ## 2.2 Core Principles (Granular Breakdown) ### A. Single Responsibility at the Service Level > **"Do one thing and do it well."** (Unix philosophy) > Each microservice corresponds to **one bounded context** in **Domain-Driven Design (DDD)** terminology by Eric Evans. ### B. Independent Deployability A service is truly independent only if it satisfies: $$\text{Independence} = f(\text{Code Independence}, \text{Data Independence}, \text{Deploy Independence})$$ ### C. Decentralized Data (Polyglot Persistence) > [!WARNING] > **Anti-pattern to avoid: Shared Database.** If two services share a single database table, they are *not* truly decoupled — a schema change in one breaks the other. ### D. Failure Isolation The probability that the *entire system* is up is: $$P_{\text{system up}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(S_i \text{ is up})$$ With **redundancy** (running $k$ replicas of $S_i$): $$P(S_i \text{ up}) = 1 - (1 - p_i)^k$$ $$P_{\text{system up}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - (1 - p_i)^{k_i}\right]$$ where: - $p_i$ = probability that one replica of $S_i$ is up - $k_i$ = number of replicas of $S_i$ - $n$ = total number of services ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Symbol / Definition | Engineering Use | |---|---------|---------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Number of services | $n$ | Granularity metric | | 2 | System availability | $A = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Reliability planning | | 3 | Redundancy gain | $1-(1-p_i)^{k_i}$ | High-availability design | | 4 | Coupling metric | $C = \frac{\text{Inter-service calls}}{\text{Total method calls}}$ | Lower is better | | 5 | Cohesion metric | $Cohesion = \frac{\text{Related functions within } S_i}{\text{Total functions in } S_i}$ | Higher is better | | 6 | Conway's Law | $\text{Org structure} \rightarrow \text{System design}$ | Team topology | | 7 | Fallback (Circuit Breaker) | Open / Half-Open / Closed states | Fault tolerance | | 8 | Service granularity | $\text{Right} = f(\text{Business capability})$ | Decomposition rule | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Netflix** — Pioneered microservices; 700+ services; uses Hystrix for circuit breaking - **Amazon** — Migrated from monolith to "SOA 2.0" (microservices) in early 2000s - **Uber** — Domain-oriented microservices architecture (DOMA) - **Spotify** — Squad model, each squad owns a microservice - **Kubernetes** — De facto container orchestrator for deploying microservices - **Istio / Linkerd** — Service mesh for traffic management, security, observability --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Architecture Walkthrough ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Microservices Outperform Monoliths at Scale ### Scenario Setup Consider a monolithic e-commerce application deployed on **one server** with: - Catalog module (40% CPU usage) - Checkout module (30% CPU usage) - Reviews module (5% CPU usage) - Reporting module (25% CPU usage) **Total CPU load** = 100% → server is saturated. ### Step 1: Monolithic Scaling Cost To handle **2× traffic**, we must scale the *entire* monolith: $$N_{\text{monolith servers}} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cost} \propto 2 \times 1 = 2$$ ### Step 2: Microservices Scaling Cost We split into 4 services. Traffic pattern shows only **Catalog** is hot (80% of new traffic): | Service | CPU before | CPU after 2× traffic | Replicas needed | |---------|-----------|---------------------|-----------------| | Catalog | 40% | 80% | 2 | | Checkout | 30% | 60% | 2 (safety) | | Reviews | 5% | 10% | 1 | | Reporting | 25% | 50% | 1 | $$N_{\text{microservice replicas}} = 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 6 \quad \text{across } 4 \text{ hosts}$$ ### Step 3: Cost-Benefit Analysis But each microservice is **small** and runs on **smaller VMs/containers**: $$C_{\text{micro, total}} = 2c_{\text{small}} + 2c_{\text{small}} + 1c_{\text{small}} + 1c_{\text{small}} = 6c_{\text{small}}$$ $$C_{\text{monolith, total}} = 2c_{\text{large}}$$ Since $c_{\text{small}} \ll c_{\text{large}}$ (typically $c_{\text{small}} \approx 0.25 \cdot c_{\text{large}}$): $$6 \times 0.25 \cdot c_{\text{large}} = 1.5 \cdot c_{\text{large}} \quad \text{vs} \quad 2 \cdot c_{\text{large}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Microservices cost } \approx 25\% \text{ less at this scale}}$$ ### Step 4: Granularity Trade-Off (The "Nano-service" Anti-pattern) > [!WARNING] > **Excessive decomposition is harmful.** There is an optimal granularity. Let $n$ = number of services. The **total system complexity** $T(n)$ is: $$T(n) = \underbrace{n \cdot C_{\text{intra}}}_{\text{Internal complexity}} + \underbrace{\binom{n}{2} \cdot C_{\text{inter}}}_{\text{Inter-service network complexity}}$$ where $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ counts inter-service pairs. $$\therefore T(n) = n \cdot C_{\text{intra}} + \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \cdot C_{\text{inter}}$$ **Differentiating and setting to zero** to find optimum $n^*$: $$\frac{dT}{dn} = C_{\text{intra}} + \frac{(2n - 1)}{2} \cdot C_{\text{inter}} = 0$$ $$n^* = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{C_{\text{intra}}}{C_{\text{inter}}}$$ Since $C_{\text{intra}}, C_{\text{inter}} > 0$ and $n^* > 0$ in practice, this yields a **finite optimum** — typically 5–20 services per application domain. Beyond this, you enter **"Distributed Monolith"** territory. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Monolith vs Microservices vs SOA | Dimension | Monolithic | SOA (Service-Oriented) | Microservices | |-----------|-----------|------------------------|---------------| | Service size | N/A (one unit) | Large, enterprise-wide | Small, single-purpose | | Coupling | Tight (in-process) | Loose (ESB-mediated) | Loose (HTTP/message) | | Data ownership | Shared DB | Often shared | Per-service DB | | Deployment | Whole app | Whole app | Per-service | | Tech stack | Single | Mostly single | Polyglot | | Governance | Centralized | Centralized (ESB) | Decentralized | | Failure impact | Total | Partial | Contained | | Communication | Function calls | SOAP/XML, ESB | REST/gRPC, message bus | | Best for | Small teams, MVPs | Enterprise integration | Cloud-native, scalable systems | ## 3.3 Inter-Service Communication Patterns ### A. Synchronous (Request-Response) | Protocol | Format | Strength | Weakness | |----------|--------|----------|----------| | **REST / HTTP** | JSON, XML | Simple, ubiquitous, human-readable | Latency, no built-in streaming | | **gRPC** | Protocol Buffers (binary) | Fast, strongly typed, streaming | Steeper learning curve | | **GraphQL** | Query language | Client-driven fetching | Single point of failure | ### B. Asynchronous (Event-Driven) | Pattern | Description | Example Tool | |---------|-------------|--------------| | **Pub/Sub** | Publishers emit, subscribers receive | Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SNS | | **Event Sourcing** | State = replay of events | Kafka + EventStore | | **Message Queue** | Point-to-point delivery | RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Tip:** For a 14-mark question, mention **at least 2 patterns** (one sync + one async) and explain *when* to use each. ## 3.4 Key Design Patterns in Microservices 1. **API Gateway Pattern** — Single entry point that routes to internal services 2. **Service Discovery** — Services register and find each other (Eureka, Consul) 3. **Circuit Breaker** — Stop calling a failing service (Hystrix, Resilience4j) 4. **Saga Pattern** — Distributed transactions via compensating actions 5. **CQRS** — Separate read and write models 6. **Database per Service** — Enforces loose coupling 7. **Bulkhead** — Isolate resources to prevent cascading failures 8. **Strangler Fig** — Gradually replace a monolith with microservices ## 3.5 Comparison: Pros vs Cons (Examiner's Favourite) ### ✅ Advantages - **Independent deployment** → faster release cycles - **Technology heterogeneity** → polyglot freedom - **Scalability** → horizontal scaling per service - **Fault isolation** → one failure doesn't kill the system - **Team autonomy** → Conway's Law alignment - **Reusability** → services can serve multiple front-ends (web, mobile, IoT) ### ❌ Disadvantages - **Distributed system complexity** — network failures, latency - **Data consistency** — no ACID across services (need eventual consistency) - **Network overhead** — serialization, HTTP/gRPC costs - **Testing difficulty** — integration tests are harder - **Operational overhead** — need CI/CD, monitoring, container orchestration - **Security** — more attack surface (inter-service auth, TLS everywhere) --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Monolithic vs Microservice Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph MONO["MONOLITHIC ARCHITECTURE"] user1[Client] monoApp[Single Application] ui1[UI Module] biz1[Business Logic] da1[Data Access] db1[(Shared Database)] user1 --> monoApp monoApp --> ui1 monoApp --> biz1 monoApp --> da1 da1 --> db1 end subgraph MICRO["MICROSERVICE ARCHITECTURE"] user2[Client] gw[API Gateway] s1[Catalog Service] s2[Order Service] s3[Payment Service] s4[User Service] d1[(Catalog DB)] d2[(Order DB)] d3[(Payment DB)] d4[(User DB)] user2 --> gw gw --> s1 gw --> s2 gw --> s3 gw --> s4 s1 --> d1 s2 --> d2 s3 --> d3 s4 --> d4 s2 -.HTTP.-> s3 s2 -.HTTP.-> s4 end ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — API Gateway Pattern (Detailed) ```mermaid graph LR C1[Web Client] C2[Mobile App] C3[IoT Device] GW[API Gateway
Auth, Rate Limit, Routing] SVC1[Product Service] SVC2[Inventory Service] SVC3[Cart Service] SVC4[Checkout Service] SVC5[Notification Service] MQ[(Message Queue
Kafka)] DB1[(Product DB)] DB2[(Inventory DB)] DB3[(Cart DB)] DB4[(Order DB)] C1 --> GW C2 --> GW C3 --> GW GW --> SVC1 GW --> SVC2 GW --> SVC3 GW --> SVC4 SVC4 -. async event .-> MQ MQ --> SVC5 SVC1 --> DB1 SVC2 --> DB2 SVC3 --> DB3 SVC4 --> DB4 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Circuit Breaker State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Closed Closed --> Open : Failure threshold exceeded Open --> HalfOpen : Timeout elapsed HalfOpen --> Closed : Trial call succeeds HalfOpen --> Open : Trial call fails note right of Closed : All calls pass through normally note right of Open : Calls fail fast, return fallback note right of HalfOpen : Limited trial calls allowed ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Saga Pattern (Choreography-based) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant OrderSvc as Order Service participant PaymentSvc as Payment Service participant InventorySvc as Inventory Service participant MQ as Event Bus Client->>OrderSvc: Place Order OrderSvc->>MQ: OrderCreated Event MQ->>PaymentSvc: Consume OrderCreated PaymentSvc->>MQ: PaymentProcessed Event MQ->>InventorySvc: Consume PaymentProcessed InventorySvc->>MQ: InventoryReserved Event MQ->>OrderSvc: Consume InventoryReserved OrderSvc->>Client: Order Confirmed Note over PaymentSvc,MQ: If payment fails, PaymentSvc emits PaymentFailed Note over OrderSvc,MQ: OrderSvc consumes PaymentFailed and triggers compensating CancelOrder event ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Microservices. List any four key characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Microservices is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, loosely coupled, independently deployable services, each organized around a single business capability and communicating via lightweight protocols such as HTTP/REST or messaging queues. **Four Key Characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Single Responsibility** — Each service focuses on one bounded business capability. 2. **Independent Deployability** — Each service can be deployed without affecting others. 3. **Decentralized Data** — Each service owns its private database (no shared schema). 4. **Polyglot Technology** — Each service can use a different language, framework, or database best suited to its task. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between Monolithic and Microservice architecture.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Monolithic Architecture | Microservice Architecture | |---|------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Single, unified codebase | Multiple small, independent codebases | | 2 | Tightly coupled modules | Loosely coupled services | | 3 | Shared database | Database per service | | 4 | Whole application redeployed for any change | Independent per-service deployment | | 5 | Single technology stack | Polyglot (heterogeneous tech) | | 6 | Failure of one module may crash entire app | Fault isolation between services | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **Q3 (a)** Explain the Microservice architecture in detail. Discuss its key principles and characteristics. **(7 marks)** > > **Q3 (b)** Compare Microservices with Monolithic architecture. List the advantages and challenges of Microservices. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 Marks **1. Introduction (1 mark):** Microservice architecture, coined formally by Martin Fowler and James Lewis in 2014, is a method of developing software systems as a suite of small, autonomous, self-contained services, each running in its own process and communicating via lightweight mechanisms like HTTP/REST. **2.
