```java
class Student {
    String name;            // instance variable
    static String college = "KTU University";  // static variable

    // Instance method
    void setName(String n) {
        name = n;
    }

    // Static method
    static void changeCollege(String c) {
        college = c;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", College: " + college);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student.changeCollege("APJAKTU");      // static call
        Student s1 = new Student();
        s1.setName("Anand");                    // instance call
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Name: Anand, College: APJAKTU
```

> **Marking Scheme:** Comparison Table = 4 marks + Program = 3 marks = **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is method overloading in Java? Explain the rules for overloading with an example. Why is return type not considered for method overloading?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **pass-by-value** in Java with respect to primitive and object types. Write a program to demonstrate that swapping two integers using a method does not affect the original values.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Method Overloading Definition:**

Method overloading is the process of defining **multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists** (different number, types, or order of parameters) within the same class.

**Rules for Method Overloading:**

1. Methods must have the **same name**.
2. Methods must differ in the **parameter list** (number, type, or order).
3. **Return type may be the same or different** — it is NOT considered.
4. **Access modifiers may vary**.

**Example:**

```java
class Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}
```

Here, three methods named `add` are overloaded based on parameter list differences.

**Why Return Type is NOT Considered:**

- The compiler resolves method calls based on **signature** = name + parameters.
- If return type alone were used to differentiate, calls like `obj.add(2,3)` would become **ambiguous** (which method to invoke?).
- Hence, Java forbids overloading by return type alone → **compile-time error**.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 1.5 marks + Rules (4 × 0.5) = 2 marks + Example = 2 marks + Justification = 1.5 marks = **7 marks**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Pass-by-Value in Java:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. For **primitives**, a copy of the value is passed. For **objects**, a copy of the **reference** is passed (not the object itself).

**Key Insight:**

| Type | What is copied? | Effect on original? |
|---|---|---|
| Primitive (`int`, `float`, etc.) | The actual value | ❌ No change |
| Object reference | The reference (address) | Object state can change, but reference cannot be reassigned to new object |

**Demonstration Program (Swapping Integers):**

```java
class SwapDemo {
    static void swap(int x, int y) {
        int temp = x;
        x = y;
        y = temp;
        System.out.println("Inside swap: a = " + x + ", b = " + y);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 10, b = 20;
        swap(a, b);
        System.out.println("After swap: a = " + a + ", b = " + b);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Inside swap: a = 20, b = 10
After swap: a = 10, b = 20
```

**Explanation:**

- The variables `a` and `b` in `main` are passed **by value** as `x` and `y` in `swap`.
- Changes to `x` and `y` inside `swap` **do NOT affect** the original `a` and `b`.
- Hence, swapping inside the method has **no effect on the caller's variables**.

> **Marking Scheme:** Concept of pass-by-value = 2 marks + Primitive vs Object table = 2 marks + Program + Output + Explanation = 3 marks = **7 marks**.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must memorize before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ A **method** is a named block of code inside a class.
- ✅ Method **declaration syntax**: `[modifier] returnType name(params) { body }`
- ✅ **Method signature** = method name + parameter list (NOT return type).
- ✅ Access modifiers: `public`, `private`, `protected`, *default*.
- ✅ Non-access modifiers: `static`, `final`, `abstract`, `synchronized`.
- ✅ `void` return type → no value returned.
- ✅ Non-void methods **must** return a value of the declared type on **every code path**.
- ✅ Java is **always pass-by-value** (primitives: value copy; objects: reference copy).
- ✅ **Static methods** belong to the class → called using `ClassName.method()`.
- ✅ **Instance methods** belong to objects → called using `object.method()`.
- ✅ **Method overloading** = same name, **different parameter list** (return type alone is insufficient).
- ✅ JVM creates a **stack frame** for every method call → destroyed on return.
- ✅ Local variables of a method **die** when the method exits.
- ✅ `this` keyword refers to the **current object** — usable only in instance methods.
- ✅ **Argument evaluation order** in method calls: **left-to-right**.
- ✅ A method declared `static` **cannot** access non-static members directly.

---

> 🏁 **END OF NOTES — INTRODUCTION TO METHODS (Module 1, OECST615, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> A **Constructor** is a special member function (method) of a class that is **automatically invoked** at the time of object creation. Its primary purpose is to **initialize** the data members (instance variables) of a newly created object to valid, meaningful initial values, thereby guaranteeing that no object exists in an uninitialized or undefined state.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Java-centric under OECST615 — *Object Oriented Programming*), constructors belong to the foundational pillar of **Class & Object Lifecycle Management**, directly supporting the Course Outcome:
- **CO1:** *Apply the concepts of object-oriented programming using Java/C++ to develop modular and reusable programs.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hospital Birth Certificate" Model

Imagine a **newborn baby** arriving in a hospital:
- The baby (object) is **born** (instantiated via `new`).
- The very first formal document prepared is the **Birth Certificate** — it records the baby's **name, weight, date, time, parents' names** (initial data).
- The certificate is **mandatory** — you cannot skip it.
- It is prepared **automatically** by the registrar (JVM/Compiler) the moment the baby is born.
- Its name **matches the parents' surname** (class name) — it doesn't have a free choice.
- Once recorded, the certificate **cannot be re-issued** (you cannot "re-initialize" via the same constructor — you create a *new* object).

A **Constructor** behaves exactly like this Birth Certificate — a **mandatory, automatic, same-named initializer** for every object the moment it comes into existence.

## 1.3 Why Constructors Are Required (Without Them, Programs Would Fail)

| Scenario | What Happens Without a Constructor |
|----------|------------------------------------|
| Uninitialized `int age;` | Could hold garbage value (e.g., `8473829`) |
| Object reference fields | Could be `null` → `NullPointerException` at runtime |
| File/DB handles | Would point to non-existent resources |
| Mathematical invariants | E.g., radius of circle = 0 → division-by-zero later |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compiler Safety Net:** If the programmer writes **no constructor**, the Java/C++ compiler **automatically injects** a **Default Constructor** (also called the *no-argument constructor* or *implicit constructor*) so that object creation never breaks. This is a syllabus-favourite viva question.

## 1.4 Key Properties at a Glance

- **Same name as the class** (in Java/C++; differs in C# / Python which use `__init__`).
- **No return type** — not even `void`.
- **Invoked automatically** — you do **not** call it explicitly with a dot operator.
- **Cannot be `static`, `abstract`, or `final`** (Java specifics).
- **Can be overloaded** — multiple constructors with different parameter lists.
- **Cannot be inherited**, but a subclass constructor **always calls** the parent constructor first (super-call chain).
- Per **2024 KTU marking scheme**, a constructor **diagram is mandatory** for full marks in 7+ mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Types of Constructors

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands coverage of the following constructor taxonomy. Each type has a distinct semantic role in object lifecycle.

### Type 1 — Default (Implicit / Synthesized) Constructor

- **Who creates it:** The Java/C++ compiler.
- **When:** Triggered when the programmer defines **zero** constructors in the class.
- **Body:** Empty — only a hidden `super();` call to `java.lang.Object` (in Java) or default memberwise initialization (in C++).
- **Disappears the moment you write even a single constructor.**

### Type 2 — No-Argument (User-Defined) Constructor

- **Who creates it:** The programmer.
- **Signature:** `public ClassName() { ... }`
- **Difference from Default:** The default is *compiler-injected*; this is *programmer-written* but parameter-less. It can contain custom initialization logic.

### Type 3 — Parameterized Constructor

- **Who creates it:** The programmer.
- **Signature:** `public ClassName(Type1 arg1, Type2 arg2, ...) { ... }`
- **Purpose:** Allows **custom initial state** at object creation time.
- **If defined, the Default Constructor vanishes** — the compiler no longer auto-generates one.

### Type 4 — Copy Constructor

- **Who creates it:** The programmer.
- **Signature:** `public ClassName(const ClassName &obj)` (C++) / Java has no built-in but is **simulated** via `this(obj)` chaining or `clone()`.
- **Purpose:** Creates a **new object as a clone** of an existing object.
- **KTU 2024 Note:** Explicitly tested in C++ module questions; in Java, the `Cloneable` interface replaces it.

### Type 5 — Private / Protected Constructor (Sentinel Pattern)

- Used to **prevent instantiation** (e.g., `java.lang.Math`).
- Common in **Singleton Design Pattern** and **Utility Classes**.
- A class with only private constructors **cannot be subclassed** externally.

## 2.2 The "Constructor Rules" — Rigid & Non-Negotiable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 Golden Rules of Constructors (Exam-Favourite)**

1. The constructor name must **exactly match** the class name (case-sensitive).
2. Constructors **must not** declare a return type — not even `void`.
3. Constructors **cannot** be marked `static`, `final`, `abstract`, or `synchronized` (Java).
4. Constructors are **invoked implicitly** by `new` (or `newInstance()` via reflection).
5. Constructors **can be overloaded** but **cannot be overridden** (no polymorphism on constructors).
6. A constructor's **first statement** is either `this(...)` (same-class chaining) or `super(...)` (parent-class chaining) — if neither is written, the compiler injects `super();` automatically.
7. The **first line of every constructor** must be either `this()` or `super()` — **never both**, **never after another statement**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Constructor | Regular Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | Same as class | Any valid identifier |
| **Return Type** | None (not even `void`) | Mandatory (`void` / data-type) |
| **Invocation** | Automatic via `new` | Manual via object reference |
| **Inheritance / Overriding** | Cannot be overridden; can be overloaded | Can be both inherited & overridden |
| **Purpose** | Initialize object state | Define behavior |
| **Compiler Default** | Auto-provided if absent | Never auto-provided |
| **Memory Location** | Loaded into Class metadata (Method Area) | Same as constructors |
| **Keyword** | None — implicit | Can be `static`, `final`, etc. |

## 2.4 Constructor Overloading — Polymorphism at Birth

**Constructor Overloading** = declaring **multiple constructors** in the same class with **different parameter lists** (different number, type, or order of parameters).

This is **Compile-Time Polymorphism** (Static Binding) and is the constructor-equivalent of method overloading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** A `Date` class can be constructed as `new Date()` (today), `new Date(15)` (15th of current month), or `new Date(15, 8, 2024)` (full custom date) — same class, multiple legitimate "birth certificates" depending on the information available at creation time.

## 2.5 Constructor Chaining via `this()`

- Calling one constructor from another constructor in the **same class**.
- **Must be the very first statement** of the calling constructor.
- Used to **avoid code duplication** by centralizing initialization logic in a single "master" constructor.
- Forms a **chain** that **must terminate** at a `super()` call (parent constructor).

## 2.6 Destructor — The Counterpart (C++ Focus)

- A destructor (`~ClassName()`) is automatically invoked when an object **goes out of scope** or is explicitly `delete`-d.
- Java **does not have** traditional destructors — it uses **Garbage Collection** + the deprecated `finalize()` method (removed in Java 18+).
- KTU 2024 question: *"Differentiate between constructor and destructor"* — **must** include a side-by-side table.

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** Constructor initializes hardware register pointers to safe defaults.
- **GUI Frameworks:** Constructors of Swing/JavaFX components set up the initial visual state (size, title, color).
- **Database ORMs (Hibernate):** Constructor accepts row data to materialize entity objects.
- **Game Engines:** Constructor loads mesh/texture references for `Player` and `Enemy` objects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Protocol:** Every code block below is **analytically derived** step-by-step. Read the *explanation line* above every code block — these are the marking-scheme keywords KTU examiners actively scan for.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Evolution of a `Student` Class

### Step 1: Start with a class that has no constructor

The compiler will inject a default constructor automatically.

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;
}
// Compiler auto-generates: Student() { super(); }
```

**Explanation line:** When no constructor is declared, the **Java compiler** synthesizes a public no-arg constructor that calls `super();` to invoke `Object()`.

### Step 2: Add a user-defined no-arg constructor

This **replaces** the compiler-generated one. The body can now contain logic.

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;

    Student() {
        rollNo = 0;
        name  = "Not Assigned";
        System.out.println("No-arg constructor called");
    }
}
```

**Explanation line:** A user-defined no-arg constructor **suppresses** the compiler's default. It still implicitly calls `super();` on line 1 of its body.

### Step 3: Add a parameterized constructor (overloading begins)

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;

    Student() {
        this(0, "Not Assigned");
    }

    Student(int r, String n) {
        this.rollNo = r;
        this.name   = n;
    }
}
```

**Explanation line:** The no-arg constructor **chains** to the parameterized one using `this(0, "Not Assigned")`. Note that `this(...)` is the **first executable statement** of the no-arg constructor — this is non-negotiable per KTU marking rules.

### Step 4: Demonstrate the call sequence

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();
        Student s2 = new Student(101, "Arjun");
    }
}
```

**Execution trace (for viva marks):**
1. `new Student()` → no-arg constructor → `this(0,"Not Assigned")` → parameterized constructor → `Object()` → fields set to `0`, `"Not Assigned"`.
2. `new Student(101,"Arjun")` → parameterized constructor → `Object()` → fields set to `101`, `"Arjun"`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Copy Constructor in C++ (Full Walkthrough)

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>
using namespace std;

class Book {
private:
    char* title;
    double price;

public:
    // Parameterized Constructor
    Book(const char* t, double p) {
        title = new char[strlen(t) + 1];
        strcpy(title, t);
        price = p;
    }

    // Copy Constructor (Deep Copy)
    Book(const Book& other) {
        title = new char[strlen(other.title) + 1];
        strcpy(title, other.title);
        price = other.price;
    }

    void display() {
        cout << "Title: " << title << " | Price: " << price << endl;
    }

    ~Book() {
        delete[] title;
    }
};
```

### Step-by-step analytical trace:

1. **`Book b1("OOP in Java", 499.99);`** → parameterized constructor allocates a **new heap buffer** of 12 bytes, copies the string, assigns price.
2. **`Book b2 = b1;`** → C++ invokes the **user-defined copy constructor** (not memberwise copy) because we declared one.
3. **Inside the copy constructor:** a *separate* heap buffer is allocated for `b2.title` — this is **deep copy** and prevents the **dangling-pointer / double-free** disaster that a default shallow copy would cause.
4. **`b1.display()` and `b2.display()`** → both print correct, independent data.
5. **Destructor calls:** when `main` ends, `b2.~Book()` runs first (stack LIFO), then `b1.~Book()` — no double-free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the KTU Examiner Awards Full Marks:** A complete answer must show *both* the constructor pair *and* the destructor with `delete[]` — and explicitly mention the word **"deep copy"** in the explanation. A shallow-copy default copy constructor is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Constructor Chaining with `this()` and `super()`

```java
class Person {
    String name;
    int age;

    Person() {
        this("Unknown", 0);
    }

    Person(String n) {
        this(n, 18);
    }

    Person(String n, int a) {
        this.name = n;
        this.age  = a;
    }
}

class Employee extends Person {
    double salary;

    Employee() {
        this(0.0);
    }

    Employee(double s) {
        super("Default", 30);
        this.salary = s;
    }
}
```

### Analytical trace for `new Employee(50000.0)`:

1. JVM pushes frame → `Employee(double)` constructor starts.
2. First line: `super("Default", 30)` → calls `Person(String, int)`.
3. `Person(String, int)` sets `name = "Default"`, `age = 30`. Returns.
4. Back in `Employee(double)`: line 2 executes → `this.salary = 50000.0`.
5. Object fully constructed.

### The Rule of Termination (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):

- Every `this()` chain **must terminate** at a constructor that calls `super()`.
- If the chain loops (e.g., `A() { this(); }` and `B() { this(); }`) → **infinite recursion** → `StackOverflowError` at runtime.

---

## 3.4 Constructor vs Method — Exam-Ready Analytical Distinction

| S.No. | Constructor | Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Name = Class name | Name = any identifier |
| 2 | No return type | Must have return type |
| 3 | Called implicitly via `new` | Called explicitly via `obj.method()` |
| 4 | Used to initialize state | Used to expose/execute behavior |
| 5 | Cannot be inherited | Can be inherited |
| 6 | Cannot be overridden | Can be overridden |
| 7 | Compiler provides default | Compiler never provides default |
| 8 | Cannot be `abstract` | Can be `abstract` |
| 9 | Memory: Method area | Memory: Method area |
| 10 | Invoked **once per object** (at birth) | Invoked **multiple times** in lifetime |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Protocol:** All diagrams below use **Mermaid** syntax. Node IDs are strictly alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compiler safety net. Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric — no markdown formatting inside node quotes.

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy — Types of Constructors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CONSTRUCTORS] --> B[DEFAULT IMPLICIT]
    A --> C[NO ARG USER DEFINED]
    A --> D[PARAMETERIZED]
    A --> E[COPY CONSTRUCTOR]
    A --> F[PRIVATE PROTECTED]
    B --> B1[Compiler injected]
    B --> B2[Body is empty]
    B --> B3[Disappears if any user constructor written]
    C --> C1[Programmer defined]
    C --> C2[No parameters]
    C --> C3[Can have logic]
    D --> D1[Accepts arguments]
    D --> D2[Enables custom init]
    D --> D3[Suppresses default]
    E --> E1[Accepts same class object]
    E --> E2[Used for cloning]
    E --> E3[Deep copy in C++]
    F --> F1[Prevents instantiation]
    F --> F2[Used in Singleton]
    F --> F3[Utility classes]
```

## 4.2 Object Creation Lifecycle — From `new` to Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[STEP 1 Class loaded in Method Area] --> S2
    S2[STEP 2 new keyword triggers constructor search] --> S3
    S3[STEP 3 Compiler checks matching constructor] --> S4
    S4[STEP 4 super call to parent class] --> S5
    S5[STEP 5 Parent constructor executes top to bottom] --> S6
    S6[STEP 6 this chaining resolves if present] --> S7
    S7[STEP 7 Current class instance variables initialized] --> S8
    S8[STEP 8 Constructor body executes] --> S9
    S9[STEP 9 Object reference returned to calling code]
```

## 4.3 Constructor Chaining — `this()` vs `super()` Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Constructor called] --> Q2{this or super written explicitly}
    Q2 -->|No| Q3[Compiler injects super Object]
    Q2 -->|Yes this| Q4[Chains to same class constructor]
    Q2 -->|Yes super| Q5[Chains to parent class constructor]
    Q4 --> Q6{Does chain terminate at super}
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Valid construction flow]
    Q6 -->|No infinite loop| Q8[StackOverflowError at runtime]
```

## 4.4 Memory Architecture — Constructor Call Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    MEM[HEAP MEMORY] --> OBJ1[Student Object s1]
    MEM --> OBJ2[Student Object s2]
    OBJ1 --> D1[rollNo equals 101]
    OBJ1 --> D2[name equals Arjun]
    OBJ2 --> D3[rollNo equals 0]
    OBJ2 --> D4[name equals Not Assigned]
    STK[CALL STACK] --> F1[main frame]
    F1 --> F2[Student s1 constructor frame]
    F1 --> F3[Student s2 constructor frame]
    F2 --> F4[Object super frame]
    F3 --> F4
    META[METHOD AREA] --> CL[Student class metadata]
    CL --> CC1[Constructor Student int String]
    CL --> CC2[Constructor Student no args]
    CL --> V1[rollNo instance variable template]
    CL --> V2[name instance variable template]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 1]`
**What is a constructor? List any two of its characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **constructor** is a special member function that is **automatically invoked** when an object of a class is created. Its primary role is to **initialize** the data members of the newly created object to valid initial values.

**Two characteristics:**
1. The name of the constructor must be **exactly the same** as the class name.
2. Constructors **do not have any return type**, not even `void`, and they are called implicitly by the `new` keyword at the time of object creation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP, Module 1]`
**Differentiate between a constructor and a method in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Constructor | Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Name must match the class name. | Name can be any valid identifier. |
| 2 | No return type is specified. | Must have a return type (`void` or data-type). |
| 3 | Invoked automatically during object creation. | Invoked explicitly using an object reference. |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (University ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**a)** Explain the different types of constructors in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Java supports the following types of constructors:

**1. Default Constructor (Implicit Constructor)**
The Java compiler automatically provides a default no-argument constructor **only if** the programmer does not define any constructor. It calls `super()` to invoke the parent class constructor and contains an empty body.

```java
class Demo {
    int x;
}
// Compiler provides: Demo() { super(); }
Demo d = new Demo(); // x is initialized to 0
```

**2. No-Argument (User-Defined) Constructor**
A programmer-written constructor that takes **no parameters**. It is used to perform custom initialization logic.

```java
class Demo {
    int x;
    Demo() {
        x = 100;
        System.out.println("No-arg constructor invoked");
    }
}
```

**3. Parameterized Constructor**
A constructor that accepts one or more parameters to allow **custom initial values** at object creation time.

```java
class Demo {
    int x;
    Demo(int value) {
        x = value;
    }
}
Demo d = new Demo(50); // x becomes 50
```

**4. Copy Constructor (Simulated in Java)**
Java does not have a built-in copy constructor like C++, but it is **simulated** by passing an object of the same class.

```java
class Demo {
    int x;
    Demo(Demo obj) {
        this.x = obj.x;
    }
}
Demo d1 = new Demo(20);
Demo d2 = new Demo(d1); // d2.x = 20
```

**5. Private Constructor**
Used to **prevent instantiation** from outside the class — common in Singleton patterns and utility classes like `java.lang.Math`.

---

**b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **constructor overloading** and **constructor chaining** using `this()`. Explain the execution flow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Box {
    int length, breadth, height;

    Box() {
        this(1, 1, 1);
        System.out.println("No-arg constructor");
    }

    Box(int side) {
        this(side, side, side);
        System.out.println("Single-arg constructor");
    }

    Box(int l, int b, int h) {
        this.length  = l;
        this.breadth = b;
        this.height  = h;
        System.out.println("Three-arg constructor");
    }

    int volume() {
        return length * breadth * height;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box();
        Box b2 = new Box(5);
        Box b3 = new Box(2, 3, 4);
        System.out.println("Vol b1 = " + b1.volume());
        System.out.println("Vol b2 = " + b2.volume());
        System.out.println("Vol b3 = " + b3.volume());
    }
}
```

**Execution flow for `new Box()`:**
1. `Box()` executes → `this(1,1,1)` is the first statement.
2. Control jumps to `Box(int l, int b, int h)`.
3. Instance variables are initialized to `1, 1, 1`.
4. `Box(int, int, int)` finishes; control returns to `Box()`.
5. `"No-arg constructor"` is printed.

**Output:**
```
Three-arg constructor
No-arg constructor
Three-arg constructor
Single-arg constructor
Three-arg constructor
Vol b1 = 1
Vol b2 = 125
Vol b3 = 24
```

**Key Exam Point:** The `this(...)` call **must be the first line** of the constructor, and the **chain must terminate** at a constructor that calls `super()` (or relies on the implicit one).

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**a)** Explain the concept of a **copy constructor** in C++ with an example. Why is a user-defined copy constructor necessary for classes with pointer members? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **copy constructor** in C++ is a special constructor that initializes a new object as a **copy of an existing object** of the same class. Its signature is:

```cpp
ClassName(const ClassName& oldObj);
```

**Example:**

```cpp
class Student {
    int id;
    char* name;
public:
    Student(int i, const char* n) {
        id = i;
        name = new char[strlen(n) + 1];
        strcpy(name, n);
    }

    Student(const Student& s) {
        id = s.id;
        name = new char[strlen(s.name) + 1];
        strcpy(name, s.name);
    }

    void display() {
        cout << "ID: " << id << " Name: " << name << endl;
    }

    ~Student() { delete[] name; }
};
```

**Why user-defined copy constructor is necessary:**

If the class has **pointer members**, the **default compiler-generated copy constructor** performs a **shallow copy** — both the original and the copy point to the **same memory location**. This leads to:

1. **Dangling Pointer:** When the first object is destroyed, `delete[] name` frees the memory. The second object still holds the now-invalid pointer.
2. **Double Free:** When the second object's destructor runs, it tries to free the same memory again → runtime crash.
3. **Shared Mutation:** Modifying one object's name also modifies the other (because they share the same buffer).

The **user-defined deep copy** allocates **new memory** for the copy, ensuring each object owns its resources independently.

---

**b)** Compare and contrast **constructors and destructors** in C++. Provide a real-world scenario where destructors are critical. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Constructor | Destructor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Name = Class name | Name = `~ClassName` |
| 2 | Invoked at object **creation** | Invoked at object **destruction** |
| 3 | Can be **overloaded** | Cannot be overloaded (only one) |
| 4 | Can have parameters | Cannot have parameters |
| 5 | Can use `this()` chaining | No chaining concept |
| 6 | Allocates / initializes resources | Releases / cleans up resources |
| 7 | Called once per object (at birth) | Called once per object (at death) |

**Real-World Scenario: Database Connection Pool**

```cpp
class DBConnection {
    Connection* conn;
public:
    DBConnection(const string& url) {
        conn = DriverManager::getConnection(url);
        cout << "Connection opened" << endl;
    }

    void query(string sql) {
        Statement* st = conn->createStatement();
        st->executeQuery(sql);
    }

    ~DBConnection() {
        conn->close();
        delete conn;
        cout << "Connection closed & memory freed" << endl;
    }
};
```

When a `DBConnection` object goes out of scope (e.g., end of a function or program), the **destructor is automatically invoked**, ensuring the database connection is **closed gracefully** and the allocated memory is **released**. Without a destructor, connection leaks and memory leaks would accumulate, eventually crashing the system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `this(...)` as the first statement** → causes the *Compilation Error: "call to this must be first statement in constructor"* and **loses 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing constructor with method:** A constructor with a `void` return type becomes a **method**, not a constructor — examiners deduct **3 marks** if this is not caught.
> 3. **Missing destructor `~ClassName()`** in C++ questions about classes with pointers — **guaranteed 2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Writing `super()` and `this()` in the same constructor** → impossible in Java. Pick exactly one.
> 5. **Forgetting that a programmer-defined constructor disables the default** — students often write `new MyClass()` and expect the default to still exist; examiners will deduct 1 mark per such oversight.
> 6. **Skipping the Mermaid/architecture diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a **diagram for full marks** on any 7+ mark constructor question.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **constructor** is a special initialization routine automatically invoked on object creation.
- It has **no return type** and **shares the class name**.
- **Types:** Default, No-arg, Parameterized, Copy, Private.
- If **any** user-defined constructor exists, the **default disappears**.
- **Constructor overloading** = multiple constructors with different parameter lists → enables compile-time polymorphism.
- **Constructor chaining via `this()`** reduces duplication but **must be the first line** and **must terminate** at a `super()` call.
- **Inheritance rule:** subclass constructor's first action is to invoke a parent constructor (`super(...)` or implicit `super()`).
- Constructors **cannot be inherited or overridden** — but they **can be overloaded**.
- **Destructor (`~ClassName`)** in C++ releases resources; Java relies on **Garbage Collection** + deprecated `finalize()` (removed since Java 18).
- A **deep copy** via a user-defined copy constructor is **mandatory** for classes containing pointer members to prevent dangling pointers and double-free errors.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw a **Mermaid/architecture diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is the single highest-ROI visual aid for OOP Module 1 questions.
- **Memory locations:** Constructor code lives in the **Method Area**; the *object* it initializes lives in the **Heap**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Modifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Access Modifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 | Access Modifiers — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), **Access Modifiers** (also called **access specifiers** or **visibility modifiers**) are reserved keywords in a programming language that govern the **visibility**, **accessibility**, and **scope of encapsulation** for classes, methods, constructors, and data members. They define a strict boundary rule determining *which* other classes or packages can legally interact with a given entity.

In Java (the reference language for KTU OECST615), access modifiers are the primary mechanism that implements the OOP pillar of **Encapsulation** at the language-enforcement level. There are exactly **four** access modifiers in Java:

1. **`private`** — Most restrictive.
2. **`default`** (package-private) — Implicit, no keyword required.
3. **`protected`** — Inheritance-aware visibility.
4. **`public`** — Least restrictive (globally accessible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Access modifiers are a *Module 1 core competency*. Questions typically test (a) the exact visibility table, (b) the difference between `protected` and `default`, and (c) the role of access modifiers in implementing **data hiding** under encapsulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House & Property" Model

Imagine each class as a **house on a private property** in a planned township (your program):

- **`private`** = The **master bedroom** of the house. Only the members of *that same household* (the defining class itself) can enter. Guests, neighbours, and outsiders are strictly turned away.
- **`default`** = The **private lawn** of the house. Any neighbour in the *same residential society* (same package) can walk on it, but outsiders from other societies cannot.
- **`protected`** = The **fenced backyard with a family gate**. The neighbours in your society can use it, AND your direct **children** (subclasses) living in another society (different package) also have the key through inheritance.
- **`public`** = The **public park entrance** of the township. Anyone, from any society or city, can come in freely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need this?** Without access modifiers, *any* part of a large codebase could touch *any* other part. This is called **tight coupling**, and it makes software fragile, insecure, and impossible to maintain. Access modifiers force programmers to expose only what is necessary — a cornerstone of robust OOP design.

## 1.3 Quick-Reference Definition Table

| Modifier | Keyword | Visibility Scope |
|----------|---------|-----------------|
| Private | `private` | Same class only |
| Default (Package-Private) | *(no keyword)* | Same package only |
| Protected | `protected` | Same package **+ subclasses** (any package) |
| Public | `public` | Everywhere (all classes, all packages) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 1 | Deep Theoretical Analysis & Visibility Rules

## 2.1 The Four Modifiers — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 `private` Access Modifier

- **Visibility:** Strictly confined to the **defining class only**.
- **Accessible from:** The same class.
- **Not accessible from:** Subclasses (even in the same package), other classes in the same package, classes in other packages.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequent *Part A (3-mark)* question is *"Can a `private` member be accessed from a subclass in the same package?"* The answer is **NO** — `private` ignores inheritance and package entirely.
- **Primary Use Case:** Implementing **data hiding** by making fields `private` and exposing them via public getter/setter methods (Encapsulation pattern).

### 2.1.2 Default (Package-Private) Access Modifier

- **Visibility:** No keyword is written. The access level is automatically the **default** when no modifier is specified.
- **Accessible from:** The same class, other classes in the **same package**.
- **Not accessible from:** Classes in any *other* package, even subclasses.
- **KTU Trap:** Many students assume `default` is a *fifth* keyword. It is **not a keyword at all** — it is the *absence* of any of the other three modifiers.
- **Primary Use Case:** Internal helper classes and package-level utility methods that should not be part of the public API.

### 2.1.3 `protected` Access Modifier

- **Visibility:** Same package **+** all subclasses (regardless of the package they live in).
- **Accessible from:** Same class, other classes in the same package, **and** any subclass in any package.
- **Not accessible from:** Non-subclass classes in a different package.
- **The Tricky Rule:** `protected` becomes accessible across packages **only through inheritance**. A subclass can access inherited `protected` members, but it **cannot** use them via a reference of the parent type coming from outside the inheritance chain.
- **Primary Use Case:** Designing a class framework where subclasses are expected to override or extend behaviour.

### 2.1.4 `public` Access Modifier

- **Visibility:** **Unrestricted / Global**.
- **Accessible from:** Every class in every package in the project (subject to module/visibility rules in modular Java 9+).
- **Primary Use Case:** The class API — method signatures meant to be invoked by external clients.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The "formula" here is the visibility matrix. Memorize this table — it accounts for the majority of access modifier marks in KTU exams.

| Visibility Granted To | `private` | `default` | `protected` | `public` |
|----------------------|:---------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|
| Same Class | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Same Package Subclass | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Same Package Non-Subclass | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Different Package Subclass | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Different Package Non-Subclass | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** ✔ = Accessible, ✘ = Not Accessible. This single table is the highest-yield revision artifact for the Access Modifiers topic in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional software engineering (the kind KTU graduates will practice), access modifiers enable:

- **API Design:** A library author marks only the documented methods as `public`. Internal state stays `private`. This prevents clients from depending on implementation details.
- **Versioning & Maintenance:** You can freely change a `private` field's type without breaking any external code — because no external code could touch it.
- **Security:** Sensitive fields (passwords, balances, internal flags) are never exposed as `public`.
- **Framework Design:** Frameworks like Spring and JavaFX use `protected` extensively so that user code can extend base classes while keeping the contract safe.

## 2.4 Special Case: Classes vs. Members

| Entity | Allowed Modifiers |
|--------|-------------------|
| Top-level Class | `public` or `default` only |
| Inner Class (Nested) | All four allowed (`private`, `default`, `protected`, `public`) |
| Interface (pre-Java 9) | All methods implicitly `public` |
| Class Members (Fields, Methods) | All four allowed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** A **top-level class** (i.e., a `.java` file's primary class) **cannot** be declared `private` or `protected`. The compiler will reject it. Only nested/inner classes can use those modifiers. This is a common 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 1 | Step-by-Step Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Demonstrating Each Modifier with a Working Example

Below is an exhaustive Java program that places all four access modifier behaviours in action. Read each line and its explanation.

```java
// File 1: package university.department;
package university.department;

public class Faculty {
    private    int    employeeId   = 101;    // Rule 1: Strictly class-private
                  String  department  = "CSE";  // Rule 2: Default - package-only
    protected  String  designation = "Professor"; // Rule 3: Package + subclasses
    public     String  collegeName = "KTU Model College"; // Rule 4: Global

    public void showPrivate() {
        // ✔ ALLOWED: accessing private member from same class
        System.out.println("Private ID: " + employeeId);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of the rules used above:**

- `employeeId` is `private`. Only `Faculty` (and any nested class inside it) can read/write it.
- `department` has **no keyword** — it is `default`. Any class in `university.department` can use it; classes in other packages cannot.
- `designation` is `protected`. Accessible in `university.department` AND by any subclass of `Faculty`, even one that lives in a completely different package.
- `collegeName` is `public` — visible to the entire world.

## 3.2 Cross-Package Subclass Access — The Tricky Case

```java
// File 2: package university.admissions;
// Lives in a DIFFERENT package
package university.admissions;
import university.department.Faculty;

public class VisitingFaculty extends Faculty {
    public void demo() {
        // System.out.println(employeeId);   // ✘ COMPILE ERROR: private
        // System.out.println(department);   // ✘ COMPILE ERROR: default
        System.out.println(designation);     // ✔ OK: protected, accessed via inheritance
        System.out.println(collegeName);     // ✔ OK: public
    }
}
```

**Conversion logic, line by line:**

1. `employeeId` → `private` → **rejected** by compiler. The subclass `VisitingFaculty` is not inside `Faculty`, so private access is denied.
2. `department` → `default` → **rejected**. The subclass is in package `university.admissions`, not `university.department`.
3. `designation` → `protected` → **allowed** because `VisitingFaculty` *is a* `Faculty` (subclass relationship). The compiler treats the access as legitimate inheritance.
4. `collegeName` → `public` → **always allowed**.

## 3.3 Non-Subclass Cross-Package — The Strictest Case

```java
// File 3: package university.admissions;
// NOT a subclass of Faculty
package university.admissions;
import university.department.Faculty;

public class AdminOffice {
    public void printDetails() {
        Faculty f = new Faculty();
        // System.out.println(f.employeeId);   // ✘ private
        // System.out.println(f.department);   // ✘ default (different package)
        // System.out.println(f.designation);  // ✘ protected (not a subclass)
        System.out.println(f.collegeName);     // ✔ public only
    }
}
```

**Conversion logic:**

- Even though `AdminOffice` *uses* `Faculty`, it does not *inherit* from it. So `protected` collapses into the same restriction as `default`. Only `public` survives.
- This is the exact rule the KTU examiner tests in 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions.

## 3.4 The Encapsulation Pattern (Why `private` Matters)

The single most important application of `private` in OOP is **encapsulation**:

$$
\text{Encapsulation} = \text{Data Hiding} + \text{Controlled Access via Public Methods}
$$

```java
public class BankAccount {
    private double balance;   // Hidden internal state

    public double getBalance() {      // Read-only controlled access
        return balance;
    }

    public void deposit(double amount) {   // Write via validated method
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance = balance + amount;
        }
    }
}
```

**Why is this superior to making `balance` `public`?**

$$
\text{If } \texttt{balance} \text{ were public: } \text{any code could set } \texttt{balance} = -10^{9}
$$

$$
\text{With } \texttt{private} \text{ + method: validation enforced at one single boundary point}
$$

This eliminates an entire class of bugs. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly links access modifiers to encapsulation, and a 14-mark question often asks you to "Demonstrate encapsulation using access modifiers with an example."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 1 | Structural Diagram — Access Control Flow

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Visibility Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Java Access Modifiers] --> B[private]
    A --> C[default]
    A --> D[protected]
    A --> E[public]

    B --> B1[Same Class Only]
    B --> B2[Most Restrictive]
    B --> B3[Used for Data Hiding]

    C --> C1[Same Class]
    C --> C2[Same Package]
    C --> C3[Package Private - No Keyword]

    D --> D1[Same Class]
    D --> D2[Same Package]
    D --> D3[Subclasses in Any Package]
    D --> D4[Inheritance Aware]

    E --> E1[Every Class in Every Package]
    E --> E2[Global Visibility]
    E --> E3[Public API Marker]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Encapsulation Boundary

The diagram below models how a class forms a protective boundary around its data, exposing only a controlled public surface — the very essence of access modifier usage.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ExtCls[External Class in Different Package]
        EC[Client Code]
    end

    subgraph MyClass[Encapsulated Class Boundary]
        PUB[public method: getBalance]
        PRO[protected method: calculateInterest]
        DEF[default method: logTransaction]
        PVT[private field: balance]
    end

    EC -->|allowed| PUB
    EC -->|denied| PRO
    EC -->|denied| DEF
    EC -->|denied| PVT

    PUB --> PVT
    PRO --> PVT
    DEF --> PVT
```

**Reading the architecture:**

- The **External Client Code** sits outside the class boundary.
- Only the `public` method crosses the boundary. `protected`, `default`, and `private` are sealed off.
- Inside the class, *all* members (regardless of modifier) can interact freely — this is why `public` and `protected` methods can manipulate the `private` `balance` field.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Modifier Should I Pick?

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need global access?} -->|Yes| USE_PUBLIC[Use public]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a subclass-only member?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| USE_PROTECTED[Use protected]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it shared inside one package only?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| USE_DEFAULT[Use default - no keyword]
    Q3 -->|No| USE_PRIVATE[Use private]
```

**Practical mapping rule of thumb:**

$$
\text{public} \rightarrow \text{API} \mid \text{protected} \rightarrow \text{Framework Hooks} \mid \text{default} \rightarrow \text{Package Utilities} \mid \text{private} \rightarrow \text{Internal State}
$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define access modifiers in Java. List the four access modifiers available in Java with their visibility scope.**

**Model Answer:**

Access modifiers are reserved keywords in Java that control the **visibility and accessibility** of classes, methods, and data members. They enforce the OOP principle of **encapsulation** by restricting unauthorized access.

The four access modifiers in Java are:

1. **`private`** — Accessible only within the same class.
2. **`default`** (package-private) — Accessible within the same package; no keyword required.
3. **`protected`** — Accessible within the same package **and** by subclasses in any package.
4. **`public`** — Accessible from anywhere, across all packages.

*(Examiner note: All four must be named with their correct scope for full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `private` and `protected` access modifiers in Java with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `private` | `protected` |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Keyword | Required | Required |
| Same class | ✔ Accessible | ✔ Accessible |
| Same package, non-subclass | ✘ Not accessible | ✔ Accessible |
| Different package, subclass | ✘ Not accessible | ✔ Accessible (via inheritance only) |
| Different package, non-subclass | ✘ Not accessible | ✘ Not accessible |

**Example:**

```java
package university.dept;
public class A {
    private int x = 10;
    protected int y = 20;
}

package university.admission;
import university.dept.A;
public class B extends A {
    void demo() {
        // System.out.println(x);  // ERROR - private not inherited
        System.out.println(y);     // OK - protected accessible via inheritance
    }
}
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In Part B, the student answers **one** question from a module choice set. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four access modifiers in Java with a neat visibility table. How do they support the OOP principle of **encapsulation**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to demonstrate **encapsulation** using a `Student` class with `private` fields `name`, `rollNumber`, and `cgpa`. Provide public getter and setter methods with **input validation** (cgpa must be between 0 and 10). Create a main method to test the class.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The four access modifiers in Java, in increasing order of visibility, are:

1. **`private`** — The most restrictive. A `private` member is visible only inside the class that defines it. It cannot be accessed by subclasses (even in the same package) or by any other class.

2. **`default`** — Also called *package-private*. When no modifier is written, this is the default. The member is accessible to all classes in the **same package** but hidden from classes in other packages — including subclasses.

3. **`protected`** — A subclass-aware modifier. The member is accessible within the same package (like default) **and** by subclasses living in *any* package, provided the access is through inheritance.

4. **`public`** — The least restrictive. The member is accessible from any class in any package, making it part of the public API.

**Visibility Table:**

| Visibility | `private` | `default` | `protected` | `public` |
|------------|:---------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|
| Same Class | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Same Package, Subclass | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Same Package, Non-Subclass | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Different Package, Subclass | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Different Package, Non-Subclass | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |

**Relation to Encapsulation:** Encapsulation is the OOP principle of *bundling data and the methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class)* and *restricting direct outside access to some of the object's components*. Access modifiers are the **language-level enforcement mechanism** for this principle. By declaring fields as `private`, the class hides its internal state. By exposing carefully chosen `public` methods, the class provides a controlled interface — this is sometimes called the **"black-box" design**. Modifiers like `protected` allow controlled extension through inheritance, and `default` enforces module-level boundaries within a package.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

```java
public class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNumber;
    private double cgpa;

    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }

    public void setName(String name) {
        if (name != null && !name.isEmpty()) {
            this.name = name;
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid name.");
        }
    }

    public int getRollNumber() {
        return rollNumber;
    }

    public void setRollNumber(int rollNumber) {
        if (rollNumber > 0) {
            this.rollNumber = rollNumber;
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid roll number.");
        }
    }

    public double getCgpa() {
        return cgpa;
    }

    public void setCgpa(double cgpa) {
        if (cgpa >= 0.0 && cgpa <= 10.0) {
            this.cgpa = cgpa;
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid CGPA. Must be 0-10.");
        }
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Roll No: " + rollNumber);
        System.out.println("CGPA: " + cgpa);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student();
        s.setName("Ananya");
        s.setRollNumber(42);
        s.setCgpa(9.2);
        s.display();
        s.setCgpa(15.0);   // Validation triggers
    }
}
```

**Explanation of validation logic:** The `setCgpa` method accepts the input only if the value lies in the closed interval $[0, 10]$. Any out-of-range value is rejected and the internal `cgpa` field remains unchanged. This is the *practical* benefit of `private` + public setters — **the boundary check exists in exactly one place**, no matter how many times the field is updated across the program.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between `default` and `protected` access modifiers. Explain with a **cross-package subclass example** why `protected` is *strictly more permissive* than `default`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a class hierarchy with a `Shape` superclass in package `geometry.base` having a `protected double area` field, and a subclass `Circle` in package `geometry.derived`. Write the code and show that `Circle` can access `area` while a non-subclass in `geometry.derived` **cannot**.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Difference between `default` and `protected`:**

| Aspect | `default` | `protected` |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Keyword | None | `protected` |
| Package-only access | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes |
| Cross-package subclass access | ✘ No | ✔ Yes |

**Why `protected` ⊃ `default`:** The `default` modifier closes its visibility at the package boundary. A subclass living in another package is treated as an *outsider* by the package-level rule. `protected`, however, explicitly extends visibility to subclasses **regardless of package**, provided the access is through inheritance (i.e., the member is accessed on a `this` or subclass-type reference, not on a parent reference from outside the inheritance chain).

**Code demonstration:**

```java
// File: geometry/base/Shape.java
package geometry.base;
public class Shape {
    int packageField = 1;          // default
    protected int protField = 2;   // protected
}

// File: geometry/derived/Circle.java
package geometry.derived;
import geometry.base.Shape;

public class Circle extends Shape {
    void demo() {
        // System.out.println(packageField); // ERROR - default, different package
        System.out.println(protField);      // OK - protected, inherited
    }
}
```

This proves `protected` permits what `default` forbids.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

```java
// File: geometry/base/Shape.java
package geometry.base;

public class Shape {
    protected double area;

    public Shape(double area) {
        this.area = area;
    }
}
```

```java
// File: geometry/derived/Circle.java
package geometry.derived;
import geometry.base.Shape;

public class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double radius) {
        super(0);                          // initialize parent area
        this.radius = radius;
        this.area = Math.PI * radius * radius;   // ✔ can access protected 'area' via inheritance
    }

    public void printArea() {
        System.out.println("Area = " + area);
    }
}
```

```java
// File: geometry/derived/GeometryHelper.java
package geometry.derived;
import geometry.base.Shape;

public class GeometryHelper {
    public void show() {
        Shape s = new Shape(100);
        // System.out.println(s.area);   // ERROR - protected, non-subclass, different package
    }
}
```

**Logic:** In `Circle`, `area` is accessed *implicitly via inheritance* (`this.area = ...`) — the compiler accepts it. In `GeometryHelper`, the access is on a `Shape` reference from a class that does *not* extend `Shape`, in a different package — the compiler rejects it. This is the precise rule that distinguishes `protected` from `default`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Writing `default` as a keyword.** It is **NOT** a keyword. The default access is the *absence* of any modifier. Writing `default int x;` is a syntax error. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing `protected` cross-package access.** Students often write code where a non-subclass class accesses a `protected` field via a parent reference. The compiler rejects it; full marks lost if the code "works" in your head but won't compile.
> 3. **Missing the `public` keyword on a top-level class.** Forgetting `public class Main` only marks the class package-private; examiners deduct if the question requires global access.
> 4. **Skipping the visibility table** in 7-mark questions. The visibility matrix is worth at least **2-3 marks** by itself; never omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✔ There are **exactly four** access modifiers in Java: `private`, `default`, `protected`, `public`.
- ✔ `default` is **not a keyword** — it is the *absence* of a modifier. Also called *package-private*.
- ✔ Visibility expands in the order: **`private` < `default` < `protected` < `public`**.
- ✔ `protected` ⊃ `default` in exactly **one** situation: **cross-package subclasses**.
- ✔ `private` members are **never** accessible from a subclass — not even in the same package.
- ✔ A **top-level class** can only be `public` or `default`. It cannot be `private` or `protected`.
- ✔ **Nested (inner) classes** can use all four access modifiers.
- ✔ Access modifiers are the **Java language mechanism for encapsulation** — pair `private` fields with `public` getters/setters.
- ✔ In KTU 14-mark questions, always include the **visibility table** and a **working code example** for full marks.
- ✔ The most common KTU trap: confusing the `protected` cross-package access rule. Remember: subclass only, and only through inheritance.
- ✔ Use `private` for fields, `public` for the API, `protected` for subclass-extension hooks, and `default` for package-level helpers — this is the **industry-standard** convention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: this Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** this Keyword

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Java, the **`this` keyword** is a **reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — the instance on which a method or constructor is currently being invoked. It is an **implicit, final, non-static** reference automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and passed as a hidden argument to every instance method and constructor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OECST615, Module 1):** The `this` keyword is examined as a foundational pillar of Java's object model. It must be understood in conjunction with constructors, instance variables, method chaining, and constructor overloading.

Mathematically, if we denote the current object instance as $O_{current}$ and a method invocation as $M(O_{current}, args...)$, then:

$$this \equiv O_{current}$$

The reference `this` is **type-compatible with the enclosing class** $C$, and is automatically typed as $C$ itself.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Room Number" Intuition

Imagine you are staying in a hotel. The hotel has hundreds of rooms, but every room has a unique number. When housekeeping knocks on the door and says *"May I clean **this** room?"*, the word **"this"** does not refer to a fixed room — it refers to **whichever room the staff is currently standing in front of**.

Similarly, in Java:

- The **class** is the hotel blueprint.
- The **objects** are the individual rooms.
- The **`this` keyword** is the room number tag that automatically points to the **specific room (object)** the code is currently working inside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** `this` is *not* a fixed reference. Its value changes dynamically depending on the calling object. For an object `obj1`, `this` inside `obj1.method()` points to `obj1`. For `obj2.method()`, `this` points to `obj2`.

---

## 1.3 Characteristics of `this` (Bullet-Form Snapshot)

- **Implicit:** It is automatically supplied by the JVM; the programmer never declares it.
- **Final:** The reference `this` cannot be reassigned (i.e., `this = new Object();` is a compile-time error).
- **Non-static:** It is associated with an instance, not a class.
- **Type:** The static type of `this` is the enclosing class $C$.
- **Scope:** It is available in **instance methods**, **constructors**, and **inner classes**, but **not** in `static` methods (since there is no current object in a static context).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Using `this` inside a `static` method results in the compile-time error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Six Principal Uses of the `this` Keyword

The Java Language Specification (JLS) and the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus collectively identify **six canonical uses** of `this`:

### Use 1: Differentiating Instance Variables from Local Variables (Most Common)
When a parameter or local variable **shadows** an instance variable of the same name, `this` is used to disambiguate.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int age;
    
    Student(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;  // LHS = instance variable, RHS = parameter
        this.age = age;
    }
}
```

### Use 2: Invoking a Constructor from Another Constructor (Constructor Chaining)
`this(args)` must be the **first statement** in the constructor body. This is called **explicit constructor invocation**.

```java
class Box {
    int l, b, h;
    Box() { this(1, 1, 1); }              // calls Box(int,int,int)
    Box(int l) { this(l, 1, 1); }         // calls Box(int,int,int)
    Box(int l, int b, int h) {            // canonical constructor
        this.l = l; this.b = b; this.h = h;
    }
}
```

### Use 3: Passing the Current Object as a Parameter
A reference to the current object can be passed to another method or constructor.

```java
class Helper {
    void display(Car c) { System.out.println(c); }
}
class Car {
    void show() { new Helper().display(this); }   // pass current Car
}
```

### Use 4: Returning the Current Object from a Method
This enables **method chaining** (fluent interface design pattern).

```java
class Builder {
    Builder setName(String n) { /* set */ return this; }
    Builder setAge(int a)    { /* set */ return this; }
}
// Usage:
// new Builder().setName("John").setAge(21);
```

### Use 5: Returning the Current Object from a Constructor
Although rarely used, a constructor can `return this;` (as a return statement, not as a value).

### Use 6: Used Inside an Inner Class to Refer to the Outer Class Object
The syntax is `OuterClass.this.member`.

```java
class Outer {
    int x = 10;
    class Inner {
        int x = 20;
        void test() {
            System.out.println(this.x);       // 20 (Inner's x)
            System.out.println(Outer.this.x); // 10 (Outer's x)
        }
    }
}
```

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Why `this` Exists (The "How")

1. **Object Instantiation:** When `new MyClass(args)` is executed, the JVM allocates heap memory for the new object.
2. **Hidden Argument Passing:** The JVM internally treats the call as `instanceMethod(hiddenThis, args)`. The hidden `this` is a reference to the newly allocated object.
3. **Field Resolution:** When the method accesses a non-static field, the JVM performs a lookup: *local scope → enclosing class fields (accessed via `this`) → inherited fields*.
4. **Stack Frame:** Each method invocation creates a stack frame. One slot in that frame is reserved for the `this` reference of the receiving object.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Use Case | Syntax Pattern | KTU Exam Frequency | Common Marks |
|---|----------|----------------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Disambiguate instance var vs local var | `this.field = param;` | Very High | 2–5 |
| 2 | Constructor chaining | `this(args);` *(first line only)* | Very High | 3–7 |
| 3 | Pass current object | `method(this);` | Medium | 2–3 |
| 4 | Return current object (fluent API) | `return this;` | Medium | 3–5 |
| 5 | Return from constructor | `return this;` | Low | 1–2 |
| 6 | Inner class → outer class | `OuterClass.this.field;` | Low–Medium | 2–4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Rule for KTU 2024:** `this(args)` or `this` as a call **must** be the **first executable statement** inside a constructor. Otherwise: *Compile-time error: "call to this must be first statement in constructor."*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Builder Design Pattern (GoF):** Method chaining via `return this;` is the cornerstone of the Builder pattern, used in frameworks like Lombok, Java's `StringBuilder`, and Android's `AlertDialog.Builder`.
- **Event-Driven Systems (Swing/JavaFX):** Anonymous inner classes often use `OuterClass.this` to access the enclosing GUI controller.
- **ORM Frameworks (Hibernate, JPA):** Entity classes use `this` inside `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `toString()` to refer to the persistent entity instance.
- **Fluent API Design:** Libraries like `Stream API` (`stream.filter(...).map(...).collect(...)`) rely heavily on the chainable return-this pattern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Disambiguating Instance vs Local Variable

Consider the following canonical KTU-style snippet:

```java
class Account {
    private double balance;       // instance variable
    
    public void deposit(double balance) {  // local parameter shadows it
        if (balance > 0) {
            this.balance = this.balance + balance;
        }
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Symbol Identification**

The class has an instance field $B_{inst}$ representing the account's balance. The method has a parameter $B_{param}$ representing the deposit amount.

$$B_{inst} = \text{member field of the current Account object}$$
$$B_{param} = \text{local argument passed to deposit()}$$

**Step 2 — Naked `balance` Resolution**

Inside the method, the bare identifier `balance` refers to the **closest scope**, which is the parameter $B_{param}$ (since parameters are local). The instance variable $B_{inst}$ is **shadowed**.

$$\text{Scope Hierarchy:} \quad \text{Local (param)} \rightarrow \text{Instance} \rightarrow \text{Class} \rightarrow \text{Inherited}$$

**Step 3 — Prefixing with `this`**

The expression `this.balance` forces the compiler to look up `balance` starting from the **instance scope** (skipping the local parameter).

$$\text{this.balance} \equiv B_{inst}$$
$$\text{balance} \equiv B_{param}$$

**Step 4 — Assignment Semantics**

The statement

```java
this.balance = this.balance + balance;
```

is therefore formally:

$$B_{inst} \leftarrow B_{inst} + B_{param}$$

That is, *add the deposit to the existing balance and reassign.*

**Step 5 — Final Reduced Form**

Using shorthand $\Delta$ for the new balance:

$$\Delta B_{inst} = B_{inst}^{old} + B_{param}$$

**Step 6 — Verification Trace**

Let $B_{inst}^{old} = 1000$ and $B_{param} = 500$. Then:

$$\Delta B_{inst} = 1000 + 500 = 1500$$

The object's internal state transitions from $1000 \rightarrow 1500$, confirming correct shadow resolution. ✓

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — Constructor Chaining Resolution

```java
class Rectangle {
    int length, breadth;
    
    Rectangle() { 
        this(10);                    // calls Rectangle(int)
        System.out.println("Default");
    }
    Rectangle(int side) { 
        this(side, side);            // calls Rectangle(int, int)
        System.out.println("Square");
    }
    Rectangle(int l, int b) { 
        this.length = l; 
        this.breadth = b; 
        System.out.println("Custom");
    }
}
```

**Call:** `new Rectangle();`

### Resolution Chain

| Step | Constructor Entered | Action | Output |
|------|--------------------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `Rectangle()` | Invokes `this(10)` | — |
| 2 | `Rectangle(int)` | Invokes `this(10,10)` | — |
| 3 | `Rectangle(int,int)` | Assigns `length=10, breadth=10` | "Custom" |
| 4 | Returns to `Rectangle(int)` | Continues after `this()` | "Square" |
| 5 | Returns to `Rectangle()` | Continues after `this()` | "Default" |

### Output Order (Top-to-Bottom on Console)

```
Custom
Square
Default
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Constructor chaining follows a *last-in, first-out* call-stack pattern. The deepest constructor executes first, and control unwinds back to the caller constructor.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common `this` Scenarios & Outcomes

| Scenario | Code | Compile? | Reason |
|----------|------|----------|--------|
| `this` in instance method | `void m() { System.out.println(this); }` | ✅ Yes | Instance context present |
| `this` in static method | `static void m() { System.out.println(this); }` | ❌ No | No current object in static context |
| `this` as first stmt in ctor | `A() { this(5); }` | ✅ Yes | First statement rule satisfied |
| `this` as second stmt | `A() { x=1; this(5); }` | ❌ No | Must be first statement |
| `this` reassignment | `this = new A();` | ❌ No | `this` is final |
| `return this;` from method | `A get() { return this; }` | ✅ Yes | Enables chaining |
| `this` inside anonymous class | `new Thread() { void r() { System.out.println(this); } };` | ✅ Yes | Anonymous class is still a class |
| `Outer.this` in inner class | `Outer.this.field;` | ✅ Yes | Qualifies outer reference |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
