<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the `this` keyword in Java? Why is it called a reference variable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `this` keyword in Java is an **implicit, final reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — i.e., the instance on which a method or constructor is currently being invoked. It is automatically created by the JVM and passed as a hidden argument to every non-static member. It is called a *reference variable* because it stores the **memory address** of the current object on the heap, just like any other object reference. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Can `this` be used inside a `static` method? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
No, `this` **cannot** be used inside a `static` method. This is because `static` members belong to the **class itself**, not to any particular object, and the `this` keyword always refers to the **current object instance**. Since no specific object is associated with a static context, the compiler raises the error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context."* **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the `this` keyword in Java. List and describe its **four major uses** with suitable code snippets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **constructor chaining** using the `this` keyword. Show the output and trace the call flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

The `this` keyword is an implicit reference to the current object. Its four major uses are:

**Use 1: Disambiguating instance variable from parameter**

```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    double salary;
    
    Employee(String name, double salary) {
        this.name = name;          // this.name = instance var
        this.salary = salary;      // name and salary on RHS = parameters
    }
}
```

**Use 2: Constructor chaining using `this(args)`**

```java
Employee()                  { this("Unknown", 0.0); }
Employee(String name)       { this(name, 25000.0); }
Employee(String name, double salary) {
    this.name = name;
    this.salary = salary;
}
```

**Use 3: Passing the current object as a parameter**

```java
void register(HRDepartment hr) {
    hr.addEmployee(this);
}
```

**Use 4: Returning the current object (enabling chaining)**

```java
Employee setSalary(double s) {
    this.salary = s;
    return this;
}
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

```java
class Box {
    int length, breadth, height;
    
    Box() {
        this(10);
        System.out.println("Default Box created.");
    }
    
    Box(int side) {
        this(side, side, side);
        System.out.println("Cube Box created with side " + side);
    }
    
    Box(int l, int b, int h) {
        this.length = l;
        this.breadth = b;
        this.height = h;
        System.out.println("Custom Box: " + l + "x" + b + "x" + h);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b = new Box();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Custom Box: 10x10x10
Cube Box created with side 10
Default Box created.
```

**Call Flow Trace:**

| Step | Constructor | Output Line |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | `main()` calls `new Box()` | — |
| 2 | `Box()` invokes `this(10)` | — |
| 3 | `Box(int)` invokes `this(10,10,10)` | — |
| 4 | `Box(int,int,int)` assigns fields | "Custom Box: 10x10x10" |
| 5 | Returns to `Box(int)`, prints | "Cube Box created with side 10" |
| 6 | Returns to `Box()`, prints | "Default Box created." |

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is method chaining? Demonstrate how the `this` keyword enables the **fluent interface** design pattern with a complete Java program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the use of `this` in the context of **inner classes**. Write a program that uses `OuterClass.this` to access the enclosing class's members from an inner class. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Method Chaining / Fluent Interface:** A design technique where multiple method calls are linked in a single statement because each method returns the current object via `return this;`. This produces readable, builder-like code.

```java
class Pizza {
    private String size;
    private boolean cheese;
    private boolean pepperoni;
    
    public Pizza setSize(String size) {
        this.size = size;
        return this;
    }
    
    public Pizza addCheese() {
        this.cheese = true;
        return this;
    }
    
    public Pizza addPepperoni() {
        this.pepperoni = true;
        return this;
    }
    
    public void describe() {
        System.out.println("Pizza: size=" + size + 
                           ", cheese=" + cheese + 
                           ", pepperoni=" + pepperoni);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Pizza p = new Pizza()
                    .setSize("Large")
                    .addCheese()
                    .addPepperoni();
        p.describe();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Pizza: size=Large, cheese=true, pepperoni=true
```

**Explanation:** Each setter ends with `return this;` so the next call operates on the same object. Without `this`, the chain would break after the first method returns `void`.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Use of `this` in Inner Classes:**

When an inner (non-static) class has a member with the same name as the outer class, `OuterClass.this` disambiguates which instance is being referenced.

```java
class University {
    String name = "KTU Kerala";
    
    class Department {
        String name = "CSE";
        
        void display() {
            System.out.println("Department name: " + this.name);
            System.out.println("University name: " + University.this.name);
        }
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        University u = new University();
        University.Department d = u.new Department();
        d.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Department name: CSE
University name: KTU Kerala
```

**Explanation:** Inside `display()`, the keyword `this` refers to the `Department` object, while `University.this` explicitly qualifies the reference to the enclosing `University` object.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting `this` in shadowing scenarios** — Examiner deducts 1–2 marks if `this.field = param;` is written as `field = param;`.
> 2. **Placing `this(args)` as second statement** — Full mark loss for that sub-part; *first statement rule* is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Using `this` in `static main()`** — Some students try `this.x` inside `main`. This is a compilation error in Java and attracts 0 marks for the sub-question.
> 4. **Confusing `this()` (constructor call) with `this` (reference)** — Always teach the difference: `this()` with parentheses = constructor invocation; `this` without = object reference.
> 5. **Omitting trace tables** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards step-by-step traces for output-based questions; skipping the trace loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- `this` is an **implicit, final reference** to the current object, supplied automatically by the JVM.
- It is **not** a keyword in the strict reserved-word sense in some older specs, but it is treated as a **keyword-like literal** in Java.
- `this` **cannot** be used in `static` methods or `static` initializers.
- Primary uses: (1) **field disambiguation**, (2) **constructor chaining**, (3) **passing current object**, (4) **returning current object**, (5) **inner class qualification**.
- `this(args)` must be the **first statement** of a constructor; only one such call per constructor is permitted.
- Method chaining via `return this;` is the foundation of the **Builder / Fluent API** pattern.
- In inner classes, `OuterClass.this.field` resolves naming conflicts with the enclosing class.
- `this` cannot be reassigned — it is a **final** reference, so `this = null;` or `this = new X();` is illegal.
- KTU Module 1 typically awards 5–10 marks cumulatively for questions involving `this`, `super`, and constructor overloading combined.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymorphism - Method Overloading, Using Objects as Parameters, Returning Objects, Recursion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Polymorphism - Method Overloading, Using Objects as Parameters, Returning Objects, Recursion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Polymorphism? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymorphism (Greek: poly = many, morph = forms)** is the ability of a single interface, function, or operator to operate on different data types or classes, exhibiting different behaviors based on the actual type of the operand at runtime. It is one of the four foundational pillars of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) along with **Encapsulation**, **Abstraction**, and **Inheritance**. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, polymorphism is treated at the **compile-time (static)** level through **Method Overloading** and **Operator Overloading** within the same class scope. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a single button on a TV remote labeled **"POWER"**: - On a TV, it turns the television on/off. - On an AC, it turns the air conditioner on/off. - On a washing machine, it turns the machine on/off. The **button shape and label (interface)** is identical, but the **internal action (behavior)** depends on which **appliance (object)** you point it at. That is polymorphism — **one name, many forms**. In Java, the same concept applies: the method name is the "label," and the "form" is determined by the **signature** (number, type, and order of parameters). --- ## 1.2 Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism) > [!NOTE] > **Method Overloading** is the mechanism of defining **two or more methods within the SAME class** that share the **same name** but differ in their **parameter list** (number of parameters, types of parameters, or order of parameters). The compiler resolves which method to invoke **at compile time**, which is why it is also called **Static Binding** or **Early Binding**. ### Conceptual Analogy: The "Add" Counter A kitchen counter that can: - Add **two integers** (2 + 3) - Add **two doubles** (2.5 + 3.7) - Add **three integers** (1 + 2 + 3) The action is always "add," but the input differs. Java lets you define a single `add(...)` method with several "forms." --- ## 1.3 Using Objects as Parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > In Java, all object references are passed **by value of the reference** (often called "pass by reference" colloquially). This means a **copy of the reference** to the object is passed to the method, not the actual object. As a result, the method can invoke methods on the object and modify the object's internal state, but it **cannot** rebind the caller's reference to a new object. ### Conceptual Analogy: The Hotel Key Card Imagine you check into a hotel and the receptionist gives you a **key card (reference copy)** to Room 305. You can: - ✅ Enter Room 305 and rearrange the furniture (modify the object's state). - ❌ Tell the receptionist that the room is now Room 999 (you cannot change the original room number stored at the front desk). --- ## 1.4 Returning Objects > [!NOTE] > A Java method can return a **reference to an object** of any class type, including user-defined classes. This is extremely useful in **factory method design patterns**, where a method constructs and returns a newly instantiated object, encapsulating the creation logic inside the calling method. ### Conceptual Analogy: The Vending Machine A vending machine has a button **"Get Drink"**. When pressed, it doesn't hand you raw materials (water, sugar, flavor). It internally builds the **finished product (object)** and returns it. Similarly, a Java method can internally instantiate an object and return the reference to the caller. --- ## 1.5 Recursion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recursion** is a programming technique in which a **method calls itself** directly or indirectly to solve a problem. Every recursive solution is composed of two essential components: > 1. **Base Case** — the termination condition that stops further recursive calls. > 2. **Recursive Case** — the part where the method invokes itself with a **smaller sub-problem** that progresses toward the base case. ### Conceptual Analogy: The Russian Nesting Dolls (Matryoshka) You open a doll to find a smaller doll inside. You keep opening until you reach the **smallest solid doll (base case)**. As you close them back up, each layer "returns" a result to the caller. Recursion works exactly the same way — calls stack up, and each one "unwinds" once the base case is hit. --- ## 1.6 Quick Reference Summary (Syllabus Highlights) | # | Concept | Type of Polymorphism | Binding | |---|---------|----------------------|---------| | 1 | Method Overloading | Compile-Time (Static) | Early | | 2 | Objects as Parameters | Pass by Reference Value | N/A | | 3 | Returning Objects | Object as Return Type | N/A | | 4 | Recursion | Self-Referential Method | N/A | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Method Overloading — Rules & Mechanics ### 2.1.1 Ways to Overload a Method The compiler distinguishes overloaded methods by their **method signature**, which consists of the **method name + parameter list**. The return type alone is **NOT** sufficient to overload a method. > [!NOTE] > **Valid ways to differentiate overloaded methods:** > 1. **Number of parameters** — `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(int a, int b, int c)` > 2. **Type of parameters** — `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)` > 3. **Order of parameters** — `display(int a, String s)` vs `display(String s, int a)` > [!WARNING] > **The following do NOT constitute valid overloading (compile-time error):** > - Changing **only** the return type > - Changing **only** the access modifier > - Changing **only** the `static` keyword ### 2.1.2 Automatic Type Promotion in Overloading When the exact match is not found, the Java compiler applies **widening primitive conversion** to find a matching method. The promotion order (smaller to larger) is: $$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$ $$\text{char} \rightarrow \text{int}$$ If `add(int, int)` is defined and we call `add(byte, byte)`, Java automatically promotes both bytes to `int` and invokes `add(int, int)`. If no match is found after promotion, a **compile-time error** occurs. ### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Math libraries** (e.g., `Math.pow(double, double)`, `Math.pow(int, int)`) — same name, different argument types. - **Print functions** (`System.out.println(int)`, `println(String)`, `println(double)`) — universal printing with one name. - **API design consistency** — reduces cognitive load on developers. --- ## 2.2 Using Objects as Parameters — Pass by Reference Value ### 2.2.1 Memory Model When you pass an object to a method, Java creates a **copy of the reference** (the memory address) on the method's stack frame. Both the caller and the callee point to the **same object** on the heap. **Two consequences:** 1. The method **can** modify the instance variables of the object (since it shares the same heap object). 2. The method **cannot** make the caller's reference point to a new object (reassignment inside the method affects only the local copy). ### 2.2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Linked list traversal** — pass a `Node` reference to traverse and modify list elements. - **GUI event handling** — pass `Component` references to update UI dynamically. - **Database row processing** — pass `ResultSet`/`Entity` objects to update fields. --- ## 2.3 Returning Objects ### 2.3.1 Mechanics A method signature can declare any class type as its return type. The method must either return an object of that exact type or a subtype (covariant return). **Typical use cases:** - **Factory methods** that hide construction logic. - **Builder pattern** step methods returning the modified object. - **Getters** that return deep copies of internal state. ### 2.3.2 Real-World Engineering Utility - **`Calendar.getInstance()`** — returns a fresh `Calendar` object. - **`Integer.valueOf(String s)`** — parses a string and returns an `Integer` object. - **DAO layers** — `findUser(int id)` returns a `User` object or `null`. --- ## 2.4 Recursion — Mechanics ### 2.4.1 The Recursive Call Stack Each recursive call creates a new **stack frame** in the JVM's call stack, holding: - Local variables of that invocation. - The return address (where to resume after the call). - A reference to the next recursive invocation. The recursion **unwinds** in **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** order — the deepest call resolves first, propagating its return value upward. ### 2.4.2 Termination Guarantee A well-formed recursive function must guarantee that the parameters progress toward the base case in every step. Otherwise, **`StackOverflowError`** is thrown by the JVM. ### 2.4.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Tree and graph traversal** (BST search, DFS, BFS). - **Divide and conquer algorithms** (merge sort, quicksort, binary search). - **Parsing and compiler design** (expression trees, recursive descent parsing). - **Fractal generation** in computer graphics. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Key Rule | Example | Pitfall | |---------|----------|---------|---------| | Method Overloading — Name | Same method name in same class | `add()` and `add(int, int)` | ❌ Changing only return type | | Method Overloading — Params | Must differ in number/type/order | `sum(int, int)` vs `sum(int, int, int)` | ❌ Changing only `static` | | Type Promotion | byte → short → int → long → float → double | `add(byte, byte)` matches `add(int, int)` | Varargs is lowest priority | | Pass Object as Param | Copy of reference passed | `m(obj)` shares heap memory | Cannot reassign caller's reference | | Return Object | Method can return class type | `public Student getStudent()` | Must return compatible subtype | | Recursion — Base Case | Stops the recursion | `if (n == 0) return 1;` | Missing base → `StackOverflowError` | | Recursion — Recursive Case | Calls itself with smaller problem | `return n * fact(n-1);` | Must ensure progress toward base | | Stack Frame Memory | Each call adds a frame | Depth `n` → `O(n)` memory | Very deep recursion → memory blow-up | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Method Resolution Algorithm (Compile Time) The Java compiler resolves an overloaded method call using the following **3-phase algorithm**: **Phase 1 — Exact Match:** Search for a method whose parameter types match the argument types **exactly** (no promotion, no autoboxing, no varargs). **Phase 2 — Widening Primitive Conversion:** If no exact match, attempt to find a method reachable by **widening primitive conversions** (e.g., `int` → `long`, `float` → `double`). **Phase 3 — Autoboxing / Varargs:** If still no match, attempt to find a method that uses **autoboxing** (`int` → `Integer`) or **varargs** (`int...`). If none of the phases yield a match → **Compile-Time Error: no suitable method found**. ### Worked Example: Tracing Method Resolution **Method definitions in class `Demo`:** - `void show(int a, double b)` - `void show(double a, int b)` - `void show(int... a)` **Call:** `obj.show(10, 20);` **Step-by-step resolution trace:** - **Step 1 — Exact match search:** No method takes `(int, int)`. → No match. - **Step 2 — Widening primitive conversion:** Can the call match `show(int, double)`? Second argument `20` is `int`, not `double`. Widening would need to convert the **second** argument to `double`, but then the signature becomes `(int, double)` — wait, the call is `(10, 20)` which is `(int, int)`. Widening the second `int` to `double` makes the call `show(int, double)`, but the first method expects `show(int, double)`. The original arguments `(10, 20)` are `(int, int)`. To match, we would need to widen **20** to `20.0`, giving `(int, double)`. This matches the first method! - **Step 3 — Compile time:** The compiler binds the call to `show(int, double)`. The second `int` argument is automatically promoted to `double`. **Result:** `show(10, 20)` invokes `show(int a, double b)` with `a = 10`, `b = 20.0`. --- ## 3.2 Recursion Trace — Factorial Derivation **Problem:** Compute $n!$ recursively. **Definition (mathematical):** $$ n! = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \text{ (Base Case)} \\ n \times (n-1)! & \text{if } n > 0 \text{ (Recursive Case)} \end{cases} $$ **Java implementation trace for `fact(4)`:** - **Step 1:** `fact(4)` is called. Base case (n == 0)? No. Compute `4 * fact(3)`. **Pause** — push return address onto stack. - **Step 2:** `fact(3)` is called. Base case? No. Compute `3 * fact(2)`. **Pause** — push return address. - **Step 3:** `fact(2)` is called. Base case? No. Compute `2 * fact(1)`. **Pause** — push return address. - **Step 4:** `fact(1)` is called. Base case? No. Compute `1 * fact(0)`. **Pause** — push return address. - **Step 5:** `fact(0)` is called. Base case! Return `1`. **Pop** stack. - **Step 6:** Back in `fact(1)`: `1 * 1 = 1`. Return 1. **Pop**. - **Step 7:** Back in `fact(2)`: `2 * 1 = 2`. Return 2. **Pop**. - **Step 8:** Back in `fact(3)`: `3 * 2 = 6`. Return 6. **Pop**. - **Step 9:** Back in `fact(4)`: `4 * 6 = 24`. Return 24. **Pop**. **Final result:** $\text{fact}(4) = 24$ **Time Complexity:** Each call does O(1) work, and there are $n+1$ calls. $$ T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) \implies T(n) = O(n) $$ **Space Complexity:** The call stack depth is $n+1$. $$ S(n) = O(n) $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pass by Value vs. Pass by Reference (Object) | Action in Method | Caller Sees Change? | Reason | |------------------|---------------------|--------| | Modify instance variable (e.g., `obj.x = 5`) | ✅ Yes | Same heap object shared | | Call mutator method (e.g., `obj.setX(5)`) | ✅ Yes | Same heap object shared | | Reassign reference (e.g., `obj = new Foo()`) | ❌ No | Only local copy of reference changes | | Nullify reference (e.g., `obj = null`) | ❌ No | Only local copy of reference changes | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Recursion vs. Iteration | Property | Recursion | Iteration | |----------|-----------|-----------| | Termination | Base case | Loop condition | | Memory | $O(\text{depth})$ stack frames | $O(1)$ extra memory | | Readability | Elegant for tree/graph problems | Cleaner for simple loops | | Performance | Function call overhead | Faster (no stack push/pop) | | Risk | `StackOverflowError` | Infinite loop | | Use case | Divide & conquer, backtracking | Linear scans, accumulators | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Polymorphism Classification (Module 2 Scope) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Polymorphism in Java]:::root --> B[Compile-Time Polymorphism]:::static A[Polymorphism in Java]:::root --> C[Run-Time Polymorphism]:::dynamic B[Compile-Time Polymorphism]:::static --> B1[Method Overloading]:::static2 B[Compile-Time Polymorphism]:::static --> B2[Operator Overloading]:::static2 B1[Method Overloading]:::static2 --> B1a[Different Number of Params]:::leaf B1[Method Overloading]:::static2 --> B1b[Different Types of Params]:::leaf B1[Method Overloading]:::static2 --> B1c[Different Order of Params]:::leaf C[Run-Time Polymorphism]:::dynamic --> D[Method Overriding]:::dynamic2 D[Method Overriding]:::dynamic2 -.Scope Module 3.-> E[Not in Module 2]:::na classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a44,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef static fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1a4d80,color:#ffffff classDef static2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#3a78b0,color:#ffffff classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#7fa9c9,color:#0b2a44 classDef dynamic fill:#c00000,stroke:#800000,color:#ffffff classDef dynamic2 fill:#e06060,stroke:#b03030,color:#ffffff classDef na fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#a6a6a6,color:#404040,stroke-dasharray: 4 3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Module 2 strictly focuses on Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism).** Run-Time Polymorphism via Method Overriding belongs to Module 3 (Inheritance). --- ## 4.2 Recursion Call Stack — `fact(3)` Visualization ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller as Main Method participant F3 as fact(3) participant F2 as fact(2) participant F1 as fact(1) participant F0 as fact(0) Caller->>F3: call fact(3) Note over F3: Push frame { n=3 } F3->>F2: call fact(2) Note over F2: Push frame { n=2 } F2->>F1: call fact(1) Note over F1: Push frame { n=1 } F1->>F0: call fact(0) Note over F0: Push frame { n=0 } Note over F0: BASE CASE HIT F0-->>F1: return 1 Note over F0: Pop frame Note over F1: 1 * 1 = 1 F1-->>F2: return 1 Note over F1: Pop frame Note over F2: 2 * 1 = 2 F2-->>F3: return 2 Note over F2: Pop frame Note over F3: 3 * 2 = 6 F3-->>Caller: return 6 Note over F3: Pop frame ``` --- ## 4.3 Pass-by-Reference-Value Memory Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StackFrame[Call Stack] R1[main: refA
points to 0xABC]:::ref R2[modify: refB
points to 0xABC]:::ref end subgraph Heap[Heap Memory - Shared Object] O1[Object at 0xABC
fieldX = 10]:::heapobj end R1 -.same address.-> O1 R2 -.same address.-> O1 R2 -- modifies fieldX --> O1 O1 -- now fieldX = 99 --> R1 classDef ref fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 classDef heapobj fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Both `refA` (in `main`) and `refB` (in `modify`) hold the **same memory address** `0xABC`. When `modify` changes the object's field, **both** references observe the change because they point to the same heap object. --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Returning Objects (Factory Pattern) ```mermaid flowchart TD Client[Client Code]:::client -->|calls| FactoryMethod[Factory Method]:::factory FactoryMethod -->|instantiates| NewObject[Newly Built Object]:::obj NewObject -->|returns reference| Client Client -->|uses returned object| Operations[Invoke Methods on Object]:::ops classDef client fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000 classDef factory fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 classDef obj fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 classDef ops fill:#f4b084,stroke:#c65911,color:#000000 ``` **Explanation of flow:** 1. The client calls a **factory method** (no constructor visibility needed). 2. The method internally **constructs** the object (allocation + initialization). 3. The method **returns** a reference to the freshly built object. 4. The client uses the reference to invoke operations. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define polymorphism. Differentiate between compile-time and run-time polymorphism with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymorphism** is the ability of a single interface or method name to exhibit different behaviors based on the type of data it operates on. It is derived from Greek: *poly* (many) and *morph* (forms). | Aspect | Compile-Time Polymorphism | Run-Time Polymorphism | |--------|---------------------------|------------------------| | Mechanism | Method Overloading | Method Overriding | | Binding | Early / Static binding | Late / Dynamic binding | | Resolution | At compile time | At run time via JVM | | Example | `add(int, int)` and `add(double, double)` | `Shape.draw()` overridden in `Circle`, `Square` | | Performance | Faster (no overhead) | Slightly slower (virtual dispatch) | *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for differentiation table + 1 for examples)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is recursion? State the two essential components of a recursive solution.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Recursion** is a programming technique in which a method calls itself, either directly or indirectly, to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller sub-problems. **Two essential components:** 1. **Base Case (Termination Condition):** The condition under which the recursion stops and returns a direct value without making any further recursive calls. Without a base case, the recursion would continue indefinitely, resulting in a `StackOverflowError`. 2. **Recursive Case:** The branch where the method calls itself with arguments that are **strictly closer to the base case**, ensuring progress toward termination. Each recursive call must reduce the problem size. **Example:** Computing factorial of $n$: $$ \text{fact}(n) = \begin{cases} 1 & n = 0 \text{ (base case)} \\ n \times \text{fact}(n-1) & n > 0 \text{ (recursive case)} \end{cases} $$ *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for base case + 1 for recursive case)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]** **A.** (a) Explain method overloading in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the rules for valid method overloading and the concept of automatic type promotion with an example. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Method overloading is the mechanism of defining multiple methods within the **same class** that share the **same name** but have **different parameter lists**. The compiler decides which method to invoke **at compile time**, making it a form of **static (compile-time) polymorphism**. **Rules for valid method overloading:** 1. Overloaded methods **must differ** in their parameter list — either by: - Number of parameters - Types of parameters - Order of parameters 2. The **return type alone** cannot distinguish overloaded methods (compile-time error). 3. The **access modifier** alone cannot distinguish them. 4. The `static` keyword alone cannot distinguish them. 5. **Constructors** can also be overloaded. **Example 1 — Different number of parameters:** ```java class Calculator { int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; } } ``` **Example 2 — Different types of parameters:** ```java class Display { void show(int x) { System.out.println("Integer: " + x); } void show(String s) { System.out.println("String: " + s); } void show(double d) { System.out.println("Double: " + d); } } ``` **Example 3 — Different order of parameters:** ```java class Register { void record(int id, String name) { System.out.println("ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name); } void record(String name, int id) { System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", ID: " + id); } } ``` **Automatic Type Promotion:** If the exact argument type is not found, the Java compiler applies **widening primitive conversion** to match a method. The promotion hierarchy is: $$ \text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double} $$ **Example:** ```java class Promo { void display(int x) { System.out.println("int method"); } void display(double x) { System.out.println("double method"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Promo p = new Promo(); p.display(10); // Exact match: int method p.display(10.5f); // float promotes to double: double method p.display('A'); // char promotes to int: int method } } ``` **Three-phase resolution algorithm (already covered in Section 3.1):** - Phase 1: Exact match - Phase 2: Widening primitive conversion - Phase 3: Autoboxing / Varargs *(7 marks: 2 for definition + rules, 2 for examples, 2 for type promotion, 1 for resolution algorithm)* --- **A.** (b) Write a Java program to compute the sum of digits of a given integer using **recursion**. Explain the base case and recursive case. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Mathematical Formulation:** Let $n$ be a non-negative integer with decimal representation $d_k d_{k-1} \dots d_1 d_0$. The sum of digits is: $$ \text{sumDigits}(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & n = 0 \text{ (base case)} \\ (n \bmod 10) + \text{sumDigits}(n / 10) & n > 0 \text{ (recursive case)} \end{cases} $$ **Java Program:** ```java import java.util.Scanner; class DigitSumRecursion { // Recursive method to compute sum of digits static int sumDigits(int n) { // Base case: when n becomes 0, return 0 if (n == 0) { return 0; } // Recursive case: extract last digit, add to recursive call return (n % 10) + sumDigits(n / 10); } public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter a positive integer: "); int num = sc.nextInt(); int result = sumDigits(num); System.out.println("Sum of digits of " + num + " = " + result); } } ``` **Explanation of Base Case and Recursive Case:** - **Base Case (`n == 0`):** When all digits have been processed, $n$ becomes 0. The method returns 0, terminating the recursion. This prevents infinite recursion and `StackOverflowError`. - **Recursive Case (`(n % 10) + sumDigits(n / 10)`):** The expression `n % 10` extracts the **last digit** of $n$. The expression `n / 10` **removes** the last digit (integer division). The method calls itself with this smaller number, ensuring progress toward the base case. **Dry Run for `sumDigits(1234)`:** - `sumDigits(1234)` = $4 + \text{sumDigits}(123)$ - `sumDigits(123)` = $3 + \text{sumDigits}(12)$ - `sumDigits(12)` = $2 + \text{sumDigits}(1)$ - `sumDigits(1)` = $1 + \text{sumDigits}(0)$ - `sumDigits(0)` = $0$ *(base case reached)* - Unwinding: $1 + 0 = 1 \to 2 + 1 = 3 \to 3 + 3 = 6 \to 4 + 6 = 10$ **Output:** ``` Enter a positive integer: 1234 Sum of digits of 1234 = 10 ``` **Time and Space Complexity:** $$ T(n) = T(n/10) + O(1) \implies T(n) = O(\log_{10} n) $$ $$ S(n) = O(\log_{10} n) \text{ (stack depth)} $$ *(7 marks: 2 for program logic, 2 for base/recursive case explanation, 2 for dry run, 1 for complexity)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]** **B.** (a) Explain the concept of passing objects as parameters to methods in Java. Write a Java program to demonstrate that the method receives a copy of the reference, and changes to the object's state are visible to the caller, but reassignment of the reference inside the method is NOT visible. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Concept — Pass by Value of Reference:** In Java, all object references are passed **by value of the reference** (commonly referred to as *pass by reference* in colloquial terms). This means: - A **copy of the memory address** is passed to the method. - The method's local parameter and the caller's variable both point to the **same object on the heap**. - The method **can** modify the object's instance variables (visible to caller). - The method **cannot** rebind the caller's reference to a different object. **Java Program Demonstrating Both Behaviors:** ```java class Student { String name; int marks; Student(String n, int m) { this.name = n; this.marks = m; } void setMarks(int m) { this.marks = m; } void display() { System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Marks: " + marks); } } class ObjectParamDemo { // Method 1: Modifies object state (VISIBLE to caller) static void modifyState(Student s) { s.setMarks(95); // Changes the actual object on the heap System.out.println("Inside modifyState -> Marks changed to 95"); } // Method 2: Reassigns reference (NOT visible to caller) static void reassignReference(Student s) { s = new Student("NewStudent", 50); // Local copy rebinds; caller unaffected s.display(); System.out.println("Inside reassignReference -> Reassigned local copy"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Student stu = new Student("Arjun", 75); System.out.println("--- Original State ---"); stu.display(); System.out.println("\n--- Calling modifyState ---"); modifyState(stu); stu.display(); // Marks = 95 (CHANGE IS VISIBLE) System.out.println("\n--- Calling reassignReference ---"); reassignReference(stu); stu.display(); // Still Marks = 95 (REASSIGNMENT NOT VISIBLE) } } ``` **Output:** ``` --- Original State --- Name: Arjun, Marks: 75 --- Calling modifyState --- Inside modifyState -> Marks changed to 95 Name: Arjun, Marks: 95 --- Calling reassignReference --- Name: NewStudent, Marks: 50 Inside reassignReference -> Reassigned local copy Name: Arjun, Marks: 95 ``` **Explanation:** - In `modifyState`, the line `s.setMarks(95)` operates on the **same heap object** that the caller's `stu` points to. Hence, the change is **visible**. - In `reassignReference`, the line `s = new Student(...)` creates a **new object** and makes the **local parameter `s`** point to it. The caller's `stu` still points to the original `Arjun` object. Hence, **no change** is visible in `main`. **Memory Visualization:** ``` main stack: stu [0xA1] --------+ | modifyState stack: | s [0xA1] ------+----> Heap: Student@0xA1 {name="Arjun", marks=75} reassignReference stack: s [0xB2] -------> Heap: Student@0xB2 {name="NewStudent", marks=50} (caller's stu is unchanged at 0xA1) ``` *(7 marks: 2 for concept, 2 for code, 2 for output and explanation, 1 for memory diagram)* --- **B.** (b) Write a Java program that uses a method to **return an object**. The method should accept two integers, compute their sum and product, and return a `Result` object containing both values. Demonstrate the use of the returned object in `main`. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Class Definition — `Result`:** ```java class Result { int sum; int product; Result(int s, int p) { this.sum = s; this.product = p; } void display() { System.out.println("Sum = " + sum + ", Product = " + product); } } ``` **Factory Method + Main Demonstration:** ```java class ObjectReturnDemo { // Method that RETURNS an object of type Result static Result compute(int a, int b) { int s = a + b; int p = a * b; Result r = new Result(s, p); // Object created inside the method return r; // Reference returned to caller } public static void main(String[] args) { int x = 12, y = 5; // Calling the method and capturing the returned object Result obj = compute(x, y); // Using the returned object System.out.println("Inputs: x = " + x + ", y = " + y); obj.display(); // Accessing fields directly via the returned reference System.out.println("Square of sum = " + (obj.sum * obj.sum)); System.out.println("Square of product = " + (obj.product * obj.product)); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Inputs: x = 12, y = 5 Sum = 17, Product = 60 Square of sum = 289 Square of product = 3600 ``` **Explanation:** 1. The method `compute` has the return type `Result` (a user-defined class). 2. Inside `compute`, a new `Result` object is instantiated with the computed sum and product. 3. The reference `r` is returned to `main` and stored in `obj`. 4. The caller can invoke methods (`display()`) or access fields (`obj.sum`, `obj.product`) on the returned object. 5. This pattern is known as the **Factory Method** — the calling code does not need to know the construction details. **Why this is useful:** - Encapsulates object construction. - Enables fluent APIs (Builder pattern). - Allows conditional object creation (returning `null` or different subtypes). *(7 marks: 2 for `Result` class, 3 for `compute` method and `main`, 1 for output + explanation, 1 for real-world relevance)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 2 — Polymorphism):** > 1. **Confusing overloading with overriding** — Overloading happens within the same class with different parameter lists. Overriding happens between parent and child classes with the SAME signature (Module 3 topic). Mixing these loses **2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Forgetting the base case in recursion** — A recursive method without a base case causes `StackOverflowError`. Examiners explicitly check for both base case and recursive case explanations. > 3. **Confusing "pass by reference" with "pass by value of reference"** — Java is strictly **pass by value**. For objects, the *value* being passed is the *reference*. Writing "Java passes objects by reference" will lose marks. > 4. **In type promotion problems**, students often forget that `char` promotes to `int` (not to `String`). Writing `p.show('A')` and saying it calls `show(String)` is wrong — it calls `show(int)`. > 5. **Return type alone does not constitute overloading** — Writing `int add(int a)` and `double add(int a)` is a compile-time error, not overloading. > 6. **Recursion trace dry run** — Examiners want you to show at least 3–4 levels of stack unwinding. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 2 — Polymorphism High-Yield Revision Points:** - **Polymorphism** = "many forms" → one interface, multiple implementations. - **Module 2 deals with Compile-Time (Static) Polymorphism** via **Method Overloading**. - **Method Overloading Rules:** - Same method name in the same class. - Must differ in **number / type / order** of parameters. - Return type, access modifier, and `static` alone **cannot** differentiate. - **Constructors** can also be overloaded. - **Type Promotion Order:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double` and `char → int`. - **3-Phase Resolution:** Exact match → Widening → Autoboxing / Varargs. - **Pass by Value of Reference (Object Parameters):** - Copy of reference is passed. - Modifying instance variables → **visible to caller**. - Reassigning reference → **NOT visible to caller**. - **Returning Objects:** - Any class can be a return type. - Enables **factory method pattern** and **builder pattern**. - Useful when construction logic is complex or conditional. - **Recursion Components:** - **Base case** — terminates recursion. - **Recursive case** — calls itself with smaller sub-problem. - **Recursion Examples (commonly asked):** - Factorial: $n! = n \times (n-1)!$ - Fibonacci: $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$ - Power: $x^n = x \times x^{n-1}$ - Sum of digits: $\text{sumDigits}(n) = (n \bmod 10) + \text{sumDigits}(n/10)$ - Tower of Hanoi, Binary search, GCD (Euclidean algorithm). - **Recursion Complexity:** - Linear recursion: $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ stack space. - Logarithmic recursion (e.g., binary search): $O(\log n)$ time, $O(\log n)$ space. - **Stack Overflow Risk:** Missing or unreachable base case → `StackOverflowError`. - **Iteration vs Recursion:** Recursion is elegant for trees/graphs; iteration is more memory-efficient. - **Common Exam Traps:** - Overloading with only return type change → **compile error**. - Calling `add(byte, byte)` when only `add(int, int)` exists → **type promotion** applies. - Recursion without base case → **infinite recursion**. - "Java is pass by reference" → **WRONG** (Java is pass by value; the value for objects is a reference).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overriding, Dynamic Method Dispatch, Using final with Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Method Overriding

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Method Overriding** is a core feature of Java's runtime polymorphism in which a subclass provides a *specific implementation* for a method that is already declared in its direct superclass. The overriding method must carry an **identical signature** (method name and parameter list) and a **covariant return type**, and it cannot be more restrictive in access modifier visibility than the parent's version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Method Overriding is the *enabling mechanism* behind Runtime (Dynamic) Polymorphism. Without overriding, the JVM cannot perform dynamic method dispatch.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Manager Override" Scenario
Imagine a large corporate office. The parent organization (the **Superclass**) issues a blanket rule: *"Every employee must submit a weekly report."* Different departments (the **Subclasses**) — such as *Engineering*, *Sales*, and *HR* — all inherit this rule. However, *Engineering* submits Git commit logs, *Sales* submits CRM pipeline numbers, and *HR* submits attendance summaries. The **rule name** is the same (`submitReport()`), but the **actual behavior is department-specific**. This is exactly what method overriding accomplishes in Java — same contract, different implementations based on the object type.

---

## 1.2 Dynamic Method Dispatch

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD)** is the runtime resolution mechanism performed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** in which a call to an overridden method is bound to the *actual object's method implementation* rather than the *reference variable's declared type*. This is the technical process that *executes* runtime polymorphism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DMD requires **two conditions** to operate:
> 1. **Upcasting** — A superclass reference variable must point to a subclass object (e.g., `Animal a = new Dog();`).
> 2. **Method Overriding** — The subclass must have overridden the inherited method.
>
> Without both, DMD has nothing to resolve and falls back to compile-time binding.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control"
Consider a universal TV remote. The remote has a single **"Power" button**. When you point it at a *Samsung* TV, the Samsung powers on with its specific boot sequence. When you point it at an *LG* TV, the LG powers on with its own boot sequence. The **button (the reference variable)** is the same, but the **TV (the actual object)** decides what happens. The JVM acts exactly like the remote — it never knows the concrete type at compile time, but at runtime it sends the signal to the right object.

---

## 1.3 Using `final` with Inheritance

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
In Java, the **`final`** keyword is a non-access modifier that *restricts modification*. When applied in the context of inheritance, it locks down three distinct elements:

1. **Final Class** — Cannot be subclassed (e.g., `String`, `Math` are final).
2. **Final Method** — Cannot be overridden by any subclass.
3. **Final Variable** — Cannot be reassigned; acts as a constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Using `final` with inheritance is a **design-level decision**. It signals to the compiler and other developers that the class/method contract is *closed for modification* but *open for use* (an early form of the **Open/Closed Principle** in SOLID design).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Blueprint"
Think of an architect who finalizes the design of a building's foundation. A structural engineer can still build *on top* of that foundation (extending), but cannot *redesign* the foundation itself. A `final` class or method is exactly that — a sealed blueprint: usable, but unchangeable. This guarantees **predictable behavior** across the entire inheritance hierarchy.

---

## 1.4 The Trifecta Relationship

| Concept | Role in the Trinity | Phase of Operation |
|---|---|---|
| **Method Overriding** | Defines *what* changes per subclass | Source-code level |
| **Dynamic Method Dispatch** | Decides *which* version runs | Runtime (JVM) |
| **`final` Keyword** | Locks *which* parts cannot change | Compile-time restriction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "explain runtime polymorphism" in a 14-mark question, examiners expect all three concepts woven together: overriding (definition), DMD (mechanism), and an explanation of *why* Java supports this and *how* `final` can disable it.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Method Overriding — Rules of the Contract

The Java compiler enforces a strict set of rules whenever a subclass attempts to override an inherited method. Violating any of these rules results in a **compile-time error**.

### Operational Breakdown

- **Identical Signature** — The overriding method must have the *exact* same name and *exact* same parameter list (including order and types) as the parent method. Changing the parameter list creates an *overload*, not an *override*.
- **Covariant Return Type (Java 5+)** — The return type of the overriding method can be a *subclass* of the parent's return type. For example, if the parent returns `Object`, the child may return `String`.
- **Access Modifier Cannot Be Narrowed** — If the parent declares a method as `public`, the child *cannot* declare it as `protected` or `private`. However, it can be *widened* (e.g., parent `protected` → child `public`).
- **Instance Methods Only** — `static` methods are *hidden*, not overridden. `private` methods are neither inherited nor overridden.
- **No New Checked Exceptions** — The overriding method cannot throw *new or broader* checked exceptions, but it can throw *fewer* or *narrower* ones.
- **Invoking the Parent Version** — Use `super.methodName()` from within the subclass to explicitly call the parent class's overridden version.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these rules?** They preserve the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** — a child class must be *behaviorally substitutable* for its parent. If overriding could narrow access or throw a `SQLException` where the parent threw none, polymorphism would break client code.

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Method Dispatch — The JVM Resolution Process

Dynamic Method Dispatch is not magic; it is a concrete, well-defined algorithm executed by the JVM at runtime.

### Operational Breakdown (The "How" and "Why")

1. **Bytecode Generation** — When the compiler encounters a method call on a reference variable, it generates an *invokevirtual* bytecode instruction (for instance methods) rather than a direct call.
2. **Reference vs. Object** — The *reference variable's* declared type determines **which methods are *visible*** (compile-time check). The *actual object's* runtime class determines **which method body *executes***.
3. **Method Table Lookup (vtable)** — Every class loaded by the JVM has an internal **virtual method table (vtable)**. When a method is called, the JVM fetches the object's class, walks up the inheritance chain in the vtable, and finds the *first matching* method signature.
4. **Upcasting Prerequisite** — For DMD to be *meaningful*, the reference must be of a parent type pointing to a child object. Otherwise, there is no ambiguity to resolve.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In large-scale **MVC frameworks** (Spring, Struts) and **plugin architectures**, DMD is the silent engine that lets a framework invoke *user-defined* code without knowing the user's class names in advance. The framework defines a base `Controller` class; users subclass it; the framework's dispatcher calls `controller.handleRequest()` — and the correct user's logic runs.

---

## 2.3 Using `final` with Inheritance — Three Lock-Down Modes

### Operational Breakdown

- **`final` Class** — Declared as `final class MyClass { ... }`. The compiler **rejects** any `extends MyClass`. Examples: `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.Math`, all wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Double`). This guarantees *immutability* and *security* (critical for `String` because it is used as a HashMap key).
- **`final` Method** — Declared as `final void display() { ... }`. Subclasses can still inherit it, but they **cannot override** it. Used to *template-method* design where the base class behavior must not be altered.
- **`final` Variable** — Declared as `final int MAX = 100;`. The value **cannot be reassigned**. Effectively a compile-time constant when initialized inline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inverse Relationship:** `final` *prevents* what overriding *enables*. By marking a method `final`, you are telling the JVM: *"Do not perform dynamic method dispatch for this method — the binding is fixed at compile time."* This is a micro-optimization because the JVM can skip the vtable lookup.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Security:** `String` is `final` to prevent malicious subclasses from overriding `equals()` or `hashCode()` and breaking hash-based collections.
- **API Stability:** Library designers use `final` to lock critical algorithms, ensuring client code cannot break internal invariants.
- **Performance:** `final` methods allow the JIT compiler to perform aggressive inlining.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-READY TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS FOR KTU 2024 ESE**

| Feature | Method Overriding | Method Overloading | `final` Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **When Resolved** | Runtime | Compile-time | Compile-time (locked) |
| **Where Defined** | Across Superclass ↔ Subclass | Within the same class | Inherited by subclass |
| **Signature** | Must be **identical** | Must **differ** in parameters | N/A — defined once |
| **Return Type** | Must be **same or covariant** | Can differ freely | N/A |
| **Access Modifier** | Cannot be **narrowed** | Can differ freely | N/A |
| **Polymorphism Type** | Runtime (Dynamic) | Compile-time (Static) | Disables polymorphism |
| **Keyword Used** | `@Override` annotation (optional) | None required | `final` modifier |
| **`static` Allowed?** | No (static methods are *hidden*) | Yes | Yes (final static is a constant) |
| **DMD Enabled?** | Yes | No | No |

### Summary of Compile-Time vs. Runtime Binding

| Binding Phase | Trigger | Java Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Compile-time (Early Binding)** | Method overloading, `static` methods, `private` methods, `final` methods | Direct invocation by compiler |
| **Runtime (Late Binding)** | Overridden instance methods called via superclass reference | Dynamic Method Dispatch via JVM vtable |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Demonstrations

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Method Overriding — Full Java Demonstration

The following Java program demonstrates **method overriding** with all the rules in action. Every step is annotated for KTU 2024 board-level clarity.

### Step 1 — Establish the Superclass

```java
// Superclass definition
class Animal {
    String name;

    Animal(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    // Method to be overridden
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println(name + " makes a generic animal sound.");
    }

    // Final method — cannot be overridden
    public final void breathe() {
        System.out.println(name + " is breathing.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The `Animal` class is a generic blueprint. It defines a `speak()` method meant to be specialized, and a `breathe()` method marked `final` to *lock* the behavior — no subclass can change how an animal breathes.

### Step 2 — Create Two Subclasses That Override `speak()`

```java
class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog(String name) {
        super(name); // Call parent constructor
    }

    @Override
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println(name + " says: Woof!");
    }
}

class Cat extends Animal {
    Cat(String name) {
        super(name);
    }

    @Override
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println(name + " says: Meow!");
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** Each subclass provides its *own* version of `speak()`. The `@Override` annotation is **optional but highly recommended** — it instructs the compiler to verify that overriding actually occurs, catching signature typos.

### Step 3 — Invoke Dynamic Method Dispatch

```java
public class PolymorphismDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Upcasting: Parent reference, Child object
        Animal myPet;

        myPet = new Dog("Buddy");
        myPet.speak();   // Output: Buddy says: Woof!
        myPet.breathe(); // Output: Buddy is breathing.

        myPet = new Cat("Whiskers");
        myPet.speak();   // Output: Whiskers says: Meow!
        myPet.breathe(); // Output: Whiskers is breathing.
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Logic:**

1. At **compile time**, the compiler verifies that `speak()` exists on the `Animal` reference type. ✓
2. At **runtime**, the JVM checks the *actual* object — first a `Dog`, then a `Cat` — and dispatches the correct `speak()`.
3. The `breathe()` method is `final`, so it is **statically bound**. No DMD is needed; the parent's version always runs.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: The "Why DMD Fails Without Upcasting" Scenario

The following is a critical KTU-favorite counter-example. Read carefully — this is a **common board question**.

### Demonstration

```java
class Shape {
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic Shape.");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle.");
    }
}

class TestBinding {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();   // Direct reference — NO upcasting
        c.draw();                   // Output: Drawing a Circle.
    }
}
```

**Analytical Commentary:**

- Here, the reference type (`Circle`) and the object type (`Circle`) are **identical**. There is *no ambiguity*, so DMD is technically happening under the hood, but it is **observationally indistinguishable** from compile-time binding.
- The **power** of DMD emerges only when the reference type *differs* from the object type — i.e., when upcasting is used. This is the scenario in Section 3.1.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: `final` Enforcement in Action

### Demonstration

```java
final class Vehicle {
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle starting.");
    }
}

// The following line, if uncommented, causes a COMPILE ERROR:
// class Car extends Vehicle { }   // ERROR: Cannot inherit from final 'Vehicle'

class Account {
    final double MIN_BALANCE = 500.0; // Final variable — constant

    void checkBalance() {
        // The following line, if uncommented, causes a COMPILE ERROR:
        // MIN_BALANCE = 1000.0;   // ERROR: cannot assign a value to final variable
        System.out.println("Minimum balance: " + MIN_BALANCE);
    }
}
```

**Analytical Commentary:**

- The compiler rejects the inheritance of `Vehicle` outright. This is **compile-time enforcement** — the restriction is permanent and absolute.
- The `MIN_BALANCE` field becomes a *compile-time constant* when initialized inline, allowing the JVM to inline its value for performance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is the **highest-yield summary** for a 14-mark KTU question. It captures all distinguishing features in a single table.

| Dimension | Method Overriding | Dynamic Method Dispatch | `final` with Inheritance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Provide subclass-specific behavior | Decide *which* overridden method runs | *Prevent* overriding/inheritance |
| **Where it lives** | In source code (subclass) | In JVM runtime engine | In source code (modifier) |
| **Triggered by** | Subclass defining same-signature method | Virtual method call on supertype reference | `final` keyword in declaration |
| **Binding Time** | N/A (definition is static) | **Runtime** | **Compile-time lock** |
| **Polymorphism Type** | Runtime polymorphism | The mechanism *enabling* runtime polymorphism | **Disables** polymorphism |
| **Performance Impact** | Slight overhead (vtable lookup) | Slight overhead (vtable lookup) | **Optimization** (JIT can inline) |
| **Real Example** | `equals()` overridden in `String` | `Map.put()` calling `hashCode()` on `Object` reference | `String` class being `final` |
| **Can be disabled by** | Making method `static`, `private`, or `final` | Upcasting not used | N/A (it is the disabler) |

### Conceptual Equation of the Trinity

$$
\text{Runtime Polymorphism} = \text{Method Overriding} \times \text{Dynamic Method Dispatch}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{If } \texttt{final} \text{ is applied, then } \text{Runtime Polymorphism} = 0}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the multiplication?** Because *both* conditions are required simultaneously for runtime polymorphism to manifest. If overriding is absent, DMD has nothing to resolve. If DMD is absent (e.g., method is `static` or `final`), the parent's version always runs. Both factors must be *non-zero*.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram — Inheritance with Overriding

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a classic KTU-favorite inheritance hierarchy where `Animal` is the superclass and `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Cow` override the `speak()` method.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        -String name
        +speak() void
        +breathe() final void
        +eat() void
    }
    class Dog {
        +speak() void
        +fetch() void
    }
    class Cat {
        +speak() void
        +scratch() void
    }
    class Cow {
        +speak() void
    }
    Animal <|-- Dog : extends
    Animal <|-- Cat : extends
    Animal <|-- Cow : extends
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The arrow `<|--` denotes inheritance (the open-triangle arrow points from subclass to superclass).
- `breathe()` is marked `final` in `Animal`; therefore, `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Cow` inherit it but **cannot override it**.
- `speak()` is overridden in every subclass, providing type-specific behavior.

---

## 4.2 Dynamic Method Dispatch — Runtime Resolution Flow

The following flowchart depicts the **logical flow** the JVM follows when it encounters an overridden method call.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Method call encountered: ref.speak] --> B{Is speak static, private, or final?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Static Binding: Direct call to ref's class version]
    B -- No --> D[Dynamic Method Dispatch Activated]
    D --> E[Inspect actual runtime object type]
    E --> F[Search object's class vtable for speak]
    F --> G{Found in subclass?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Invoke subclass overridden version]
    G -- No --> I[Walk up to parent class in vtable]
    I --> J{Parent has speak?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Invoke parent version]
    J -- No --> L[COMPILE ERROR: method not found]
```

**Operational Reading:**
- The JVM's *first* decision is whether DMD is even *necessary*. If the method is `static`, `private`, or `final`, the compiler already knows the target at compile time.
- For instance methods, the JVM consults the **vtable** — a per-class dispatch table populated at class-loading time.

---

## 4.3 `final` Keyword — Three Lock-Down Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F1[Final Class]
        FC1[Cannot be extended] --> FC2[Example: String, Math]
    end
    subgraph F2[Final Method]
        FM1[Cannot be overridden] --> FM2[Inherited as-is by subclasses]
    end
    subgraph F3[Final Variable]
        FV1[Cannot be reassigned] --> FV2[Acts as constant]
    end
    style F1 fill:#f9e,stroke:#333
    style F2 fill:#bef,stroke:#333
    style F3 fill:#feb,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each `final` mode applies to a different *element* of the class.
- The visual grouping clarifies that `final` is a **single keyword with three distinct semantic effects** depending on where it is placed.

---

## 4.4 Runtime Polymorphism — End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Source Code: Animal ref = new Dog] --> COMPILE[Compiler: verifies speak exists on Animal]
    COMPILE --> BYTECODE[Bytecode: invokevirtual speak]
    BYTECODE --> JVM{JVM Runtime Engine}
    JVM --> CLASSLOAD[ClassLoader loads Dog.class and Animal.class]
    CLASSLOAD --> VTABLE[Build vtable for Dog: speak resolves to Dog.speak]
    VTABLE --> EXECUTE[Invoke Dog.speak at runtime]
    EXECUTE --> OUTPUT[Output: Woof!]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- This end-to-end view ties the **compile-time** verification to the **runtime** dispatch.
- The `invokevirtual` bytecode instruction is the *bridge* between the two phases.
- The vtable is built *once* at class loading, not on every call — making DMD efficient despite being "dynamic."
