# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding** — Overloading is *same name, different parameters, in the same class*. Overriding is *same signature, across superclass-subclass*. Examiners deduct heavily for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation in code answers** — Examiners award partial credit for demonstrating awareness of the annotation. Omitting it is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing `static` methods as overrides** — Static methods are *hidden*, not overridden. Writing `static` in an "overriding" example is an automatic 2-mark cut.
> 4. **Missing the `super.methodName()` concept** — When asked to call the parent version, students often forget `super.` syntax.
> 5. **Confusing `final` class with `final` method** — `final` class prevents inheritance entirely; `final` method only prevents *overriding*, not inheritance. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Method Overriding. List any two rules that must be followed while overriding a method in Java.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Method Overriding is a feature of Java in which a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its parent (superclass). It is the foundation of runtime polymorphism and is resolved via dynamic method dispatch at runtime.

**Two Rules (0.75 Marks each):**
1. The overriding method must have the **same name and same parameter list** as the parent method. The return type must be the same or covariant (a subclass of the parent's return type).
2. The overriding method **cannot have a more restrictive access modifier** than the parent method. For example, if the parent method is `public`, the child cannot declare it as `protected` or `private`.

> [!NOTE]
> A third valid rule for extra credit: The overriding method cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions.

---

### Question 2: What is Dynamic Method Dispatch? Why is it called "dynamic"?
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD) is the runtime mechanism by which the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) determines which version of an overridden method to execute, based on the *actual runtime type* of the object rather than the *declared type* of the reference variable. It is enabled when a superclass reference variable points to a subclass object (upcasting) and the subclass has overridden the inherited method.

**Why "Dynamic" (1 Mark):** It is called *dynamic* because the method-to-actual-code binding occurs **at runtime**, not at compile time. The compiler cannot determine which version will run; only the JVM, while the program executes, can resolve the correct method body using the object's vtable.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has an internal choice — the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both are provided below for practice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Method Overriding** in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules that must be followed for valid method overriding. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Dynamic Method Dispatch**? Write a Java program to demonstrate how a superclass reference variable can be used to call overridden methods of different subclass objects. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Method Overriding (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** Method Overriding is a mechanism in Java where a subclass redefines a method inherited from its superclass, providing a specialized implementation. The method name, return type (or covariant return type), and parameter list must match the parent method exactly. Overriding is the cornerstone of runtime polymorphism.

**Rules (3 Marks — list with brief explanation):**
1. **Same Signature:** The overriding method must have the identical name and parameter list. *(0.75 Marks)*
2. **Access Level:** Cannot be more restrictive. Widening is allowed. *(0.75 Marks)*
3. **No New Checked Exceptions:** The overriding method cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions. *(0.5 Marks)*
4. **Instance Methods Only:** Static methods are hidden, not overridden. Private methods are neither inherited nor overridden. *(0.5 Marks)*
5. **Covariant Return Type:** The return type can be a subclass of the parent's return type (Java 5+ feature). *(0.5 Marks)*

**Example Code (2 Marks):**

```java
class Bank {
    double rateOfInterest() {
        return 5.0;
    }
}

class SBI extends Bank {
    @Override
    double rateOfInterest() {
        return 6.5;
    }
}

class ICICI extends Bank {
    @Override
    double rateOfInterest() {
        return 7.0;
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** `SBI` and `ICICI` both override `rateOfInterest()` from `Bank`, providing bank-specific interest rates.

---

#### Part (b) — Dynamic Method Dispatch (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the runtime mechanism by which the JVM resolves calls to overridden methods. The JVM examines the *actual runtime type* of the object that a reference variable points to, and dispatches the corresponding overridden method version. This enables runtime polymorphism.

**Java Program (4 Marks):**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape.");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle.");
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Square.");
    }
}

public class DispatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s; // Superclass reference

        s = new Circle();
        s.draw(); // Output: Drawing a Circle.

        s = new Square();
        s.draw(); // Output: Drawing a Square.

        s = new Shape();
        s.draw(); // Output: Drawing a generic shape.
    }
}
```

**Output (1 Mark):**
```
Drawing a Circle.
Drawing a Square.
Drawing a generic shape.
```

**Conclusion (Included for full marks):** The reference variable `s` is of type `Shape`, but each time it is reassigned to a different object, the JVM dynamically dispatches the *correct* `draw()` version at runtime. This is the essence of dynamic method dispatch.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain how the **`final` keyword** is used with inheritance in Java. Discuss its use with **classes**, **methods**, and **variables** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Method Overriding** and **Method Overloading** in Java. Why is method overriding considered essential for runtime polymorphism? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — `final` Keyword with Inheritance (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):** The `final` keyword in Java is a non-access modifier used to *restrict* further modification. In the context of inheritance, it can be applied to classes, methods, and variables to lock their behavior.

**Final Class (2 Marks):** A class declared as `final` cannot be extended by any subclass. This is used to prevent inheritance of sensitive or immutable classes.

```java
final class SecurityToken {
    // ...
}

// This causes a compile error:
// class SuperToken extends SecurityToken { } // ERROR
```

**Final Method (2 Marks):** A method declared as `final` cannot be overridden by any subclass, though it can be inherited. This locks the implementation.

```java
class Database {
    public final void connect() {
        System.out.println("Connecting securely.");
    }
}
```

**Final Variable (2 Marks):** A variable declared as `final` becomes a constant; its value cannot be reassigned after initialization.

```java
class Config {
    final int MAX_CONNECTIONS = 100;
    // MAX_CONNECTIONS = 200;  // COMPILE ERROR
}
```

---

#### Part (b) — Overriding vs. Overloading + Role in Polymorphism (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | Method Overriding | Method Overloading |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Redefining parent method in subclass | Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists |
| **Location** | Across two classes (parent-child) | Within the same class |
| **Signature** | Must be **identical** | Must **differ** in parameters |
| **Binding** | Runtime (Dynamic) | Compile-time (Static) |
| **Polymorphism** | Runtime polymorphism | Compile-time polymorphism |
| **`@Override` annotation** | Recommended | Not applicable |

**Why Overriding is Essential for Runtime Polymorphism (3 Marks):**
1. **Behavior Specialization:** Overriding allows each subclass to express its own unique behavior for the same method signature. *(1 Mark)*
2. **Substitutability:** A parent reference can point to any subclass object, and the JVM invokes the *correct* overridden method via dynamic method dispatch. *(1 Mark)*
3. **Open/Closed Principle:** Overriding lets us *extend* functionality without modifying the parent class — a key software engineering principle. *(1 Mark)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

### 🎯 Core Definitions
- **Method Overriding:** Subclass provides specific implementation of a method already defined in the superclass; requires identical signature.
- **Dynamic Method Dispatch:** Runtime JVM mechanism that resolves calls to overridden methods based on the actual object type.
- **`final` Keyword:** Non-access modifier that prevents inheritance (class), overriding (method), or reassignment (variable).

### 🎯 Must-Know Rules
- Overriding requires **same name, same parameter list, covariant return type**.
- Overriding **cannot narrow access** — it can only widen.
- `static`, `private`, and `final` methods **cannot be overridden** (they are bound at compile time).
- The overriding method **cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions**.
- Use `super.methodName()` to invoke the parent class's overridden version.

### 🎯 Key Conditions for DMD
1. **Upcasting:** Parent reference → Child object.
2. **Overriding:** The child must have overridden the method.
3. **Instance Method:** Only instance methods participate in DMD.

### 🎯 The `final` Trifecta
- `final class` → cannot be **extended**.
- `final method` → cannot be **overridden** (but can be inherited).
- `final variable` → cannot be **reassigned** (becomes a constant).

### 🎯 Common KTU Confusions (AVOID)
- ❌ Confusing *overloading* (compile-time) with *overriding* (runtime).
- ❌ Saying that `static` methods can be overridden — they are *hidden*, not overridden.
- ❌ Forgetting that `private` methods are *not inherited*, hence not overridden.
- ❌ Forgetting the `@Override` annotation in code-based answers.

### 🎯 Memory Aid — "OVR-FIN"
| Letter | Stands For |
|---|---|
| **O** | Overriding requires same signature |
| **V** | Variables become constants |
| **R** | Return type must be same or covariant |
| **F** | Final locks down |
| **I** | Instance methods only for overriding |
| **N** | Narrowing access is not allowed |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packages and Interfaces - Packages - Defining a Package, CLASSPATH, Access Protection, Importing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages and Interfaces - Packages - Defining a Package, CLASSPATH, Access Protection, Importing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Package** in Java is a *namespace* that organizes a set of related *classes*, *interfaces*, *enumerations*, and *sub-packages*. It functions as a **container** or **logical grouping mechanism** that prevents naming conflicts, controls access (encapsulation at the directory level), and enables easier location and use of classes. Conceptually, a package is a **folder/directory hierarchy** mapped to the underlying file system.

A package provides **four core functionalities** as mandated by the KTU OECST615 syllabus:
1. **Logical Grouping** of related types
2. **Access Protection** (visibility control)
3. **Name Space Management** (avoiding class-name collisions)
4. **Code Reusability** through `import` directives

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Library Filing System**
> Imagine a large university library. If all books were dumped into a single giant room without shelves, finding *Java: The Complete Reference* would be near impossible. Instead, the librarian uses a **standardized categorization system**:
> - **Building** = Package (`java.util`, `java.io`)
> - **Floor** = Sub-package (`java.util.concurrent`)
> - **Shelf** = Class (`ArrayList`, `HashMap`)
> - **Cabinet/Box** = Access modifier (`public`, `private`, `protected`, *default*)
>
> A student (the JVM) finds a book (class) by knowing the **building address** (`CLASSPATH`) and the **catalog number** (`import statement`).

### 1.3 Key Technical Constants & Conventions

- **Package names are written in all lowercase** to avoid conflict with class names (a KTU 2024 coding standard).
- The **Java reserved keyword** for creating a package is `package`.
- The **fully qualified name (FQN)** of a class is the concatenation of its package path with the class name, separated by dots (e.g., `java.util.ArrayList`).
- A compilation unit (`.java` file) can contain **at most one `package` statement**, and it must be the **first non-comment, non-whitespace statement** in the source file.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Java Package Prefixes (Industry Standard)**
> - `java.*` — Core Java API (mandatory)
> - `javax.*` — Extensions / Standard extensions
> - `org.*` — Non-profit organizations
> - `com.*` — Commercial organizations (e.g., `com.ktu.university`)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorization of Packages

Packages are classified into two broad categories mandated by the KTU syllabus:

#### A. Built-in (Pre-defined) Packages
These ship with the Java Development Kit (JDK). Key packages include:

| Package Name | Purpose | High-Yield Classes |
|---|---|---|
| `java.lang` | Fundamental language classes (auto-imported) | `String`, `Math`, `System`, `Object`, `Thread` |
| `java.util` | Utility framework (collections, date/time) | `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Scanner`, `Date` |
| `java.io` | Input/Output streams | `File`, `FileReader`, `BufferedReader` |
| `java.net` | Networking operations | `Socket`, `URL`, `InetAddress` |
| `java.awt` | Abstract Window Toolkit (GUI) | `Button`, `Frame`, `Color` |
| `java.sql` | Database connectivity (JDBC) | `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Note on `java.lang`**
> The `java.lang` package is **implicitly imported** into every Java program. You never need to write `import java.lang.*;`. This is a frequently tested **2-mark KTU question**.

#### B. User-defined Packages
These are packages created by the programmer to organize project-specific classes.

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Package Mechanics

#### Pillar 1 — Defining a Package
- A package is defined using the `package` keyword.
- The directory structure of the file system **must mirror** the package name.
- Example: `package com.ktu.student;` requires the file to reside in the path `com/ktu/student/`.

#### Pillar 2 — CLASSPATH
- **CLASSPATH** is an **environment variable** (or compiler flag) that tells the **JDK tool** (e.g., `javac`, `java`) and the **JVM ClassLoader** *where to find user-defined and third-party package class files (`.class` files)*.
- The default CLASSPATH is the **current directory (`.`)**.
- Syntax: `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myjava\lib;D:\projects\bin`
- The separator on Windows is `;` and on Linux/macOS is `:`.

#### Pillar 3 — Access Protection
- Packages provide the **fourth tier of access control** (the other three are class, method, and block).
- A class has only two valid top-level access states: `public` or *default (package-private)*.
- Members within a class have four states: `public`, `protected`, *default*, and `private`.

#### Pillar 4 — Importing Packages
- The `import` statement enables the compiler to locate classes without typing their FQN repeatedly.
- Three valid forms:
  1. `import package_name.*;` (imports all classes in the package — *does NOT import sub-packages*)
  2. `import package_name.ClassName;` (imports a specific class)
  3. *No import* — use the FQN directly, e.g., `java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList();`

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Access Protection Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table — KTU Access Modifier Visibility Within and Across Packages**

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Subclass/Non-subclass) | Subclass in Different Package | Different Package (Non-subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | Yes | No | No | No |
| *default* (no keyword) | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| `protected` | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

Use `Yes` for accessible and `No` for inaccessible. The cell for `protected` access in a *different package subclass* is the most **frequently tested** nuance in KTU exams.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Banking Systems**: Use `com.bank.accounts`, `com.bank.transactions`, `com.bank.audit` to enforce architectural boundaries.
- **Android Development (Google)**: All Android apps are organized under `com.companyname.appname` package structures.
- **Modular Compilation**: Maven/Gradle build tools rely entirely on `package` declarations to map source code to JAR artifacts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Convention Used Below**
> For each procedural concept, we use a *Problem Statement → Setup → Execution → Verification* analytical matrix. All Java code blocks are fully expanded line-by-line to satisfy the KTU 14-mark long-answer valuation pattern.

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Defining and Accessing a User-Defined Package

**Problem Statement**
Create a class `Calculator` inside the package `com.ktu.mathutils`. Access this class from another class `TestCalc` residing in the default (unnamed) package, and display the result of `10 + 20`.

#### Step 1 — Directory Structure (Physical Mapping)
The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the file system mirrors the package hierarchy. Create the following directory tree:

```
ProjectRoot/
└── com/
    └── ktu/
        └── mathutils/
            └── Calculator.java
└── TestCalc.java
```

#### Step 2 — Define the Package (Source File 1: `Calculator.java`)

```java
// File: com/ktu/mathutils/Calculator.java
// The 'package' statement MUST be the first non-comment line.
package com.ktu.mathutils;

// A public class is mandatory so that other packages can import and instantiate it.
public class Calculator {

    // Public method to be accessible across packages.
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
}
```

**Conversion Logic of Step 2**
- `package com.ktu.mathutils;` instructs the compiler to compile the resulting `.class` bytecode into the `com/ktu/mathutils/` directory.
- The class is declared `public` to permit cross-package visibility (otherwise, only package-private access is allowed).

#### Step 3 — Import and Use the Class (Source File 2: `TestCalc.java`)

```java
// File: TestCalc.java (in ProjectRoot, the default package)

// Import the specific class from the user-defined package.
import com.ktu.mathutils.Calculator;

public class TestCalc {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Instantiate the imported class.
        Calculator calc = new Calculator();

        // Call the public method and display the result.
        int result = calc.add(10, 20);
        System.out.println("Sum = " + result);
    }
}
```

**Conversion Logic of Step 3**
- `import com.ktu.mathutils.Calculator;` allows us to use the short name `Calculator` instead of the FQN.
- The `main` method is the JVM entry point; the addition is performed and printed as `Sum = 30`.

#### Step 4 — Compilation and Execution (Terminal Commands)

```bash
# Step A: Compile Calculator.java — the -d flag specifies destination directory
javac -d . com/ktu/mathutils/Calculator.java

# Step B: Compile TestCalc.java
javac TestCalc.java

# Step C: Execute the program
java TestCalc
```

**Output**
```
Sum = 30
```

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — The CLASSPATH Mechanism in Action

**Problem Statement**
Demonstrate how a CLASSPATH environment variable allows a Java program in `C:\ProjectA` to use a compiled class file located in `C:\SharedLib`.

#### Step 1 — Setup the Shared Library Directory

Create `C:\SharedLib\com\ktu\mathutils\Calculator.class` (pre-compiled artifact from Problem 1).

#### Step 2 — Set the CLASSPATH Environment Variable

On **Windows CMD**:
```cmd
set CLASSPATH=.;C:\SharedLib
```

On **Linux/macOS Terminal**:
```bash
export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/SharedLib
```

**Conversion Logic**
- The dot (`.`) represents the **current working directory** — the default first lookup location.
- The second path `C:\SharedLib` is appended so the JVM can resolve the package `com.ktu.mathutils` by searching for `com/ktu/mathutils/Calculator.class` *inside* `C:\SharedLib`.

#### Step 3 — Resolution Algorithm of the JVM ClassLoader

$$
\text{JVM Class Lookup} = \text{BOOTSTRAP} \rightarrow \text{EXTENSION} \rightarrow \text{USER (CLASSPATH)}
$$

**Step-by-step resolution:**
1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** searches `rt.jar` (core Java classes).
2. **Extension ClassLoader** searches `jre/lib/ext/`.
3. **Application ClassLoader** iterates through the CLASSPATH entries in order.
4. The package `com.ktu.mathutils` is converted to the relative path `com\ktu\mathutils\`.
5. The relative path is **appended to each CLASSPATH entry** until the `.class` file is found.
6. If the class is found, it is **loaded into method memory** and verified.

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Access Protection Across Packages

**Problem Statement**
Demonstrate that a `protected` member of a class in `package1` is accessible by a subclass located in `package2`.

#### Step 1 — Source File: `BaseClass.java` in `package1`

```java
package package1;

public class BaseClass {
    // Protected field — visible to subclasses anywhere
    protected int data = 100;

    // Protected method
    protected void display() {
        System.out.println("Data = " + data);
    }
}
```

#### Step 2 — Source File: `DerivedClass.java` in `package2`

```java
package package2;

// Import the BaseClass from package1
import package1.BaseClass;

// DerivedClass EXTENDS BaseClass, establishing inheritance relationship
public class DerivedClass extends BaseClass {

    public void showInherited() {
        // Accessing protected member of superclass from a different package
        System.out.println("Inherited Data = " + data);
        display(); // Calling inherited protected method
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        DerivedClass obj = new DerivedClass();
        obj.showInherited();
    }
}
```

**Output**
```
Inherited Data = 100
Data = 100
```

**Conversion Logic**
- The subclass `DerivedClass` in `package2` inherits the `protected` field `data` and method `display()` from `BaseClass` in `package1`.
- This access is granted *only because of the `extends` relationship*; a non-subclass in `package2` would receive a **compilation error**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Import Strategies

| Import Strategy | Syntax | When Used in KTU Examinations |
|---|---|---|
| Single-class import | `import java.util.ArrayList;` | Preferred for clarity (most common in 14-mark answers) |
| On-demand (wildcard) import | `import java.util.*;` | When multiple classes from same package are needed |
| Fully qualified name (no import) | `java.util.ArrayList list = ...;` | Resolves name conflicts between two classes of the same name |
| Static import | `import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;` | Allows `sqrt(x)` without `Math.` prefix |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Java Package Directory Tree (Hierarchical Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["JAVA PROJECT ROOT"]
    root --> langPkg["java.lang"]
    root --> utilPkg["java.util"]
    root --> ioPkg["java.io"]
    root --> userPkg1["com.ktu.mathutils"]
    root --> userPkg2["com.ktu.students"]

    langPkg --> langCls1["String.class"]
    langPkg --> langCls2["Math.class"]
    langPkg --> langCls3["System.class"]

    utilPkg --> utilCls1["ArrayList.class"]
    utilPkg --> utilCls2["HashMap.class"]
    utilPkg --> utilCls3["Scanner.class"]

    ioPkg --> ioCls1["File.class"]
    ioPkg --> ioCls2["BufferedReader.class"]

    userPkg1 --> u1Cls1["Calculator.class"]
    userPkg1 --> u1Cls2["Geometry.class"]

    userPkg2 --> u2Cls1["StudentRecord.class"]
    userPkg2 --> u2Cls2["ResultProcessor.class"]

    style root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    style ioPkg fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style userPkg1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style userPkg2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — CLASSPATH Resolution Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source File TestCalc.java] --> B{Javac Compiler Invoked}
    B --> C[Reads import com.ktu.mathutils.Calculator]
    C --> D{Class Search Initiated}
    D --> E[Step 1: Check Bootstrap ClassLoader rt.jar]
    D --> F[Step 2: Check Extension ClassLoader jre/lib/ext]
    D --> G[Step 3: Iterate CLASSPATH entries]
    G --> G1[Entry 1: Current Directory .]
    G --> G2[Entry 2: C:SharedLib]
    G --> G3[Entry 3: Additional paths]

    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Class com.ktu.mathutils.Calculator.class Loaded]
    I --> J[JVM Executes main method]
    H --> K[Error: ClassNotFoundException]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Access Protection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Member Access Request] --> Q1{Same Class?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Allow1[ALLOWED]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Same Package?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Modifier Type?}
    Q3 -- private --> Deny1[DENIED]
    Q3 -- default --> Allow2[ALLOWED]
    Q3 -- protected --> Allow3[ALLOWED]
    Q3 -- public --> Allow4[ALLOWED]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Subclass in Different Package?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Modifier Type?}
    Q5 -- private --> Deny2[DENIED]
    Q5 -- default --> Deny3[DENIED]
    Q5 -- protected --> Allow5[ALLOWED via Inheritance]
    Q5 -- public --> Allow6[ALLOWED]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Modifier Type?}
    Q6 -- private --> Deny4[DENIED]
    Q6 -- default --> Deny5[DENIED]
    Q6 -- protected --> Deny6[DENIED]
    Q6 -- public --> Allow7[ALLOWED]

    style Allow1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Allow2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Allow3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style Allow5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Allow6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Allow7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Deny1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Deny2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Deny3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Deny4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Deny5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Deny6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
> *What is a package in Java? List any two built-in packages with their purpose.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A package in Java is a namespace that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages. It is a directory structure that prevents naming conflicts and controls access.
- **Two built-in packages (1 mark):**
  1. `java.lang` — Contains fundamental classes like `String`, `Math`, `Object` (auto-imported).
  2. `java.util` — Contains the collections framework and utility classes like `ArrayList`, `Scanner`, `Date`.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> *Explain the role of the `import` statement in Java. Can we import a sub-package using `import package.*`? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**
- **Role of import (1 mark):** The `import` statement makes classes of a package accessible by their short names without typing the fully qualified name.
- **Syntax example (1 mark):** `import java.util.ArrayList;`
- **Sub-package justification (1 mark):** **No**, `import package.*;` imports only the classes *directly* inside `package` and **does NOT** import sub-packages. To import a sub-package, we must use its full name, e.g., `import java.util.concurrent.*;`.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
> *(a) [7 Marks]* Explain the concept of CLASSPATH in Java. Describe how the JVM resolves a class using CLASSPATH with a suitable example.
> *(b) [7 Marks]* Write a Java program to create a user-defined package `com.ktu.banking` containing a class `Account` with methods `deposit()` and `balanceEnquiry()`. Demonstrate the use of this package from a different class.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — CLASSPATH Explanation [7 Marks]**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** CLASSPATH is an environment variable that provides the path(s) where the Java compiler (`javac`) and JVM (`java`) search for user-defined and third-party `.class` files.

2. **Setting CLASSPATH (2 marks):**
   - Windows: `set CLASSPATH=.;D:\myjava`
   - Linux/macOS: `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/myjava`
   - The dot `.` denotes the current directory.

3. **Resolution Algorithm (3 marks):**
   - The JVM consults three ClassLoaders in order: Bootstrap, Extension, and Application.
   - The package name (e.g., `com.ktu.banking`) is converted to a directory path (`com/ktu/banking/`).
   - This relative path is appended to each CLASSPATH entry until a match is found.
   - If not found, a `ClassNotFoundException` is thrown at runtime.

**Part (b) — User-Defined Package Program [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Directory Structure (1 mark)**
```
BankingProject/
├── com/
│   └── ktu/
│       └── banking/
│           └── Account.java
└── BankTest.java
```

**Step 2: Account.java (3 marks)**
```java
package com.ktu.banking;

public class Account {
    private double balance = 0.0;

    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
        }
    }

    public void balanceEnquiry() {
        System.out.println("Current Balance: " + balance);
    }
}
```

**Step 3: BankTest.java (2 marks)**
```java
import com.ktu.banking.Account;

public class BankTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account acc = new Account();
        acc.deposit(5000.0);
        acc.balanceEnquiry();
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Compilation and Output (1 mark)**
```bash
javac -d . com/ktu/banking/Account.java
javac BankTest.java
java BankTest
```
**Output:**
```
Deposited: 5000.0
Current Balance: 5000.0
```

---

> **Q3(b). [ALTERNATIVE — KTU University Exam - May 2024, 14 Marks]**
> *(a) [7 Marks]* Explain access protection in Java packages with a neat access modifier visibility table.
> *(b) [7 Marks]* Differentiate between `import package.ClassName;` and `import package.*;`. Illustrate with a Java program how name conflicts are resolved.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Access Protection [7 Marks]**

Access protection in Java packages governs the visibility of classes and their members across package boundaries. There are four access levels: `public`, `protected`, *default (package-private)*, and `private`.

**Visibility Table (5 marks):**

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Different Package) | Other Packages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | Yes | No | No | No |
| *default* | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| `protected` | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

Use `Yes` for accessible and `No` for inaccessible. The cell marked **Yes** for `protected` in a *different package subclass* is the key KTU 2024 distinction.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Packages act as a *fourth encapsulation tier* that supplements class-level encapsulation. Designers use `public` for the API surface, `protected` for extension points, *default* for package-internal helpers, and `private` for implementation details.

**Part (b) — Import Differentiations [7 Marks]**

| Aspect | `import package.ClassName;` | `import package.*;` |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Single specific class | All classes in the package |
| Compilation Time | Faster (compiler knows exact target) | Slightly slower (broader scan) |
| Code Clarity | Explicit and readable | Less explicit |
| Name Conflict Risk | Lower | Higher if two wildcards introduce same class name |

**Resolving Name Conflicts (3 marks):** When two imported classes share the same simple name, the compiler throws an *ambiguous reference* error. The fix is to drop the wildcard import and use the **Fully Qualified Name (FQN)** for the conflicting class:

```java
import java.util.Date;
// import java.sql.*;  // COMMENTED OUT to avoid conflict with java.util.Date

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // FQN used to disambiguate java.sql.Date
        java.sql.Date sqlDate = new java.sql.Date(System.currentTimeMillis());
        System.out.println("SQL Date: " + sqlDate);
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse `protected` with `default`. Remember: `default` is **NEVER** accessible to a subclass in a different package; only `protected` and `public` are.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Students write `package com.ktu.mathutils;` at the *top of the file* but place the file in the *root* directory. The compiler will then generate the class in a *sub-folder* — but if you compile with `javac` (without `-d`), the package name will be ignored. Always use `javac -d . filename.java`.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Believing `import package.*;` imports sub-packages. It **does not**. You must explicitly import every sub-package.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting that `java.lang` is auto-imported. Writing `import java.lang.String;` is **redundant** but not an error.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing the class-level access (only `public` or default) with member-level access (all four). A *non-public* top-level class cannot be imported by name in another package.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - A **package** is a *namespace* and a *directory structure* simultaneously.
> - The `package` statement must be the **first non-comment statement** in a source file.
> - **CLASSPATH** is the lookup path for `.class` files; default is the current directory `.`.
> - The **JVM ClassLoader** hierarchy: Bootstrap → Extension → Application.
> - **Four access levels:** `public` (everywhere), `protected` (everywhere + inheritance), *default* (package only), `private` (class only).
> - `java.lang` is the **only package auto-imported** by the compiler.
> - `import package.*;` imports classes, **not sub-packages**.
> - **Static import** (`import static java.lang.Math.PI;`) allows referencing static members without class-name qualification.
> - Package naming convention: **all lowercase**, reverse domain notation (e.g., `com.ktu.oop`).
> - Top-level classes can only be `public` or *default*; the `protected` and `private` modifiers are invalid at the top level.
> - The `-d` flag in `javac` is **mandatory** for user-defined packages to correctly place the `.class` files.
> - **Name conflict resolution:** Use the Fully Qualified Name (FQN) inline, e.g., `java.util.Date d = new java.util.Date();`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfaces - Interfaces v/s Abstract Classes, Defining an Interface, Implementing Interfaces, Accessing Implementations through Interface References, Extending Interface(s)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST615 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfaces - Interfaces v/s Abstract Classes, Defining an Interface, Implementing Interfaces, Accessing Implementations through Interface References, Extending Interface(s)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Interface

In the context of **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**, an **Interface** is a purely abstract type that is used to specify a behaviour contract that classes must implement. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, an interface in Java is a reference type, similar to a class, that can contain **only constants, method signatures, default methods, static methods, and nested types**. Method bodies exist only for default and static methods.

Formally, the **Java Language Specification (JLS)** defines an interface as a construct that declares methods without providing implementations, leaving the concrete behaviour to the implementing classes. An interface is declared using the `interface` keyword.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In KTU 2024 scheme, interfaces are studied as a pure abstraction mechanism. They form the foundation for design patterns like **Strategy**, **Observer**, and **Dependency Injection**. Interfaces enable **multiple inheritance of type** in Java (a class can implement multiple interfaces, unlike multiple class inheritance).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of an Abstract Class

An **Abstract Class** is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that cannot be instantiated directly but can be subclassed. It may contain both **abstract methods** (methods without a body) and **concrete methods** (methods with a body). Abstract classes are used when classes share common code but also require derived classes to provide specific implementations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Interface** = 100% abstract contract (until Java 7); from Java 8 it can have default and static methods.
> - **Abstract Class** = Partial implementation + Abstract contract.
> - Both **cannot be instantiated** directly.
> - Both can declare abstract methods that must be overridden by concrete subclasses.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Real-World Analogy: The Electrical Socket

Imagine the **electrical socket on your wall**. The socket defines a contract:
- It has **3 holes** (Phase, Neutral, Earth).
- It promises that **220V AC** will flow through it.
- It does **not care** which appliance (TV, fridge, laptop charger, mixer) you plug in.

The **socket is the interface** — it defines **what** is expected (a standard shape and voltage) but not **how** each appliance uses that power internally. A Philips TV, a Samsung fridge, and a Havells mixer are all **implementations** of this interface — they all conform to the contract (fit the socket) but have completely different internal behaviour.

Similarly, an **Abstract Class** is like a **partially built car chassis** on a factory line. The chassis provides the basic structure (wheels, body frame, engine mount) — the **concrete part**. But it leaves the **infotainment system, paint, and interior** to be decided by different variants (sedan, SUV, sports car) — the **abstract part**.

| Concept | Analogy | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Interface** | Electrical Socket | Pure contract — defines what, not how |
| **Abstract Class** | Partially built car | Common code + contract — partial implementation |
| **Concrete Class** | Finished Car | Full implementation of all behaviours |

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

- **Contract** — A set of method signatures that a class commits to implement.
- **Implementation** — The act of providing method bodies for an interface's abstract methods.
- **Upcasting** — Assigning a subclass object to a superclass/interface reference.
- **Polymorphism** — Single interface, multiple implementations.
- **Multiple Inheritance of Type** — A class can implement many interfaces.
- **Marker Interface** — An empty interface (e.g., `Serializable`) used to mark capability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 The Core Philosophy: Why Interfaces?

Object-oriented design follows the **"Program to an interface, not an implementation"** principle (from the *Gang of Four* design patterns). This decouples the **client** (the code using the object) from the **server** (the actual implementation), enabling:

1. **Loose Coupling** — Modules depend on abstractions, not concrete classes.
2. **Flexibility** — New implementations can be plugged in without changing client code.
3. **Testability** — Mock implementations can be created for unit testing.
4. **Multiple Inheritance of Type** — A class can conform to many contracts.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Interfaces Work

### Step 1 — Declaration
An interface is declared with the `interface` keyword. By default, all methods are `public abstract` and all fields are `public static final`.

### Step 2 — Implementation
A class uses the `implements` keyword to commit to the interface's contract. It must provide concrete implementations for **all abstract methods**, or declare itself as `abstract`.

### Step 3 — Polymorphic Access
A reference variable of the interface type can hold any object of any class that implements it. Method calls are resolved at **runtime** through dynamic method dispatch.

### Step 4 — Extension
Interfaces can inherit from other interfaces using the `extends` keyword. A class implementing the child interface must implement methods from all parent interfaces.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: Interface vs Abstract Class

| Feature | Interface | Abstract Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Keyword** | `interface` | `abstract class` |
| **Instantiation** | Not allowed | Not allowed |
| **Method Types Allowed** | Abstract, default, static, private (Java 9+) | Abstract and concrete |
| **Fields** | Only `public static final` constants | Any access modifier; instance variables allowed |
| **Constructor** | Not allowed | Allowed |
| **Multiple Inheritance** | A class can implement **multiple** interfaces | A class can extend **only one** abstract class |
| **Access Modifiers for Methods** | `public` only (or default) | Any (`public`, `protected`, `private`) |
| **When to Use** | Define a *role* or *capability* across unrelated classes | Share common code among related classes in a hierarchy |
| **Inheritance Keyword** | `implements` | `extends` |
| **Default Method (Java 8+)** | Allowed with `default` keyword | Not applicable — methods are either abstract or concrete |
| **State Maintenance** | Cannot hold state (no instance fields) | Can hold state (instance variables) |
| **Speed** | Slightly slower (extra indirection) | Faster (direct method calls) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | **Very High** — asked almost every year | High — commonly compared with interfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Answers**
> - Use **Interface** when: "has-a capability" relationship (e.g., a class *is* `Comparable`, *is* `Serializable`, *is* `Drawable`).
> - Use **Abstract Class** when: "is-a" relationship with shared code (e.g., `Animal` → `Dog`, `Cat`).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Java Collections Framework** | `List`, `Set`, `Map` are interfaces; `ArrayList`, `HashSet`, `HashMap` are implementations. |
| **JDBC API** | `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` are interfaces; drivers provide implementations. |
| **GUI Programming** | `ActionListener`, `MouseListener` are interfaces implemented by event handlers. |
| **Plugin Architectures** | IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ) define extension points as interfaces; plugins implement them. |
| **Dependency Injection (Spring)** | Beans are wired through interface contracts, not concrete classes. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Defining an Interface

The general syntax for defining an interface in Java follows the template:

$$
\texttt{[\textbf{access\_modifier}] \ interface \ \textit{InterfaceName} \ [\texttt{extends \textit{OtherInterface1, OtherInterface2}}] \ \{}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad // Constant declarations (implicitly public static final)}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad // Abstract method signatures (implicitly public abstract)}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad // Default methods (Java 8+) — keyword: default}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad // Static methods (Java 8+)}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad // Private methods (Java 9+) — helper for default methods}
$$

$$
\texttt{\}}
$$

### Worked Example 1: Defining a Simple Interface

```java
// File: Drawable.java
public interface Drawable {
    // Implicitly public static final
    String TOOL_NAME = "Pencil";

    // Implicitly public abstract
    void draw();
    void resize(double factor);

    // Default method (Java 8+) — has a body
    default void display() {
        System.out.println("Displaying on canvas using " + TOOL_NAME);
    }

    // Static method (Java 8+) — utility, called via interface name
    static Drawable createDefault() {
        return () -> System.out.println("Default draw");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of transitions:**

- Line 1: `public` is the access modifier. The interface is accessible everywhere.
- Line 3: `TOOL_NAME` is implicitly `public static final` — i.e., a **constant**.
- Lines 6–7: `draw()` and `resize()` are implicitly `public abstract` — no body, no curly braces.
- Lines 10–12: `default` keyword allows the method to have a body. Implementing classes may **override** it, but are not required to.
- Lines 15–18: `static` method belongs to the interface itself, not to instances. Called as `Drawable.createDefault()`.

## 3.2 Implementing an Interface

The class that agrees to the interface's contract uses the `implements` keyword.

### Worked Example 2: Implementing the `Drawable` Interface

```java
// File: Circle.java
public class Circle implements Drawable {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double radius) {
        this.radius = radius;
    }

    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle of radius " + radius);
    }

    @Override
    public void resize(double factor) {
        radius *= factor;
        System.out.println("New radius: " + radius);
    }

    // display() inherited as default — no need to override
}
```

**Logical transitions:**

- The `Circle` class uses `implements Drawable` to commit to the contract.
- It must provide `draw()` and `resize()` — otherwise the class itself must be declared `abstract`.
- `display()` is **inherited** as a default method — no override needed.
- The class is now of type `Circle` **and** of type `Drawable` (multiple types).

### Multiple Interface Implementation

A class can implement several interfaces simultaneously, separated by commas.

```java
public class SmartCircle extends Shape 
                       implements Drawable, Comparable<SmartCircle>, Serializable {
    // Must implement all abstract methods from:
    //   Drawable, Comparable<SmartCircle>, Serializable
    // Inherits concrete code from Shape (single class only)
}
```

**KTU Rule:** Java permits **one `extends`** (class) but **many `implements`** (interfaces). This is the central reason interfaces exist — to provide a safe form of multiple inheritance.

## 3.3 Accessing Implementations Through Interface References

This is the **polymorphic access** mechanism — the heart of OOP. An interface reference can point to any object of any class that implements it.

### Worked Example 3: Polymorphic Interface Access

```java
public class DrawingApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Interface reference → Circle object (Upcasting)
        Drawable shape1 = new Circle(5.0);
        
        // Interface reference → Rectangle object (Upcasting)
        Drawable shape2 = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);

        // Polymorphic call — exact method resolved at runtime
        shape1.draw();     // Output: Drawing a Circle of radius 5.0
        shape2.draw();     // Output: Drawing a Rectangle of 4.0 x 6.0

        // Calling default method (inherited)
        shape1.display();  // Output: Displaying on canvas using Pencil
    }
}
```

**Logical analysis:**

- `Drawable shape1 = new Circle(5.0);` — the compile-time type is `Drawable`, runtime type is `Circle`. This is **upcasting** and is always safe.
- `shape1.draw();` — the compiler checks that `draw()` exists in `Drawable` (it does). At runtime, the JVM sees the actual object is a `Circle` and invokes `Circle.draw()`.
- If the actual object were a `Square` or `Triangle`, the corresponding version would run — **dynamic dispatch**.

### Accessing Interface-Only Methods vs. Subclass-Specific Methods

```java
Drawable d = new Circle(5.0);
d.draw();                  // ✅ Allowed — draw() is in Drawable
d.resize(2.0);             // ✅ Allowed — resize() is in Drawable
d.display();               // ✅ Allowed — display() is in Drawable (default)

// d.getRadius();          // ❌ Compile-time error — getRadius() is in Circle, not in Drawable
```

To call `getRadius()`, you must **downcast**:

```java
((Circle) d).getRadius();  // Explicit downcast — risk of ClassCastException
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Truth**
> The compiler decides what you can call based on the **reference type** (left side of `=`). The JVM decides which version runs based on the **object type** (right side of `=`). This is the essence of polymorphism.

## 3.4 Extending Interfaces

Interfaces can inherit from other interfaces using the `extends` keyword. The child interface inherits all abstract method signatures.

### Single Interface Extension

```java
public interface Shape extends Drawable {
    double getArea();
    double getPerimeter();
}
```

**Explanation:**

- `Shape` now has all methods from `Drawable` (`draw`, `resize`, `display`) **plus** `getArea()` and `getPerimeter()`.
- A class implementing `Shape` must provide **all five abstract methods**.

### Multiple Interface Extension

```java
public interface InteractiveShape extends Drawable, Resizable {
    void onClick();
    void onHover();
}
```

**Explanation:**

- `InteractiveShape` merges contracts from `Drawable` and `Resizable`, adding two new methods.
- Any class implementing `InteractiveShape` must implement methods from **all three** interfaces.

### Class Implementation of Extended Interface

```java
public class InteractiveCircle implements InteractiveShape {
    @Override public void draw()        { /* ... */ }
    @Override public void resize(double f) { /* ... */ }
    @Override public void onClick()     { /* ... */ }
    @Override public void onHover()     { /* ... */ }
}
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Code Patterns

| Pattern | Syntax | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Interface declaration** | `public interface Name { ... }` | High |
| **Interface extending interface** | `interface B extends A { ... }` | High |
| **Interface extending multiple** | `interface C extends A, B { ... }` | High |
| **Class implementing interface** | `class X implements I { ... }` | High |
| **Class extending & implementing** | `class X extends Y implements I { ... }` | Very High |
| **Interface reference variable** | `I ref = new C();` | Very High |
| **Default method** | `default void m() { ... }` | Medium |
| **Static method in interface** | `static void m() { ... }` | Medium |
| **Marker interface** | `interface I { }` (empty) | Low–Medium |

## 3.6 Derivative Scenario: Interface as a Callback (Lambda Compatible)

In Java 8+, interfaces with **exactly one abstract method** are **functional interfaces** and can be implemented using **lambda expressions**.

```java
@FunctionalInterface
public interface Notifier {
    void send(String message);
}

// Lambda implementation
Notifier emailNotifier = message -> System.out.println("Email sent: " + message);
emailNotifier.send("Hello KTU!");   // Output: Email sent: Hello KTU!
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `@FunctionalInterface` annotation is optional but recommended. It causes a compile-time error if the interface has more than one abstract method, ensuring it remains lambda-compatible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Interface Inheritance & Class Implementation — Class Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a child interface extends multiple parent interfaces, and how a concrete class implements the resulting contract.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IA["INTERFACE A"]:::iface
    IB["INTERFACE B"]:::iface
    IC["INTERFACE C"]:::iface
    ID["INTERFACE D EXTENDS A B C"]:::iface
    CA["CLASS C1 IMPLEMENTS D"]:::concrete
    CB["CLASS C2 IMPLEMENTS D"]:::concrete
    CC["ABSTRACT CLASS C3 IMPLEMENTS D"]:::abstract

    IA --- ID
    IB --- ID
    IC --- ID
    ID --- CA
    ID --- CB
    ID --- CC

    classDef iface fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef concrete fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef abstract fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:**

- `A`, `B`, `C` are base interfaces.
- `D` extends `A`, `B`, `C` — inheriting their abstract methods.
- `C1`, `C2` are concrete classes that must implement **every** abstract method from `A`, `B`, `C`, `D`.
- `C3` is abstract — it is allowed to leave some methods unimplemented, delegating them further down the hierarchy.

## 4.2 Polymorphic Access — Block Diagram of Dynamic Dispatch

This diagram shows how a single interface reference (`Drawable`) can be used to invoke different implementations stored in a collection.

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLIENT["CLIENT CODE"]:::client
    REF["DRAWABLE REF VAR"]:::ref
    OBJ1["CIRCLE OBJECT"]:::obj
    OBJ2["RECTANGLE OBJECT"]:::obj
    OBJ3["TRIANGLE OBJECT"]:::obj
    M1["CIRCLE DRAW METHOD"]:::method
    M2["RECTANGLE DRAW METHOD"]:::method
    M3["TRIANGLE DRAW METHOD"]:::method

    CLIENT -->|"Drawable d = new Circle()"| REF
    CLIENT -->|"Drawable d = new Rectangle()"| REF
    CLIENT -->|"Drawable d = new Triangle()"| REF
    REF -.->|"runtime dispatch"| OBJ1
    REF -.->|"runtime dispatch"| OBJ2
    REF -.->|"runtime dispatch"| OBJ3
    OBJ1 --> M1
    OBJ2 --> M2
    OBJ3 --> M3

    classDef client fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2E86C1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#7D6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:**

- The **CLIENT CODE** holds a `Drawable` reference (solid red border) — the *compile-time* type.
- The dashed arrows to **CIRCLE, RECTANGLE, TRIANGLE** represent *runtime* dispatch — the JVM decides which object to bind to.
- Each object invokes its **own version** of `draw()` — this is polymorphism in action.

## 4.3 Interface vs Abstract Class — Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["NEED ABSTRACTION?"]:::start
    Q1["UNRELATED CLASSES NEED COMMON BEHAVIOUR?"]:::qstn
    Q2["SHARED CODE AMONG RELATED CLASSES?"]:::qstn
    Q3["NEED MULTIPLE INHERITANCE?"]:::qstn
    IFACE["USE INTERFACE"]:::iface
    ABS["USE ABSTRACT CLASS"]:::abs
    BOTH["USE BOTH ABSTRACT CLASS AND INTERFACE"]:::both

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|YES| Q3
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2
    Q3 -->|YES| IFACE
    Q3 -->|NO| Q2
    Q2 -->|YES| BOTH
    Q2 -->|NO| IFACE

    classDef start fill:#F4ECF7,stroke:#6C3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef qstn fill:#FEF9E7,stroke:#7D6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iface fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2E86C1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef abs fill:#FAD7A0,stroke:#D68910,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef both fill:#ABEBC6,stroke:#196F3D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:**

- If unrelated classes need common behaviour → **Interface**.
- If related classes share code → consider **Abstract Class** (often paired with an Interface for type definition).
- If multiple inheritance of type is required → **Interface is mandatory**.

## 4.4 Real-World Architectural Flow: Payment Processing System

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP["E COMMERCE APP"]:::app
    GATEWAY["PAYMENT GATEWAY INTERFACE"]:::iface
    PAY1["CREDIT CARD IMPL"]:::impl
    PAY2["UPI IMPL"]:::impl
    PAY3["NET BANKING IMPL"]:::impl
    BANK["BANK SERVER"]:::server

    APP -->|uses contract| GATEWAY
    GATEWAY --> PAY1
    GATEWAY --> PAY2
    GATEWAY --> PAY3
    PAY1 --> BANK
    PAY2 --> BANK
    PAY3 --> BANK

    classDef app fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iface fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2E86C1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef impl fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef server fill:#E8DAEF,stroke:#6C3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:**

- The **E-Commerce App** is coded against the `PaymentGateway` **interface** (not against `CreditCard` or `UPI` directly).
- This means adding a new payment method (e.g., Crypto) requires **no change** to the App's code — only a new implementation class.
- This is the **Open/Closed Principle** in action: open for extension, closed for modification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between an Interface and an Abstract Class in Java. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Interface | Abstract Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declared using the `interface` keyword. | Declared using the `abstract class` keyword. |
| 2 | Supports multiple inheritance — a class can implement many interfaces. | Does not support multiple inheritance — a class can extend only one abstract class. |
| 3 | Can contain only `public static final` constants; cannot have instance variables. | Can contain instance variables with any access modifier. |
| 4 | Cannot have constructors. | Can have constructors (used by subclasses via `super()`). |
| 5 | Methods are implicitly `public abstract` (until Java 8). | Methods can be abstract, concrete, or a mix. |

**Conclusion:** Interfaces are pure contracts used to define capabilities across unrelated classes, whereas abstract classes are used to share common code among related classes in a hierarchy.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by "accessing implementations through interface references"? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Accessing implementations through interface references means declaring a reference variable of an **interface type** and assigning it an object of any class that implements that interface. This enables **polymorphism** — the same reference can be used to invoke different implementations at runtime.

**Example:**

```java
interface Shape {
    void draw();
}

class Circle implements Shape {
    public void draw() { System.out.println("Circle drawn"); }
}

class Square implements Shape {
    public void draw() { System.out.println("Square drawn"); }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;                 // interface reference
        s = new Circle();        // upcasting
        s.draw();                // prints "Circle drawn"
        s = new Square();        // same reference, different object
        s.draw();                // prints "Square drawn"
    }
}
```

The actual method executed is decided at **runtime** based on the object's actual class — this is **dynamic method dispatch**.

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of an interface in Java. Write a Java program to define an interface `Vehicle` with methods `start()`, `stop()`, and a default method `service()`. Implement this interface in two classes: `Car` and `Bike`. Demonstrate polymorphic access by storing both objects in a `Vehicle` array and invoking `start()` and `stop()` on each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is interface inheritance? Explain with suitable Java code how one interface can extend multiple interfaces. Also, write a class that implements the derived interface.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Concept Explanation [2 marks]:**

An **interface** in Java is a reference type that defines a contract of abstract methods which implementing classes must provide. Interfaces support multiple inheritance of type and form the foundation of polymorphic design in Java.

**Java Program [5 marks]:**

```java
// File: Vehicle.java
public interface Vehicle {
    // Abstract methods
    void start();
    void stop();

    // Default method (Java 8+)
    default void service() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle sent for general servicing.");
    }
}
```

```java
// File: Car.java
public class Car implements Vehicle {
    private String model;

    public Car(String model) {
        this.model = model;
    }

    @Override
    public void start() {
        System.out.println(model + " car started with key ignition.");
    }

    @Override
    public void stop() {
        System.out.println(model + " car stopped.");
    }

    @Override
    public void service() {
        System.out.println(model + " car sent for premium servicing.");
    }
}
```

```java
// File: Bike.java
public class Bike implements Vehicle {
    private String brand;

    public Bike(String brand) {
        this.brand = brand;
    }

    @Override
    public void start() {
        System.out.println(brand + " bike started with self-start.");
    }

    @Override
    public void stop() {
        System.out.println(brand + " bike stopped.");
    }
    // service() inherited as default
}
```

```java
// File: Main.java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Array of interface references — polymorphic storage
        Vehicle[] garage = new Vehicle[3];
        garage[0] = new Car("Honda City");
        garage[1] = new Bike("Royal Enfield");
        garage[2] = new Car("Toyota Innova");

        for (Vehicle v : garage) {
            v.start();
            v.stop();
            v.service();
            System.out.println("---");
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Honda City car started with key ignition.
Honda City car stopped.
Honda City car sent for premium servicing.
---
Royal Enfield bike started with self-start.
Royal Enfield bike stopped.
Vehicle sent for general servicing.
---
Toyota Innova car started with key ignition.
Toyota Innova car stopped.
Toyota Innova car sent for premium servicing.
---
```

**Explanation of dynamic dispatch [1 mark embedded]:**

The `v.start()` call inside the loop is compiled against the `Vehicle` interface (compile-time check), but the JVM resolves the actual method at runtime based on whether `v` points to a `Car` or `Bike` object.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Concept Explanation [2 marks]:**

Interface inheritance allows an interface to inherit the abstract method declarations of one or more parent interfaces using the `extends` keyword. This is similar to class inheritance but more flexible because a child interface can extend **multiple** parent interfaces, enabling a clean form of multiple inheritance of type.

**Java Code [5 marks]:**

```java
// Parent interface 1
interface EnginePowered {
    void startEngine();
    void stopEngine();
}

// Parent interface 2
interface FuelConsumer {
    void refuel(double litres);
    double getFuelLevel();
}

// Child interface extending both
interface MotorVehicle extends EnginePowered, FuelConsumer {
    void drive();
    void applyBrakes();
}
```

```java
// Class implementing the derived interface
public class ElectricMotor implements MotorVehicle {
    private double batteryLevel;  // percentage 0-100

    public ElectricMotor(double initialLevel) {
        this.batteryLevel = initialLevel;
    }

    @Override
    public void startEngine() {
        System.out.println("Electric motor started silently.");
    }

    @Override
    public void stopEngine() {
        System.out.println("Electric motor stopped.");
    }

    @Override
    public void refuel(double kWh) {
        batteryLevel = Math.min(100, batteryLevel + kWh * 5);
        System.out.println("Recharged. Level: " + batteryLevel + "%");
    }

    @Override
    public double getFuelLevel() {
        return batteryLevel;
    }

    @Override
    public void drive() {
        System.out.println("Motor vehicle is moving forward.");
    }

    @Override
    public void applyBrakes() {
        System.out.println("Regenerative brakes applied.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation [embedded in code]:**

`ElectricMotor` must provide implementations for **all five abstract methods**: `startEngine`, `stopEngine` (from `EnginePowered`), `refuel`, `getFuelLevel` (from `FuelConsumer`), and `drive`, `applyBrakes` (from `MotorVehicle`). It cannot extend another class here — only implement the merged interface contract.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Abstract Classes and Interfaces in Java. Create an abstract class `Account` with fields `accountNumber`, `balance`, and abstract methods `deposit()` and `withdraw()`. Implement two subclasses `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of *functional interfaces* in Java 8. Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of a functional interface with a lambda expression for processing a list of integers (e.g., filtering, mapping, or sorting).

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table [3 marks]:**

| Parameter | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| **Keyword** | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| **Multiple Inheritance** | Not supported | Supported |
| **Constructors** | Allowed | Not allowed |
| **Instance Variables** | Allowed | Not allowed (only constants) |
| **Method Types** | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + default + static |
| **Access Modifiers** | Any | `public` only (for methods) |
| **When to Use** | "is-a" relationship with shared code | "has-a capability" relationship |
| **Speed** | Slightly faster (direct call) | Slightly slower (virtual dispatch) |

**Java Code [4 marks]:**

```java
// File: Account.java
public abstract class Account {
    protected String accountNumber;
    protected double balance;

    public Account(String accountNumber, double initialBalance) {
        this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
        this.balance = initialBalance;
    }

    // Concrete method
    public void displayBalance() {
        System.out.println("Account " + accountNumber + " | Balance: ₹" + balance);
    }

    // Abstract methods
    public abstract void deposit(double amount);
    public abstract void withdraw(double amount);
}
```

```java
// File: SavingsAccount.java
public class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    private static final double MIN_BALANCE = 1000.0;

    public SavingsAccount(String accNo, double initial) {
        super(accNo, initial);
    }

    @Override
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        balance += amount;
        System.out.println("₹" + amount + " deposited. New balance: ₹" + balance);
    }

    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (balance - amount >= MIN_BALANCE) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("₹" + amount + " withdrawn. New balance: ₹" + balance);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Withdrawal denied. Minimum balance ₹" + MIN_BALANCE + " must be maintained.");
        }
    }
}
```

```java
// File: CurrentAccount.java
public class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    private static final double OVERDRAFT_LIMIT = -50000.0;

    public CurrentAccount(String accNo, double initial) {
        super(accNo, initial);
    }

    @Override
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        balance += amount;
        System.out.println("₹" + amount + " deposited. New balance: ₹" + balance);
    }

    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (balance - amount >= OVERDRAFT_LIMIT) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("₹" + amount + " withdrawn. New balance: ₹" + balance);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Withdrawal denied. Overdraft limit of ₹" + Math.abs(OVERDRAFT_LIMIT) + " exceeded.");
        }
    }
}
```

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Concept Explanation [2 marks]:**

A **functional interface** in Java is an interface that contains **exactly one abstract method** (SAM — Single Abstract Method). They are eligible to be the target of **lambda expressions** and **method references**. The `@FunctionalInterface` annotation is optional but enforces this rule at compile time. Examples include `Runnable`, `Callable<T>`, `Comparator<T>`, and `ActionListener`.

**Java Program [5 marks]:**

```java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.function.Predicate;
import java.util.function.Function;

public class FunctionalInterfaceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(12, 5, 23, 8, 19, 4, 16, 27);

        System.out.println("Original List: " + numbers);

        // (i) Using Predicate<Integer> — filter even numbers
        Predicate<Integer> isEven = n -> n % 2 == 0;
        List<Integer> evens = new ArrayList<>();
        for (Integer n : numbers) {
            if (isEven.test(n)) evens.add(n);
        }
        System.out.println("Even numbers: " + evens);

        // (ii) Using Function<Integer, Integer> — square each number
        Function<Integer, Integer> square = n -> n * n;
        List<Integer> squares = new ArrayList<>();
        for (Integer n : numbers) {
            squares.add(square.apply(n));
        }
        System.out.println("Squares: " + squares);

        // (iii) Using Comparator<Integer> — sort descending
        Comparator<Integer> descending = (a, b) -> b - a;
        List<Integer> sortedDesc = new ArrayList<>(numbers);
        sortedDesc.sort(descending);
        System.out.println("Sorted descending: " + sortedDesc);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Original List: [12, 5, 23, 8, 19, 4, 16, 27]
Even numbers: [12, 8, 4, 16]
Squares: [144, 25, 529, 64, 361, 16, 256, 729]
Sorted descending: [27, 23, 19, 16, 12, 8, 5, 4]
```

**Key takeaway [embedded]:**

`Predicate`, `Function`, and `Comparator` are all built-in functional interfaces from the `java.util.function` package, enabling concise lambda-based operations on collections.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override`** — Examiners specifically look for this annotation. Marks are **deducted (½ to 1 mark)** if missing on implemented methods.
> 2. **Confusing `implements` and `extends`** — Students often write `class X extends Interface` or `class X implements Class`. The correct usages are `extends` for classes/interfaces and `implements` for interfaces **only**.
> 3. **Missing the rule that a class implementing an interface must implement ALL abstract methods** — otherwise the class itself must be `abstract`. Examiners will deduct marks for an incomplete implementation in a concrete class.
> 4. **Not writing `public` explicitly in the implementing class** — Interface methods are implicitly `public`, so overrides must also be `public`. Writing them as default/package-private causes a compile error.
> 5. **Confusing compile-time and runtime types in polymorphism questions** — Examiners expect clear statements like *"The reference type determines the methods callable at compile time; the object's actual class determines the method executed at runtime."*
> 6. **In Part B answers, students often forget to demonstrate polymorphic access** — Always include code with an interface reference variable, not just a direct object call.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Interface** = pure contract; declared with `interface` keyword; cannot be instantiated; all methods are implicitly `public abstract` (until Java 7).
- **Abstract Class** = partial implementation; declared with `abstract` keyword; cannot be instantiated; can have both abstract and concrete methods.
- A class uses **`implements`** to adopt an interface and **`extends`** to inherit a class.
- A class can implement **multiple** interfaces but can extend only **one** class.
- Interfaces can extend **multiple** other interfaces using `extends`.
- An **interface reference** can hold an object of any implementing class — this is the basis of polymorphism.
- The compiler checks method calls against the **reference type**; the JVM dispatches the actual call based on the **object type**.
- **Default methods** (Java 8+) provide backward-compatible evolution of interfaces; they have a body and can be optionally overridden.
- **Static methods** in interfaces belong to the interface itself, not to instances.
- **Functional interfaces** have exactly one abstract method and can be implemented with **lambda expressions**.
- Interfaces **cannot have constructors** and **cannot have instance fields** (only `public static final` constants).
- Use **Interface** for "has-a capability" (e.g., `Comparable`, `Serializable`, `Runnable`).
- Use **Abstract Class** for "is-a" relationship with shared code among related classes.
- The **"program to an interface"** principle enables loose coupling, flexibility, and easier unit testing.
- Java 8 introduced default and static methods in interfaces to ease API evolution.
- Java 9 added `private` methods in interfaces for code reuse among default methods.
- A concrete class that fails to implement **all** abstract methods of an interface must itself be declared `abstract`.
- Multiple inheritance through interfaces avoids the **"Diamond Problem"** of C++ because Java resolves method conflicts via explicit override rules.
- Marker interfaces (e.g., `Serializable`, `Cloneable`) contain no methods and are used to signal capability to the JVM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
